question: A 24-year-old man with type 1 diabetes mellitus is brought to the emergency department because of weakness, abdominal pain, nausea, and one episode of vomiting for 1 day. He has not taken his insulin for 3 days. His pulse is 125/min and respirations are 29/min. Examination shows dry mucous membranes. His breath has a fruity odor. Which of the following sets of laboratory values is most likely on evaluation of urine obtained before treatment? $$$ pH %%% HCO3- %%% NH4+ %%% K+ $$$. Answer Choices: A. go down go down go up go up B. go down normal go down go down C. go down go up normal go up D. go down go down go up go down
label: go down go down go up go up
rationale: The laboratory values for the urine obtained before treatment are most likely to show decreased pH, decreased HCO3-, increased NH4+, and increased K+. This aligns with the expected findings in a patient with DKA.

question: A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with chronic abdominal discomfort and cramping. She seeks medical attention now as she is concerned about the diarrhea that she has developed that is occasionally mixed with tiny streaks of blood. Her medical history is significant for lactose intolerance and asthma. She has a family history of wheat allergy and reports that she has tried to make herself vomit on several occasions to lose weight. After counseling the patient about the dangers of bulimia, physical examination reveals the rectum is red, inflamed, tender, and a perirectal abscess is seen draining purulent material. Colonoscopy demonstrates scattered mucosal lesions involving the colon and terminal ileum. A complete blood count is given below: Hb%: 10 gm/dL Total count (WBC): 12,500/mm3 Differential count: Neutrophils: 50% Lymphocytes: 40% Monocytes: 5% ESR: 22 mm/hr What is the most likely diagnosis?. Answer Choices: A. Irritable bowel syndrome B. Celiac disease C. Ulcerative colitis D. Crohn’s disease
label: Crohn’s disease
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is Crohn's disease, which explains the chronic colitis, perianal abscess, and scattered mucosal lesions involving the colon and terminal ileum.

question: Mitochondria isolated and placed in a buffered solution with a low pH begin to manufacture ATP. Which of the following is the best explanation for the effect of low external pH?. Answer Choices: A. It increases the concentration of OH-, causing the mitochondria to pump H+ to the intermembrane space. B. It increases the OH- concentration in the mitochondria matrix. C. It increases the acid concentration in the mitochondria matrix. D. It increases diffusion of H+ from the intermembrane space to the matrix.
label: It increases diffusion of H+ from the intermembrane space to the matrix
rationale: The low external pH increases the diffusion of H+ ions from the intermembrane space (or external environment in this case) into the mitochondrial matrix through ATP synthase. This facilitates ATP synthesis by utilizing the proton-motive force generated by the artificially enhanced gradient.

question: A 75-year-old man with hypercholesterolemia, coronary artery disease, and history of a transient ischemic attack (TIA) comes to your office for evaluation of hypertension. Previously, his blood pressure was controlled with diet and an ACE inhibitor. Today, his blood pressure is 180/115 mm Hg, and his creatinine is increased from 0.54 to 1.2 mg/dL. The patient reports that he has been compliant with his diet and blood pressure medications. What is the most likely cause of his hypertension?. Answer Choices: A. Renal artery stenosis B. Coarctation of the aorta C. Pheochromocytoma D. Hypothyroidism
label: Renal artery stenosis
rationale: Based on the patient's history of vascular disease, sudden increase in blood pressure and creatinine, and compliance with medication, the most likely cause of his hypertension is [LABEL].

question: A 32-year-old G1P0 woman presents to her obstetrician for a prenatal visit. She is 30 weeks pregnant. She reports some fatigue and complains of urinary urgency. Prior to this pregnancy, she had no significant medical history. She takes a prenatal vitamin and folate supplements daily. Her mother has diabetes, and her brother has coronary artery disease. On physical examination, the fundal height is 25 centimeters. A fetal ultrasound shows a proportional reduction in head circumference, trunk size, and limb length. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the patient’s presentation?. Answer Choices: A. Antiphospholipid syndrome B. Gestational diabetes C. Pre-eclampsia D. Rubella infection
label: Rubella infection
rationale: The most likely cause of the patient's presentation, including decreased head circumference and severe limb shortening, is [LABEL].

question: The most common site of osteoma is( REPEAT QUESTION). Answer Choices: A. Maxillary sinus B. Ethmoidal sinus C. Frontal sinus D. Sphenoid sinus
label: Frontal sinus
rationale: The most common site of osteoma is the [LABEL].

question: A 31-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with the complaint that over the past 6 months she has “felt out of breath and dizzy while walking, even after short distances.” She reports no other medical problems and denies taking any medications, vitamins, supplements, recreational drugs, alcohol or tobacco. Her BMI is 24kg/m2. On physical examination, the patient has a loud second heart sound over the left upper sternal border, increased jugular venous pressure, and a palpable right ventricular impulse. Which of the following is the patient most at risk of developing if her condition is allowed to persist for a prolonged period:. Answer Choices: A. Abdominal aortic aneurysm B. Right ventricular failure C. Pulmonary abscess D. Tension pneumothorax
label: Right ventricular failure
rationale: The patient's symptoms and physical examination findings are consistent with pulmonary hypertension, which can lead to right ventricular hypertrophy and eventually [LABEL] if left untreated. Therefore, the patient is most at risk of developing [LABEL] if her condition persists.

question: A 67-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 9-month history of seeing bright red blood in the toilet after defecating. Additional complaints include fatigue, shortness of breath, and mild lethargy. She denies the loss of weight, abdominal pain, or changes in dietary behavior. She consumes a balanced diet and takes multiple vitamins every day. The current vital signs include the following: temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse rate is 68/min, blood pressure is 130/81 mm Hg, and the respiratory rate is 13/min. On physical examination, you notice increased capillary refill time and pale mucosa. What are the most likely findings for hemoglobin, hematocrit, red blood cell count, and mean corpuscular volume?. Answer Choices: A. Hemoglobin: go up , hematocrit: go down , red blood cell count: go down , mean corpuscular volume: go up B. Hemoglobin: go down , hematocrit: go up , red blood cell count: go down , mean corpuscular volume: go down C. Hemoglobin: go down , hematocrit: go down , red blood cell count: go up , mean corpuscular volume: go up D. Hemoglobin: go down , hematocrit: go down , red blood cell count: go down , mean corpuscular volume: go down
label: Hemoglobin: go down , hematocrit: go down , red blood cell count: go down , mean corpuscular volume: go down
rationale: The most likely findings in this patient include decreased hemoglobin concentration, reduced hematocrit, decreased red blood cell count, and decreased mean corpuscular volume, consistent with iron deficiency anemia secondary to chronic gastrointestinal blood loss.

question: Trials stage for experimentation of adverse drug reaction in large scale population is ?. Answer Choices: A. I B. II C. III D. IV
label: IV
rationale: The stage of clinical trials where long-term adverse effects on a large number of patients are studied is Phase IV clinical trial.

question: A 42-year-old man with hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus is admitted to the hospital because of swelling and redness of the left leg for 3 days. He has chills and malaise. He is treated with intravenous clindamycin for 7 days. On the 8th day at the hospital, he has profuse, foul-smelling, and watery diarrhea. He has nausea and intermittent abdominal cramping. His temperature is 38°C (100.4°F), pulse is 97/min, and blood pressure is 110/78 mm Hg. Bowel sounds are hyperactive. Abdominal examination shows mild tenderness in the left lower quadrant. Rectal examination shows no abnormalities. His hemoglobin concentration is 14.3 g/dL, leukocyte count is 12,300/mm3, and C-reactive protein concentration is 62 mg/L (N=0.08–3.1). After discontinuing clindamycin, which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient's condition?. Answer Choices: A. Oral metronidazole B. Oral fidaxomicin C. Oral rifaximin D. Intravenous metronidazole
label: Oral fidaxomicin
rationale: After discontinuing clindamycin, [LABEL] is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient’s condition. This treatment targets Clostridium difficile colitis, which is the most likely diagnosis based on the patient’s symptoms, history of clindamycin use, and clinical findings.

question: True about mitochondrial inheritance. Answer Choices: A. An offspring bom to an affected male will be affected B. An offspring born to an affected female will be affected C. An offspring born to an affected female will be normal D. All of the above
label: An offspring born to an affected female will be affected
rationale: An offspring born to an affected female will inherit the mitochondrial disorder, as mitochondrial inheritance is exclusively maternal. Examples of mitochondrial disorders include NARP syndrome, MELAS syndrome, MERRF syndrome, and Kearns-Sayre syndrome, which present with symptoms such as global developmental delay, seizures, stroke-like episodes, hearing impairment, abnormality of vision, lactic acidosis, encephalopathy, cardiomyopathy, and myopathy.

question: A 58-year-old man comes to the emergency department with complaints of abdominal pain, swelling, and fever for the last few days. Pain is situated in the right upper quadrant (RUQ) and is dull and aching. He scores it as 6/10 with no exacerbating or relieving factors. He also complains of anorexia for the same duration. The patient experiences a little discomfort while lying flat and has been sleeping in a recliner for the past 2 days. There has been no chest pain, nausea, vomiting, or change in bowel or bladder habit. He does not use tobacco, alcohol, or any recreational drug. He is suffering from polycythemia vera and undergoes therapeutic phlebotomy every 2 weeks, but he has missed several appointments. The patient’s mother died of a heart attack, and his father died from a stroke. Temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F), blood pressure is 142/88 mm Hg, pulse is 106/min, respirations are 16/min, and BMI is 20 kg/m2. On physical examination, his heart and lungs appear normal. Abdominal exam reveals tenderness to palpation in the RUQ and shifting dullness. Laboratory test Hemoglobin 20.5 g/dL Hematocrit 62% WBC 16,000/mm3 Platelets 250,000/mm3 Albumin 3.8 g/dL Diagnostic paracentesis Albumin 2.2 g/dL WBC 300/µL (reference range: < 500 leukocytes/µL) What is the best next step in management of the patient?. Answer Choices: A. Echocardiography B. Ultrasound C. MRI D. Venography
label: Ultrasound
rationale: The best next step in the management of this patient is an abdominal [LABEL] with Doppler to evaluate for Budd-Chiari syndrome or other hepatobiliary causes of RUQ pain, fever, and ascites.

question: A 32-year-old woman comes to the physician because of increasing muscle weakness in her shoulders and legs for 6 weeks. She is unable to climb stairs or comb her hair. She has also had difficulty swallowing food for the past week. Her symptoms do not improve with rest. Physical examination shows normal muscle tone. There is bilateral weakness of the iliopsoas, hamstring, deltoid, and biceps muscles. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ bilaterally. Sensation to pinprick, temperature, and vibration is intact. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 10.7 g/dL Leukocyte count 10.800/mm3 Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 100 mm/h Serum Glucose 60 mg/dL Creatine kinase 7047 U/L Lactate dehydrogenase 2785 U/L Thyroid-stimulating hormone 4.0 μU/mL Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?". Answer Choices: A. Lumbar puncture B. Electromyography C. Tensilon test D. Temporal artery biopsy
label: Electromyography
rationale: The most appropriate next step in management is [LABEL] (EMG) to evaluate muscle activity and confirm the presence of a myopathic process.

question: A 68-year-old man with hypertension comes to the physician because of fatigue and difficulty initiating urination. He wakes up several times a night to urinate. He does not take any medications. His blood pressure is 166/82 mm Hg. Digital rectal examination shows a firm, non-tender, and uniformly enlarged prostate. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?. Answer Choices: A. Phenoxybenzamine B. Tamsulosin C. Terazosin D. α-Methyldopa
label: Terazosin
rationale: [LABEL] is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient. It addresses both the urinary symptoms caused by BPH and the elevated blood pressure, providing a dual benefit.

question: The scoring given in periodontal index for gingivitis is. Answer Choices: A. 1 B. 2 C. 4 D. 6
rationale: The scoring given in the periodontal index for gingivitis is 2, which corresponds to moderate gingivitis with inflammation and bleeding on probing. This aligns with the clinical criteria for the periodontal index.

question: A researcher is studying the ability of breast cancer cells to metastasize. Neoplastic cells obtained from 30 patients with stage IV ductal carcinoma of the breast are tagged with a fluorescent antibody. The cells are then inserted into a medium resembling normal human tissue. After 2 weeks, all samples show in vitro hematogenous invasion and migration away from the original site of insertion. Which of the following properties is most likely responsible for the ability of these neoplastic cells to metastasize?. Answer Choices: A. Loss of cellular polarity B. Overexpression of HER2/neu C. Presence of fibrous tissue capsule D. Release of matrix metalloproteinase
label: Release of matrix metalloproteinase
rationale: The [LABEL]s (MMPs) is most likely responsible for the ability of these neoplastic cells to metastasize.

question: A certain molecule acts by binding to cytochrome oxidase A3, the final enzyme in the electron transport chain. Administration of a large dose of this substance to a human would likely:. Answer Choices: A. Lead to death due to an inability of the cell to pass electrons to oxygen, thus stopping aerobic respiration and asphyxiating the cells. B. Lead to death due to an inadequate supply of ADP to accept a phosphate group at the ATP synthase enzyme. C. Have no effect as cells would switch which macronutrient they metabolize to circumvent the blocked biochemical pathway. D. Increase the cell’s ATP production as negative feedback would cause the cell to up-regulate anaerobic pathways.
label: Lead to death due to an inability of the cell to pass electrons to oxygen, thus stopping aerobic respiration and asphyxiating the cells
rationale: Administration of a large dose of this substance would likely lead to sudden death and respiratory arrest due to the inability of cells to pass electrons to oxygen, halting aerobic respiration. This conclusion aligns with the reasoning that disruption of the electron transport chain leads to ATP depletion, organ failure, and ultimately death.

question: In sickle cell anaemia, the defect can be explained as having arisen from -. Answer Choices: A. A base inseion in DNA B. A base deletion in DNA C. A base substitution in DNA D. None of the above
label: A base substitution in DNA
rationale: The defect in sickle cell anemia is caused by [LABEL], specifically a single nucleotide change in the HBB gene that results in the replacement of glutamic acid with valine in the beta-globin chain of hemoglobin.

question: What is the percentage of patients with disseminated discoid lupus erythematosus who develop SLE?. Answer Choices: A. 2% B. 22% C. 52% D. 82%
rationale: The percentage of patients with disseminated discoid lupus erythematosus who develop systemic lupus erythematosus is approximately [LABEL], which aligns with the reasoning and available epidemiological data.

question: A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with the complaint of excessive fatigue for the past few weeks. After returning home from the office, she feels too tired to climb up the stairs, comb her hair, or chew her food. She has occasionally experienced double vision. She denies any history of fever, cough, weight loss, night sweats, or snoring. Past history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals: blood pressure 124/86 mm Hg, heart rate 85/min, respiratory rate 14/min, temperature 37.0°C (98.6°F), and body mass index (BMI) 22.6 kg/m2. On examination, the right upper eyelid is slightly drooping when compared to the left side. Her eye movements are normal. Flexion of the neck is mildly weak. Muscle strength is 5/5 in all 4 limbs. When she is asked to alternately flex and extend her shoulder continuously for 5 minutes, the power in the proximal upper limb muscles becomes 4/5. The muscle tone and deep tendon reflexes are normal. What is the most appropriate test to diagnose this condition?. Answer Choices: A. CT scan chest B. Plasmapheresis C. Single-fiber electromyography D. Tensilon test
label: Single-fiber electromyography
rationale: The most appropriate test to evaluate motor activity and diagnose this condition is [LABEL] (SFEMG). This test is highly sensitive for detecting neuromuscular junction disorders, particularly myasthenia gravis, which is the most likely diagnosis based on the patient's symptoms.

question: A 24-year-old man comes to the physician because of 2 episodes of bleeding from the rectum over the past month. The patient’s father died of colon cancer at the age of 42. The patient has no history of any serious illness and takes no medications. He does not smoke. His vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows a small hard mass over the right mandible that is nontender and fixed to the underlying bone. A similarly hard and painless 5 × 5 mass is palpated over the rectus abdominis muscle. On examination of the rectum, a polypoid mass is palpated at fingertip. Proctosigmoidoscopy shows numerous polyps. Which of the following best explains these findings?. Answer Choices: A. Gardner’s syndrome B. Lynch’s syndrome C. Peutz-Jeghers syndrome D. Turcot’s syndrome
label: Gardner’s syndrome
rationale: The findings are best explained by Gardner syndrome, a phenotypic variant of familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) caused by mutations in the APC gene.

question: A 58-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his wife 30 minutes after the sudden onset of severe retrosternal chest pain radiating to his back. He has a history of hyperlipidemia, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes mellitus. He has smoked one-half pack of cigarettes daily for 20 years. Medications include aspirin, captopril, atorvastatin, and metformin. His pulse is 80/min and blood pressure is 160/60 mm Hg. A CT scan of the chest is shown. Which of the following is the strongest predisposing factor for this patient's current condition?. Answer Choices: A. Age B. Genetic collagen disorder C. Hypertension D. History of smoking
label: Hypertension
rationale: [LABEL] is the strongest predisposing factor for this patient's current condition, which is likely an aortic dissection.

question: If the mean rate of oxygen consumption of a male athlete during a training session is 2 l/min, then his rate of energy expenditure is approximately:. Answer Choices: A. 400 kJ/min. B. 200 kJ/min. C. 80 kJ/min. D. 40 kJ/min.
label: 40 kJ/min
rationale: The rate of energy expenditure is approximately [LABEL]. This matches the given answer, and the reasoning supports its correctness.

question: Which one of the following factors is labelled as cytokine in the pathogenesis of systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) ?. Answer Choices: A. Nitric oxide B. Complements C. Leukotrienes D. Tumor Necrosis factor
label: Tumor Necrosis factor
rationale: Based on the reasoning, [LABEL] (TNF) is indeed a key cytokine involved in the pathogenesis of SIRS. It plays a central role in initiating and propagating the inflammatory response, leading to complications such as multiple organ dysfunction syndrome.

question: A 38-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 4-day history of swelling and pain in her left knee. She has had similar episodes of swollen joints over the past 3 weeks. Two months ago, she had a rash on her upper back that subsided after a few days. She lives in Pennsylvania and works as a forest ranger. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F). Physical examination shows a tender and warm left knee. Arthrocentesis of the knee joint yields cloudy fluid with a leukocyte count of 65,000/mm3 and 80% neutrophils. A Gram stain of synovial fluid does not show any organisms. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's condition?. Answer Choices: A. Wearing down of articular cartilage B. Infection with round bacteria in clusters C. Postinfectious activation of innate lymphoid cells of the gut D. Infection with spiral-shaped bacteria
label: Infection with spiral-shaped bacteria
rationale: The most likely cause of this patient’s condition is Lyme disease, caused by infection with *Borrelia burgdorferi*, a spirochete. This aligns with the patient’s history of rash, recurrent joint swelling, arthrocentesis findings, and geographic/occupational exposure.

question: A 28-year-old primigravid woman at 31 weeks' gestation comes to the physician because of fever, myalgia, abdominal pain, nausea, and diarrhea for 3 days. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Her only medication is a prenatal vitamin. Her temperature is 39.4°C (102.9°F). Physical examination shows diffuse abdominal pain. Blood cultures incubated at 4°C (39.2°F) grow a gram-positive, catalase-positive organism. The pathogen responsible for this patient's presentation was most likely transmitted via which of the following modes?. Answer Choices: A. Blood transfusion B. Sexual contact C. Consumption of soft cheese D. Ingestion of cat feces
label: Consumption of soft cheese
rationale: The pathogen responsible for this patient's presentation was most likely *Listeria monocytogenes*, transmitted via the [LABEL].

question: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the pediatrician by his mother who is concerned about progressive leg weakness. His mother reports that the patient used to play outside with their neighbors for hours, but for the past few months she has seen him sitting on the sidewalk after 15 minutes because he’s too tired. The patient says his legs are “sleepy.” The patient’s mother has also had to remove the carpets from the house because the patient kept tripping over the edges. The mother reports that the patient is shy but cooperates well with his siblings and other children. He can say his first and last name and just started counting. His mother states he learned to fully walk by 15 months of age. He was hospitalized for bronchiolitis at 12 months of age, which resolved with supportive care. He had an uncomplicated orchiopexy surgery for undescended testes at 7 months of age. He has no other chronic medical conditions and takes no medications. He is up to date on his vaccinations including a flu vaccine 2 weeks ago. The patient’s mother has systemic lupus erythematous and his paternal uncle has dermatomyositis. On physical examination, bilateral calves are large in circumference compared to the thighs. Strength is 3/5 in bilateral quadriceps and 4/5 in bilateral calves. Sensation is intact. Achilles tendon reflexes are 1+ bilaterally. The patient can hop on one leg, but gets tired after 10 jumps. He has a slight waddling gait. Which of the following is the most appropriate test to confirm the diagnosis?. Answer Choices: A. Acetylcholine receptor antibody level B. Creatine kinase level C. Genetic testing D. Nerve conduction study
label: Genetic testing
rationale: The most appropriate test to confirm the diagnosis of progressive muscle weakness with calf muscle pseudohypertrophy, quadriceps muscle weakness, and a waddling gait is [LABEL] for mutations in the DMD gene, consistent with Duchenne muscular dystrophy.

question: Which one of the following hepatic lesions can be diagnosed with high accuracy by using nuclear imaging ?. Answer Choices: A. Hepatocellular Carcinoma B. Hepatic Adenoma C. Focal Nodular Hyperplasia D. Cholangiocarcinoma
label: Focal Nodular Hyperplasia
rationale: [LABEL] (FNH) is the hepatic lesion that can be diagnosed with high accuracy using nuclear imaging, particularly through hepatocyte-based imaging techniques that highlight functioning hepatocytes. This aligns with the properties of FNH, which involves hyperplastic hepatocytes that can be effectively imaged using IDA compounds.

question: A 17-year-old boy presents to the emergency department of a hospital located in the town of Recuay (which is situated at 3,400 meters above mean sea level [MAMSL]) in the Ancash Region (Peru), 48 hours after returning from a 21-day stay in Lima (the capital city of Peru at 0 MAMSL). The patient has no previous medical history. His current complaints include cough, dyspnea at rest, hemoptysis, chest pain, and vomiting. His vital signs include: blood pressure 90/60 mm Hg; heart rate 149/min; respiratory rate 37/min; temperature 36.5°C (97.7°F); and O2 saturation 71%. Physical examination reveals polypnea, perioral cyanosis, intercostal retractions, and diffuse pulmonary crackles. His laboratory results are as follows: Hemoglobin 19.2 g/dL Hematocrit 60% Leukocytes 13,000 (Bands: 12%, Seg: 78%, Eos: 0%, Bas: 0%, Mon: 6%) Urea 25 mg/dL Creatinine 0.96 mg/dL A chest X-ray is shown. Which of the following statements is true and most likely regarding this patient’s condition? . Answer Choices: A. Following a rapid and sustained increase in altitude, decreased sympathetic activity transiently increases cardiac output, blood pressure, heart rate, and venous tone B. Marked increase in pressure gradient can lead to tissue hypoxia C. The net change in response to hypoxia results in decreased cerebral blood flow D. The pulmonary vasculature relaxes in response to hypoxia
label: Marked increase in pressure gradient can lead to tissue hypoxia
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is high-altitude pulmonary edema (HAPE), caused by hypobaric hypoxia at high altitude. The marked increase in pulmonary artery pressure has led to pulmonary edema, hypoxemia, and tissue hypoxia.

question: Synchysis refers to. Answer Choices: A. Collapse of the vitreous B. Black spots in front of the eyes C. Liquefaction of the vitreous D. Detachment of the vitreous
label: Liquefaction of the vitreous
rationale: Vitreous liquefaction (synchysis) can result from various causes, including aging, degenerative myopia, retinitis pigmentosa, uveitis, trauma, thermal effects (e.g., photocoagulation, cryocoagulation), and radiation exposure. These factors contribute to the breakdown of the structural integrity of the vitreous body, leading to its liquefaction.

question: Which among the following condition is positive for double stranded DNA antibody?. Answer Choices: A. SLE B. Sjogren's syndrome C. Scleroderma D. Mixed Connective Tissue Disorder
label: SLE
rationale: Systemic lupus erythematosus ([LABEL]) is the condition positive for double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) antibodies.

question: A 57-year-old man is brought to the emergency department for crushing substernal chest pain at rest for the past 2 hours. The pain began gradually while he was having an argument with his wife and is now severe. He does not take any medications. He has smoked 1 pack of cigarettes daily for 35 years. He is diaphoretic. His temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 21/min, and blood pressure is 115/65 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 97%. Cardiac examination shows an S4 gallop. The lungs are clear to auscultation. An ECG is shown. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's condition?. Answer Choices: A. Occlusion of the left anterior descending artery B. Thromboembolism to the right interlobar pulmonary artery C. Occlusion of the left circumflex artery D. Tear in the intimal lining of the aorta
label: Occlusion of the left anterior descending artery
rationale: The most likely underlying cause of this patient's condition is [LABEL] due to acute coronary syndrome, likely triggered by atherosclerotic plaque rupture and thrombus formation.

question: A 55-year-old man presents to the emergency department for chest pain. He states that the pain started last night and has persisted until this morning. He describes the pain as in his chest and radiating into his back between his scapulae. The patient has a past medical history of alcohol abuse and cocaine abuse. He recently returned from vacation on a transatlantic flight. The patient has smoked 1 pack of cigarettes per day for the past 20 years. His temperature is 99.5°F (37.5°C), blood pressure is 167/118 mmHg, pulse is 120/min, and respirations are 22/min. Physical exam reveals tachycardia and clear air movement bilaterally on cardiopulmonary exam. Which of the following is also likely to be found in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Asymmetric blood pressures in the upper extremities B. Coronary artery thrombus C. Coronary artery vasospasm D. Pulmonary artery thrombus
label: Asymmetric blood pressures in the upper extremities
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is aortic dissection, given the patient’s chest pain radiating to the back, history of hypertension and cocaine use, and physical findings. [LABEL] are a likely finding in this patient, as they are a classic feature of aortic dissection involving the aortic arch or its branches.

question: A 41-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department with the acute-onset of severe abdominal pain for the past 2 hours. She has a history of frequent episodes of vague abdominal pain, but they have never been this severe. Every time she has had pain, it would resolve after eating a meal. Her past medical history is otherwise insignificant. Her vital signs include: blood pressure 121/77 mm Hg, pulse 91/min, respiratory rate 21/min, and temperature 37°C (98.6°F). On examination, her abdomen is flat and rigid. Which of the following is the next best step in evaluating this patient’s discomfort and stomach pain by physical exam?. Answer Choices: A. Elicit shifting dullness of the abdomen B. Percuss the point of maximal pain C. Perform light palpation at the point of maximal pain D. Auscultate the abdomen
label: Auscultate the abdomen
rationale: The next best step in evaluating this patient’s discomfort and pain is an upright chest X-ray or abdominal X-ray to assess for free air under the diaphragm, which would indicate perforation. Auscultation of the abdomen is not the most appropriate next step in this acute, potentially life-threatening scenario.

question: How many mm from the limbus is the safest site of intravitreal injection?. Answer Choices: A. 1-2 mm B. 2-3 mm C. 3-4 mm D. 4-5 mm
label: 3-4 mm
rationale: The safest site for intravitreal injection is [LABEL] from the limbus, with specific distances of 3 mm for aphakic eyes, 3.5 mm for pseudophakic eyes, and 4 mm for phakic eyes. This recommendation aligns with anatomical and clinical considerations to minimize complications.

question: Anemia in chronic renal failure is due to. Answer Choices: A. Decreased erythropoietin production B. Iron deficiency C. Hypoplastic bone marrow D. Decreased folate levels
label: Decreased erythropoietin production
rationale: The anemia in chronic renal failure is primarily due to decreased erythropoietin (EPO) production and a reduced lifespan of red blood cells. Inflammatory processes may also contribute. In acute kidney failure, the correlation between anemia and renal function is weaker, and conditions like hemolytic-uremic syndrome can stimulate erythropoiesis despite renal failure.

question: A medical student is reviewing dose-response curves of various experimental drugs. She is specifically interested in the different factors that cause the curve to shift in different directions. From her study, she plots the following graph (see image). She marks the blue curve for drug A, which acts optimally on a receptor. After drawing the second (green) curve, she discovers that this drug B has a lower ability to produce a reaction than the first one. She also discovers that more of the second drug B is required to produce the same response as the first one. Which of the following terms best describes the activity of drug B in comparison to drug A?. Answer Choices: A. Lower potency B. Higher potency C. Increased affinity D. Decreased efficacy
label: Lower potency
rationale: Drug B has [LABEL] compared to Drug A because it requires a higher dose to achieve the same response. Additionally, Drug B has lower efficacy because it produces a reduced maximal response. These differences are likely due to altered receptor-ligand activity, such as reduced binding affinity or intrinsic activity.

question: A 10-year-old woman presents to the clinic, with her mother, complaining of a circular, itchy rash on her scalp for the past 3 weeks. Her mother is also worried about her hair loss. The girl has a past medical history significant for asthma. She needs to use her albuterol inhaler once per week on average. Her blood pressure is 112/70 mm Hg; the heart rate is 104/min; the respiratory rate is 20/min, and the temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F). On exam, the patient is alert and interactive. Her lungs are clear on bilateral auscultation. On palpation, a tender posterior cervical node is present on the right side. Examination of the head is shown in the image. Which of the following is the best treatment option for the patient?. Answer Choices: A. Subcutaneous triamcinolone B. Ketoconazole shampoo C. Oral doxycycline D. Oral terbinafine
label: Oral terbinafine
rationale: The best treatment option for this patient is [LABEL], as it is effective, well-tolerated, and specifically targets the likely causative organism of tinea capitis.

question: A 7 year old boy presented with generalized edema.Urine examination revealed marked albuminuria.Serum biochemical examinations showed hypoalbuminaemia with hyperlipidemia.Kidney biopsy was undeaken.On light microscopic examination, the kidney appeared normal.Electron microscopic examination is most likely to reveal. Answer Choices: A. Fusion of foot processes of the glomerular epithelial cells B. Rarefaction of glomerular basement membrane C. Deposition of electron dense material in the basement membrane D. Thin basement membrane
label: Fusion of foot processes of the glomerular epithelial cells
rationale: Electron microscopy is most likely to reveal fusion of podocyte foot processes, which is characteristic of idiopathic steroid-sensitive nephrotic syndrome with minimal change disease. This condition is associated with proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia, and positive regulation of lipid metabolic processes. Corticosteroids are the mainstay of treatment, and the prognosis is good.

question: Which of the following substances is found in greater quantity in exhaled air?. Answer Choices: A. Carbon dioxide B. Carbon monoxide C. Nitrogen D. Oxygen
label: Nitrogen
rationale: [LABEL] is indeed the substance found in greater quantity in exhaled air. This conclusion aligns with the fact that [LABEL] is the most abundant gas in the atmosphere and remains unchanged during respiration.

question: A 35-year-old woman from San Francisco has been refusing to vaccinate her children due to the claims that vaccinations may cause autism in children. Her 10-year-old male child began developing a low-grade fever with a rash that started on his face; as the rash began to spread to his limbs, it slowly disappeared from his face. When the child was taken to a clinic, the physician noticed swollen lymph nodes behind the ears of the child. Which of the following are characteristics of the virus causing these symptoms?. Answer Choices: A. Nonenveloped, SS linear DNA B. Enveloped, SS + nonsegmented RNA C. Enveloped, SS - nonsegmented RNA D. Nonenveloped, DS segmented RNA
label: Enveloped, SS + nonsegmented RNA
rationale: The virus causing these symptoms is rubella, which is an enveloped, single-stranded positive-sense nonsegmented RNA virus.

question: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the pediatrician by his foster father because he is concerned about the boy's health. He states that at seemingly random times he will have episodes of severe difficulty breathing and wheezing. Upon questioning, the pediatrician learns that these episodes do not appear to be associated with exercise, irritants, or infection. The pediatrician suspects the child has a type of asthma that is associated with eosinophils. In this type of asthma, what is released by the eosinophils to cause bronchial epithelial damage?. Answer Choices: A. IL-5 B. Major basic protein C. IgM D. Interferon-gamma
label: Major basic protein
rationale: In this type of asthma, eosinophils release [LABEL], which causes bronchial epithelial damage. This aligns with the suspected diagnosis of eosinophilic asthma.

question: In peripheral tissues which of the following contains substance P:. Answer Choices: A. Plasma cell B. Mast cell C. Nerve terminal D. Vascular endothelium
label: Nerve terminal
rationale: The neuron projection terminus, or [LABEL], in the peripheral nervous system is indeed responsible for containing and releasing substance P. This aligns with the understanding that substance P is stored in the [LABEL]s of sensory neurons, supporting its role in pain transmission and other sensory functions.

question: A 40yr old patient developed high fever of sudden onset .Peripheral blood film showed malaria parasite pigment was yellowish brown and schizonts were 9-10microns and contained 20 merozoites each. Answer Choices: A. Pl vivax B. Pl falciparum C. Pl malariae D. Pl ovale
label: Pl vivax
rationale: The features are consistent with the diagnosis of Plasmodium vivax malaria, based on the morphological characteristics of the schizonts (size, merozoite count) and the presence of yellowish-brown pigment.

question: A 10-year-old girl is brought to the physician because of itching of the vulva and anal region for the past 2 weeks. She has difficulty sleeping because of the itching. Physical examination shows excoriation marks around the vulva and perianal region. There is minor perianal erythema, but no edema or fissures. Microscopy of an adhesive tape applied to the perianal region shows multiple ova. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Mebendazole B. Melarsoprol C. Diethylcarbamazine D. Praziquantel
label: Mebendazole
rationale: The most appropriate treatment for this patient is [LABEL], as it is a first-line therapy for Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm) infection.

question: A 35 year old male presents to the urologist for an infeility evaluation. A biopsy of his testis is performed to check sperm production and maturation. A microscopic section reveals only a few germ cells near the basal lamina in the seminiferous tubule. Which of the following cells is the germ cell closest to the basal lamina in the seminiferous tubule?. Answer Choices: A. Primary spermatocyte B. Secondary spermatocyte C. Spermatid D. Spermatogonia
label: Spermatogonia
rationale: [LABEL] are the germ cells closest to the basal lamina in the seminiferous tubule. During spermatogenesis, [LABEL] mature into primary spermatocytes, secondary spermatocytes, and spermatids, which eventually become mature spermatozoa. Acrosomes form from the Golgi apparatus, a flagellum forms from microtubules, and unneeded organelles are shed.

question: A destitute woman is admitted to the hospital with altered sensorium and dehydration; urine analysis shows mild proteinuria and no sugar; what other test would be desirable:. Answer Choices: A. Fouchet B. Rothera C. Hays D. Benedicts
label: Rothera
rationale: The most appropriate test to detect ketosis caused by starvation is [LABEL]'s test, as it directly identifies ketone bodies involved in the ketone body metabolic process.

question: A 32-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 3-week history of cough, weight loss, and night sweats. He migrated from Sri Lanka 6 months ago. He appears emaciated. His temperature is 38.1°C (100.5°F). Physical examination shows enlargement of the right supraclavicular lymph node. Chest and abdominal examination show no abnormalities. An interferon-gamma assay is positive. A biopsy specimen of the cervical lymph node is most likely to show the causal organism in which of the following locations?. Answer Choices: A. Germinal center B. Medullary sinus C. Paracortex D. Periarteriolar lymphatic sheath
label: Medullary sinus
rationale: The causal organism is most likely *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, located in the [LABEL] of the cervical lymph node, consistent with tuberculosis.

question: For which of the following patients would you recommend prophylaxis against mycobacterium avium-intracellulare?. Answer Choices: A. 22-year old HIV positive female with CD4 count of 750 cells/ microliter and a viral load of 500,000 copies/mL B. 30-year old HIV positive male with CD4 count of 20 cells/ microliter and a viral load of < 50 copies/mL C. 50-year old HIV positive female with CD4 count of 150 cells/ microliter and a viral load of < 50 copies/mL D. 36-year old HIV positive male with CD4 count of 75 cells/microliter and an undetectable viral load.
label: 30-year old HIV positive male with CD4 count of 20 cells/ microliter and a viral load of < 50 copies/mL
rationale: A 30-year-old HIV-positive male with a CD4 count of 20 cells/microliter is at significant risk for mycobacterium avium complex disease due to advanced immunosuppression. Prophylaxis is recommended in this scenario to prevent the disease.

question: A 65-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-month history of progressive shortness of breath and a dry cough. She has also noticed gradual development of facial discoloration. She has coronary artery disease, hypertension, and atrial fibrillation. She does not remember which medications she takes. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 150/85 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 95%. Examination shows blue-gray discoloration of the face and both hands. Diffuse inspiratory crackles are heard. An x-ray of the chest shows reticular opacities around the lung periphery and particularly around the lung bases. The most likely cause of this patient's findings is an adverse effect to which of the following medications?. Answer Choices: A. Lisinopril B. Metoprolol C. Amiodarone D. Warfarin
label: Amiodarone
rationale: The most likely cause of the patient’s findings is [LABEL]-induced pulmonary toxicity and cutaneous side effects. This explains the progressive dyspnea, dry cough, inspiratory crackles, reticular pattern on HRCT, and blue-gray skin discoloration.

question: Which of these is the most impoant indication for Strassmans Metroplasty done for a bicornuate uterus?. Answer Choices: A. Infeility B. Menorrhagia C. Repeated early pregnancy losses D. Associated vaginal atresia
label: Repeated early pregnancy losses
rationale: The most important indication for Strassman's Metroplasty is recurrent spontaneous abortion in women with a bicornuate uterus. This conclusion is supported by the strong association between the structural abnormality of a bicornuate uterus and recurrent pregnancy loss, as well as the primary goal of the surgery to improve pregnancy outcomes.

question: You are seeing an otherwise healthy 66-year-old male in clinic who is complaining of localized back pain and a new rash. On physical exam, his vital signs are within normal limits. You note a vesicular rash restricted to the upper left side of his back. In order to confirm your suspected diagnosis, you perform a diagnostic test. What would you expect to find on the diagnostic test that was performed?. Answer Choices: A. Gram positive cocci B. Gram negative bacilli C. Branching pseudohyphae D. Multinucleated giant cells
label: Multinucleated giant cells
rationale: The diagnostic test performed is likely a Tzanck smear, which is expected to reveal [LABEL], confirming the suspicion of herpes zoster (shingles) in this patient.

question: Primary bile acid is?. Answer Choices: A. Deoxycholic acid B. Lithocholic acid C. Chenodeoxycholic acid D. None
label: Chenodeoxycholic acid
rationale: [LABEL] is a primary bile acid synthesized from cholesterol in the liver. It plays a significant role in the cholesterol metabolic process, as evidenced by its involvement in pathways mediated by enzymes like CYP27A1 and CYP3A4. Additionally, [LABEL] and its derivatives are involved in bile-salt sulfotransferase activity, which is crucial for bile acid detoxification and excretion.

question: A 39-year-old male presents with muscle weakness in his upper and lower extremities. The patient has also noticed some trouble with swallowing. On physical exam, signs of hyperreflexia, spasticity, fasciculations, and muscle atrophy are present in an asymmetric fashion. Tongue fasciculations are also present. No sensory loss is noted. The patient does not report any abnormality with his bowel or bladder function. What will most likely be found on muscle biopsy?. Answer Choices: A. Mitochondrial proliferation leading to ragged appearance in Gomori trichrome stain B. Perimysial CD4+ infiltration and perifascicular atrophy C. Denervation and reinnervation of the muscle D. Larval cysts
label: Denervation and reinnervation of the muscle
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). A muscle biopsy would most likely show [LABEL], consistent with chronic denervation signs observed on EMG.

question: Chronic Laxative abuse can result in the formation of which type of stone:. Answer Choices: A. Xanthine B. Cysteine C. Ammonium urate D. Struvite
label: Ammonium urate
rationale: Chronic laxative abuse can lead to the formation of [LABEL] stones due to the chronic diarrheal disease and electrolyte imbalances it causes. These imbalances result in decreased urine output and increased ammonium ion concentration, promoting the binding of ammonium ions with urate to form stones.

question: In which of the following structure of central nervous system the major autonomic reflex centers are found?. Answer Choices: A. Cerebellum B. Medulla oblongata C. Hypothalamus D. Thalamus
label: Medulla oblongata
rationale: The [LABEL] serves as the major autonomic reflex center in the central nervous system, responsible for controlling vital involuntary functions such as heart rate, blood pressure, and respiration.

question: Which of the following is used for acute variceal bleeding?. Answer Choices: A. Octreotide B. Oxytocin C. Somatotropin D. Dexamethasone
label: Octreotide
rationale: [LABEL] is used for the management of acute variceal bleeding due to its ability to cause splanchnic vasoconstriction, thereby reducing portal pressure and controlling bleeding. It is administered at dosages of 50-100 micrograms per hour by continuous infusion.

question: A 54-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with a 2-month-history of diarrhea. He says that he feels the urge to defecate 3-4 times per day and that his stools have changed in character since the diarrhea began. Specifically, they now float, stick to the side of the toilet bowl, and smell extremely foul. His past medical history is significant for several episodes of acute pancreatitis secondary to excessive alcohol consumption. His symptoms are found to be due to a deficiency in an enzyme that is resistant to bile salts. Which of the following enzymes is most likely deficient in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Amylase B. Chymotrypsin C. Colipase D. Lipase
label: Colipase
rationale: The enzyme most likely deficient in this patient is [LABEL], which is resistant to bile salts and essential for fat digestion.

question: A 72-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of increasing abdominal pain for 6 hours. The pain is dull and diffuse over her abdomen and radiates to her lower back bilaterally. Three weeks ago, she was diagnosed with atrial fibrillation and started on warfarin. Her only other medication is 1 g of acetaminophen daily for osteoarthritis of her knees. Her pulse is 87/min and blood pressure is 112/75 mm Hg. Physical examination shows abdominal tenderness to palpation at both lower quadrants. A CT scan of the abdomen shows a retroperitoneal mass and hazy margins of the surrounding structures. In addition to discontinuation of warfarin, the most appropriate next step in management is administration of which of the following?. Answer Choices: A. Fresh frozen plasma and tranexamic acid B. Factor VIII and von Willebrand factor C. Phytonadione and prothrombin complex concentrate D. Protamine sulfate and hydroxyethyl starch
label: Phytonadione and prothrombin complex concentrate
rationale: The most appropriate next step in management is discontinuation of warfarin, administration of phytonadione (vitamin K), and prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) to rapidly reverse anticoagulation and address the retroperitoneal hemorrhage.

question: A 41-year-old man presents to the office with pain in his right big toe. The pain started yesterday and has been progressively getting worse to the point that it is difficult to walk. He describes his right big toe as being swollen and hot to the touch. He has never had symptoms like this before. He drinks 3 beers per night. Medical history is otherwise significant for chronic kidney disease. Physical examination is notable for an overweight gentleman in moderate pain, with an erythematous, swollen and tender right toe. He is afebrile. A joint fluid analysis in this patient is most likely to show what?. Answer Choices: A. Negatively birefringent crystals B. Positively birefringent crystals C. Glucose < 40 mg/dL D. Normal
label: Negatively birefringent crystals
rationale: A joint fluid analysis in this patient is most likely to show negatively birefringent monosodium urate crystals, consistent with a diagnosis of gout.

question: Most potent statin -. Answer Choices: A. Simvastatin B. Pravastatin C. Rosuvastatin D. Simvastatin
label: Rosuvastatin
rationale: Based on the pharmacological potency, dose-response relationship, and clinical evidence, [LABEL] is the most potent statin.

question: 65-year-old man presents with anaemia and posterior column dysfunction, the likely cause is -. Answer Choices: A. B1-deficit B. B12-deficit C. SSPE D. Multiple selerosis
label: B12-deficit
rationale: The likely cause of the patient's anemia and posterior column dysfunction is vitamin B12 deficiency.

question: Treatment of atropine toxicity -. Answer Choices: A. 2-pralidoxime B. Naloxone C. Flumazenil D. Physostigmine
label: Physostigmine
rationale: [LABEL], an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, is the drug of choice for ameliorating atropine toxicity due to its ability to counteract both central and peripheral symptoms.

question: What type of covalent bonds link the amino acids in a protein?. Answer Choices: A. Peptide bonds B. Hydrogen bonds C. Ionic bonds D. Glycosidic bonds
label: Peptide bonds
rationale: The covalent bonds that link amino acids in a protein are [LABEL].

question: A 3-day-old female infant presents with poor feeding, lethargy, vomiting after feeding, and seizures. Labs revealed ketoacidosis and elevated hydroxypropionic acid levels. Upon administration of parenteral glucose and protein devoid of valine, leucine, methionine, and threonine, and carnitine, the infant began to recover. Which of the following enzymes is most likely deficient in this infant?. Answer Choices: A. Branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase B. Phenylalanine hydroxylase C. Propionyl-CoA carboxylase D. Cystathionine synthase
label: Propionyl-CoA carboxylase
rationale: The enzyme most likely deficient in this infant is [LABEL], which is involved in the metabolism of propionyl-CoA derived from valine, isoleucine, methionine, and threonine.

question: A patient with multiple myeloma and serum ca2+15mg/dl ; the treatment -a) Oral pamidronateb) Less than 15mg/dl serum calcium dose not causes symptomsc) Mithramycind) Glucocorticoids. Answer Choices: A. abc B. acd C. bc D. bde
label: acd
rationale: The treatment for the patient should include pamidronate, mithramycin (plicamycin), and glucocorticoids, as these address both the underlying condition (plasma cell myeloma) and the complication (hypercalcemia).

question: A 67-year-old woman comes to the physician with a 6-month history of pain and swelling of both legs. The symptoms are worst at the end of the day and are associated with itching of the overlying skin. Physical examination shows bilateral pitting ankle edema. An image of one of the ankles is shown. This patient is at greatest risk for which of the following complications?. Answer Choices: A. Thrombosis of a deep vein B. Malignant transformation of lymphatic endothelium C. Biliverdin accumulation in the epidermis D. Ulceration of the cutis
label: Ulceration of the cutis
rationale: This patient is at greatest risk for venous stasis ulcers, which involve the skin of the ankle and are a common complication of chronic venous insufficiency.

question: True about treatment of nocturnal enuresis. Answer Choices: A. lmipramine B. CPZ C. Alprazolam D. Haloperidol
label: lmipramine
rationale: Imipramine is a treatment option for nocturnal enuresis, but it is not the first-line treatment. It is generally used as a second-line pharmacological option when other interventions, such as behavioral therapy and desmopressin, are ineffective or unsuitable.

question: Radiolucent periapical lesions have the following except. Answer Choices: A. Absence of lamina dura B. Hanging drop of oil effect C. Opaque diffuse appearance D. No movement on cone shift
label: Opaque diffuse appearance
rationale: The statement "[LABEL] is not a feature of radiolucent periapical lesions" is correct. Radiolucent lesions are characterized by their lytic nature, ill-defined peripheries, and gradual transitions into abnormal bone patterns. Opaque features, such as dense sclerotic regions, are associated with chronic or mixed lesions, not purely radiolucent ones.

question: A 33-year-old woman schedules an appointment at an outpatient clinic for the first time after moving to the US from Peru a few months ago. She complains of easy fatigability and shortness of breath with minimal exertion for the past 6 months. She further adds that her breathlessness is worse when she goes to bed at night. She is also concerned about swelling in her legs. As a child, she says she always had sore throats. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Medical records are unavailable, but the patient says that she has always been healthy apart from her sore throats. The blood pressure is 114/90 mm Hg, the pulse is 109/min, the respiratory rate is 26/min, and the temperature is 36.7°C (98°F). On examination, she is icteric with distended jugular veins. Bilateral basal crepitations are audible on auscultation of the lungs. Also, a high-pitched apical holosystolic murmur is audible that radiates to the left axilla. A transthoracic echocardiogram reveals mitral regurgitation with an ejection fraction of 25%. Treatment should focus on which of the following?. Answer Choices: A. Decrease total peripheral resistance B. Increase inotropy of cardiac muscle C. Increase the rate of SA node discharge D. Increase coronary blood flow
label: Decrease total peripheral resistance
rationale: The treatment should focus on decreasing total peripheral resistance to manage the mitral regurgitation and heart failure effectively. This aligns with the pathophysiology of the condition and the therapeutic goals.

question: A 76-year-old man presents after an acute onset seizure. He lives in a retirement home and denies any previous history of seizures. Past medical history is significant for a hemorrhagic stroke 4 years ago, and type 2 diabetes, managed with metformin. His vital signs include: blood pressure 80/50 mm Hg, pulse 80/min, and respiratory rate 19/min. On physical examination, the patient is lethargic. Mucous membranes are dry. A noncontrast CT of the head is performed and is unremarkable. Laboratory findings are significant for the following: Plasma glucose 680 mg/dL pH 7.37 Serum bicarbonate 17 mEq/L Effective serum osmolality 350 mOsm/kg Urinary ketone bodies negative Which of the following was the most likely trigger for this patient’s seizure?. Answer Choices: A. Reduced fluid intake B. Unusual increase in physical activity C. Metformin side effects D. Concomitant viral infection
label: Reduced fluid intake
rationale: The most likely trigger for this patient’s seizure is hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS), which was precipitated by [LABEL] leading to dehydration. The dehydration exacerbated hyperosmolarity, ultimately resulting in a seizure.

question: In alcohol withdrawal which is not seen -. Answer Choices: A. Seizure B. Blackout C. Coarse tremor D. Hallucination
label: Blackout
rationale: [LABEL] is not seen in alcohol withdrawal as it occurs during acute intoxication. This distinction is based on the temporal and physiological differences between the two states.

question: A 25-year-old African-American woman visits the doctor’s office complaining of fatigue for a couple of months. She says that she feels exhausted by the end of the day. She works as a dental assistant and is on her feet most of the time. However, she eats well and also tries to walk for 30 minutes every morning. She also says that she sometimes feels breathless and has to gasp for air, especially when she is walking or jogging. Her past medical history is insignificant, except for occasional bouts of cold during the winters. Her physical exam findings are within normal limits except for moderate conjunctival pallor. Complete blood count results and iron profile are as follows: Hemoglobin 9 g/dL Hematocrit 28.5% RBC count 5.85 x 106/mm3 WBC count 5,500/mm3 Platelet count 212,000/mm3 MCV 56.1 fl MCH 20.9 pg/cell MCHC 25.6 g/dL RDW 11.7% Hb/cell Serum iron 170 mcg/dL Total iron-binding capacity (TIBC) 458 mcg/dL Transferrin saturation 60% A peripheral blood smear is given. When questioned about her family history of anemia, she says that all she remembers is her dad was never allowed to donate blood as he was anemic. Which of the following most likely explains her cell counts and blood smear results?. Answer Choices: A. Thalassemia B. B12 deficiency C. Hemolysis D. Folate deficiency
label: Thalassemia
rationale: The most likely explanation for the patient’s symptoms, laboratory findings, and family history is [LABEL], a hereditary form of anemia.

question: All the following are indications for termination of pregnancy in APH patient except:. Answer Choices: A. 37 weeks B. IUD C. Transverse lie D. Continuous bleeding
label: Transverse lie
rationale: [LABEL] is not an independent indication for termination of pregnancy in a patient with placenta previa. However, if other complications such as severe hemorrhage or fetal distress are present, termination (via cesarean section) may be required.

question: Northern blot is used for identification of: (Repeat). Answer Choices: A. RNA B. DNA C. Protein D. Antibodies
label: RNA
rationale: Northern blotting is used for the identification of [LABEL], while Western blotting is used for the identification of protein antigens. Western blotting is particularly relevant in the context of AIDS, as it is used to detect HIV antibodies.

question: A 17-year-old girl is brought to the physician for a physical examination prior to participating in sports. She has no history of serious illness. She is on the school's cheerleading team and is preparing for an upcoming competition. Menarche was at 13 years of age, and her last menstrual period was 4 months ago. She is 167 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 45 kg (99 lb); BMI is 16.1 kg/m2. Examination shows pale skin with thin, soft body hair. The patient is at increased risk for which of the following complications?. Answer Choices: A. Hyperkalemia B. Hyperphosphatemia C. Fractures D. Hyperthyroidism
label: Fractures
rationale: The patient is at increased risk for [LABEL] due to decreased bone density, which is likely caused by a combination of low body weight, secondary amenorrhea (leading to low estrogen levels), and potential nutritional deficiencies.

question: Diabetes melitus is diagnosed if fasting blood glucose is ?. Answer Choices: A. >100 B. >126 C. >110 D. >116
rationale: The diagnosis of diabetes mellitus can indeed be confirmed by an elevated hemoglobin A1c level of ≥ 6.5% or a 2-hour plasma glucose level of ≥ 11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) during an oral glucose tolerance test. These criteria complement the initial diagnostic criteria based on random and fasting blood glucose levels, providing a comprehensive approach to diagnosing diabetes.

question: A 66-year-old man comes to the physician for a follow-up examination after a below-knee amputation of the right lower leg. Three weeks ago, he had an acute arterial embolism that could not be revascularized in time to save the leg. He now reports episodic hot, shooting, and tingling pain in the right lower leg that began shortly after the amputation. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for the past 30 years. His current medications are metformin and prophylactic subcutaneous heparin. His temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), pulse is 78/min, and blood pressure is 135/88 mm Hg. Physical examination shows a slightly erythematous stump with clean sutures. The skin overlying the stump is warm and well-perfused. Muscle strength and sensation are normal throughout the remaining extremity and the left lower extremity. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?. Answer Choices: A. Phantom limb pain B. Foreign body reaction C. Reinfarction D. Diabetic neuropathy
label: Phantom limb pain
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is [LABEL], a form of painful neuropathy. This diagnosis is supported by the nature of the pain (hot, shooting, tingling), its onset shortly after amputation, and the absence of signs suggesting infection, inflammation, or vascular complications.

question: A 64-year-old woman comes to the physician for her routine health maintenance examination. She feels well. She had cervical cancer and received radiotherapy 8 years ago. Her vital signs are within normal limits. On percussion, the spleen size is 15 cm. Otherwise, the physical examination shows no abnormalities. The laboratory test results are as follows: Hemoglobin 10 g/dL Mean corpuscular volume 88 μm3 Leukocyte count 65,000/mm3 Platelet count 500,000/mm3 Two images of the peripheral blood smear are shown on the image. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?. Answer Choices: A. Allogeneic stem cell transplantation B. Dasatinib C. Phlebotomy D. Rituximab
label: Dasatinib
rationale: The most appropriate next step in management is to confirm the diagnosis of CML by testing for the BCR-ABL1 fusion gene. If CML is confirmed, [LABEL] would be an appropriate treatment option. However, the immediate next step is diagnostic testing, not initiating treatment.

question: If the frequency of males affected with an X-linked recessive condition in a human population is .10 (one in ten), what will be the expected frequency of affected females?. Answer Choices: A. 0.01 B. 0.001 C. 0.02 D. 0.0001
rationale: The expected frequency of affected females in the population is [LABEL].

question: Which of the following markers in the blood is the most reliable indicator of recent hepatitis B- infection?. Answer Choices: A. HBsAg B. lgG anti - HBs C. lgM anti - HBc D. lgM anti - HBe
label: lgM anti - HBc
rationale: The most reliable blood marker for recent hepatitis B virus infection is IgM anti-HBc, as it is specific to acute infection and distinguishes it from chronic infection or carrier states.

question: A 2-year-old girl presents to the emergency department with a 3-minute episode of a tonic-clonic seizure. The parents deny any previous history of seizure involving the patient or the family. Physical examination reveals an afebrile, well-groomed, and playful appearance, with normal vital signs. The patient carries a pink birthmark on the right side of her face extending from the forehead to the zygomatic arch. Which of the following findings is most likely on a head CT of this patient?. Answer Choices: A. A non-enhancing hemispheric lesion B. Intraparenchymal hemorrhage C. Prominent intraparenchymal white matter calcification D. Subependymal nodule
label: Prominent intraparenchymal white matter calcification
rationale: Based on the reasoning process, the most likely finding on a head CT of this patient, considering the clinical presentation and the suspicion of Sturge-Weber syndrome, would be cerebral calcifications. Therefore, the conclusion aligns with the provided answer: [LABEL] is the most likely finding on a head CT of this patient.

question: A 9-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department for sudden-onset slurring of speech and weakness of her right arm and leg. She has a mild intellectual disability. She is at the 10th percentile for weight and at the 85th percentile for height. Physical examination shows bilateral inferonasal subluxation of the lens and a high-arched palate. Her fingers are long and slender. Neurological examination shows an extensor plantar response on the left. This patient is most likely to respond to treatment with which of the following?. Answer Choices: A. Supplementation of methionine B. Restriction of phenylalanine C. Supplementation of vitamin B6 D. Restriction of cysteine
label: Supplementation of vitamin B6
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is homocystinuria, and the patient is most likely to respond to treatment with pyridoxine (vitamin B6) supplementation.

question: A 75 year-old gentleman presents to his general practitioner. He is currently being treated for hypertension and is on a multi-drug regimen. His current blood pressure is 180/100. The physician would like to begin treatment with minoxidil or hydralazine. Which of the following side effects is associated with administration of these drugs?. Answer Choices: A. Persistent cough B. Systemic volume loss C. Fetal renal toxicity D. Reflex tachycardia
label: Reflex tachycardia
rationale: [LABEL] is indeed a side effect associated with the administration of minoxidil or hydralazine. This occurs due to their potent vasodilatory effects, which trigger compensatory activation of the sympathetic nervous system.

question: A 55-year-old man comes to the physician because of episodic retrosternal chest pain and shortness of breath for the past 6 months. His symptoms occur when he takes long walks or climbs stairs but resolve promptly with rest. He has a history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, for which he takes ipratropium bromide. His pulse is 81/min and blood pressure is 153/82 mm Hg. Physical examination shows mild expiratory wheezing over both lungs. Additional treatment with a beta blocker is considered. Which of the following agents should be avoided in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Atenolol B. Labetalol C. Betaxolol D. Bisoprolol
label: Labetalol
rationale: Given the patient's history of COPD and the presence of wheezing, the use of [LABEL], a non-selective beta blocker, should be avoided due to the risk of exacerbating respiratory symptoms. Instead, if a beta blocker is necessary for managing angina, a cardioselective beta blocker (e.g., metoprolol or bisoprolol) may be considered, as they have a lesser impact on bronchial smooth muscle and are generally safer in patients with COPD.

question: A 66-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his daughter because of 3 days of fever, chills, cough, and shortness of breath. The cough is productive of yellow sputum. His symptoms have not improved with rest and guaifenesin. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which he takes hydrochlorothiazide. He has a 30-pack-year history of smoking. His temperature is 38.9 C (102.0 F), blood pressure 88/56 mm Hg, and heart rate 105/min. Following resuscitation with normal saline, his blood pressure improves to 110/70 mm Hg. His arterial blood gas is as follows: Blood pH 7.52, PaO2 74 mm Hg, PaCO2 28 mm Hg, and HCO3- 21 mEq/L. Which of the following acid-base disturbances best characterizes this patient's condition?. Answer Choices: A. Normal acid-base status B. Metabolic acidosis C. Respiratory acidosis D. Respiratory alkalosis
label: Respiratory alkalosis
rationale: The patient's condition is best characterized as [LABEL], which is associated with a decreased serum bicarbonate concentration due to renal compensation. The most likely underlying cause is pneumonia, potentially complicated by sepsis.

question: You are seeing a patient in clinic who presents with complaints of weakness. Her physical exam is notable for right sided hyperreflexia, as well as the finding in video V. question: Cariostatic level of fluoride in drinking water is:. Answer Choices: A. 1 ppm B. 1.5 ppm C. 2 ppm D. 2.5 ppm
label: 1 ppm
rationale: The cariostatic level of fluoride in drinking water is [LABEL], which effectively prevents dental caries by supporting tooth mineralization and strengthening enamel without causing abnormalities in dental structure or carious teeth.

question: Lineage specific T Cell marker is. Answer Choices: A. CD 1 B. CD 19 C. CD 3 D. CD 4

rationale: CD3E is considered a lineage-specific marker for T cells because it is an integral component of the T cell receptor complex and is expressed exclusively on T cells.

question: A 32-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a skiing accident. The patient had been skiing down the mountain when he collided with another skier who had stopped suddenly in front of him. He is alert but complaining of pain in his chest and abdomen. He has a past medical history of intravenous drug use and peptic ulcer disease. He is a current smoker. His temperature is 97.4°F (36.3°C), blood pressure is 77/53 mmHg, pulse is 127/min, and respirations are 13/min. He has a GCS of 15 and bilateral shallow breath sounds. His abdomen is soft and distended with bruising over the epigastrium. He is moving all four extremities and has scattered lacerations on his face. His skin is cool and delayed capillary refill is present. Two large-bore IVs are placed in his antecubital fossa, and he is given 2L of normal saline. His FAST exam reveals fluid in Morison's pouch. Following the 2L normal saline, his temperature is 97.5°F (36.4°C), blood pressure is 97/62 mmHg, pulse is 115/min, and respirations are 12/min. Which of the following is the best next step in management?. Answer Choices: A. Close observation B. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy C. Diagnostic peritoneal lavage D. Emergency laparotomy
label: Emergency laparotomy
rationale: [LABEL] is the best next step in management for this patient. The clinical presentation and FAST exam findings indicate significant intra-abdominal bleeding, and the patient's hemodynamic instability necessitates immediate surgical intervention.

question: A 25-year-old woman presents to a medical clinic with complaints of right-sided chest pain, which is exacerbated during inspiration. rationale: The most specific test to help diagnose this patient's condition is the [LABEL] (dsDNA), which is indicative of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).

question: Patient because of positive biopsy findings and negative workup undergoes a radical prostatectomy. question: A 36-year-old man undergoes surgical intervention due to a right upper quadrant stab wound. His gallbladder was found to be lacerated and is removed. It is sent for histological evaluation. rationale: Based on the reasoning above, the histological section is likely taken from a site of the gallbladder that does not adjoin the liver. This conclusion is supported by the absence of liver tissue or features indicating liver contact in the histological evaluation.

question: A 35-year-old woman comes to the physician for the evaluation of fatigue over the past 6 months. During this period, she has also had fever, joint pain, and a recurrent skin rash on her face. She has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for the past 15 years. Her temperature is 38.5°C (101.3°F), pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Physical examination shows a facial rash that spares the nasolabial folds and several oral ulcers. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?. Answer Choices: A. Autoantibodies against postsynaptic acetylcholine receptors B. question: A 52-year-old woman has had dyspnea and hemoptysis for 1 month. She has a history of rheumatic fever as a child and has had a cardiac murmur since early adulthood. question: A 13-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her parents for the evaluation of progressive hair loss over the past 2 months. The parents report that they have noticed increased number of hairless patches on their daughter's head. The patient denies any itching. There is no personal or family history of serious illness. Further examination shows poor hair growth of the eyebrows and eyelashes. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?. question: Which of the following is the classical CSF finding seen in TBM?. Answer Choices: A. Increased protein, decreased sugar, increased lymphocytes B. Increased protein, sugar and lymphocytes C. Decreased protein, increased sugar and lymphocytes D. Increased sugar, protein and neutrophils

rationale: The CSF findings in TBM are characterized by increased protein levels, decreased glucose concentration, and an increased lymphocyte count. In the early stages, there may be a predominance of neutrophils. These findings align with the classical presentation of TBM and are critical for its diagnosis.

question: A 15-year-old boy is brought to the Emergency department by ambulance from school. He started the day with some body aches and joint pain but then had several episodes of vomiting and started complaining of a terrible headache. The school nurse called for emergency services. label: Oxidase-positive and ferments glucose and maltose

question: A 65-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her daughter for fever and cough. question: A patient has Rinne test +ve for left ear and weber test is lateralized to right ear, what type of deafness may be seen in this case?. Answer Choices: A. Left conductive HL B. Right conductive HL C. Left sensorineural HL D. Right sensorineural HL

question: A 12-year-old boy is brought in by his mother with a 2-day history of fever and generalized weakness. question: A newborn male is evaluated in the hospital nursery 24 hours after birth for cyanosis. question: A 22-year-old woman comes to the physician because of hearing loss and unsteadiness while standing and walking for the past 2 months. question: A 57-year-old man comes to the physician for a follow-up evaluation of chronic, retrosternal chest pain. The pain is worse at night and after heavy meals. question: A 70-year-old man presented to a medical clinic for a routine follow-up. He has had hypertension for 20 years and is currently on multiple anti-hypertensive medications. question: An epidemiologist is interested in studying the clinical utility of a free computerized social skills training program for children with autism. A total of 125 participants with autism (mean age: 12 years) were recruited for the study and took part in weekly social skills training sessions for 3 months. Participants were recruited from support groups in a large Northeastern US city for parents with autistic children. Parents in the support group were very eager to volunteer for the study, and over 300 children were placed on a waiting list while the study was conducted. At baseline and at the end of the 3-month period, participants were observed during a videotaped social play exercise and scored on a social interaction rating scale by their parents. Social interaction rating scores following the 3-month intervention were more than twice as high as baseline scores (p < 0.001). During exit interviews, one parent commented, ""I knew from the start that this program was going to be life-changing for my son!"" This sentiment was echoed by a number of other parents. Which of the following is the most likely explanations for the study's result?". Answer Choices: A. Social desirability bias B. Observer bias C. Sampling bias D. Confounding bias
label: Observer bias
rationale: The most likely explanation for the study's result is [LABEL]. Parents, as evaluators, may have unintentionally rated their children more favorably due to their expectations or subjective perceptions of improvement. While the intervention itself may have contributed to some improvement, the lack of independent or blinded evaluation makes [LABEL] the most plausible explanation for the dramatic increase in scores.

question: A 40-year-old woman visits her physician’s office with her husband. Her husband says that she has been complaining of recurring headaches over the past few months. question: A 65-year-old obese man presents to his primary care clinic feeling weak. He was in the military and stationed in Vietnam in his youth.
