This imaging study will help confirm the presence of a pituitary adenoma, which is suggested by the partial suppression of cortisol with high-dose dexamethasone and the clinical features consistent with Cushing's disease.

question: A 62-year-old man comes to the physician because of fatigue and decreased urine output for 2 weeks. He has not been to the physician for many years and takes no medications. Serum studies show a urea nitrogen concentration of 42 mg/dL and a creatinine concentration of 2.3 mg/dL. Urinalysis shows heavy proteinuria. A photomicrograph of a section of a kidney biopsy specimen is shown. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?. Answer Choices: A. Diabetes mellitus B. Amyloidosis C. Fibromuscular dysplasia D. Severe hypertension
label: Diabetes mellitus
rationale: The most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms is [LABEL], specifically diabetic nephropathy leading to chronic kidney disease. This conclusion is supported by the presence of heavy proteinuria, elevated serum urea nitrogen and creatinine, and the likely biopsy findings of glomerular damage consistent with diabetes.

question: A 50-year-old man presents to the emergency department with pain and swelling of his right leg for the past 2 days. Three days ago he collapsed on his leg after tripping on a rug. It was a hard fall and left him with bruising of his leg. Since then the pain and swelling of his leg have been gradually increasing. Past medical history is noncontributory. He lives a rather sedentary life and smokes two packs of cigarettes per day. The vital signs include heart rate 98/min, respiratory rate 15/min, temperature 37.8°C (100.1°F), and blood pressure 100/60 mm Hg. On physical examination, his right leg is visibly swollen up to the mid-calf with pitting edema and moderate erythema. Peripheral pulses in the right leg are weak and the leg is tender. Manipulation of the right leg is negative for Homan’s sign. What is the next best step in the management of this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Make a diagnosis of deep vein thrombosis based on history and physical B. Perform a venous ultrasound C. Start intravenous heparin therapy immediately D. Perform intravenous venography within 24 hours
label: Perform a venous ultrasound
rationale: The next best step in the management of this patient is to [LABEL] to confirm or rule out deep vein thrombosis (DVT).

question: A 35-year-old hypertension patient. CXR shows?. Answer Choices: A. Left ventricular hypertrophy B. Pulmonary artery hypertension C. Coarctation of aorta D. Cor pulmonale
label: Coarctation of aorta
rationale: The CXR findings, in the context of a 35-year-old patient with hypertension, strongly suggest coarctation of the aorta as the underlying cause. This conclusion aligns with the clinical presentation and imaging findings.

question: Which of the following is NOT a source of atmospheric carbon?. Answer Choices: A. Respiration B. Photosynthesis C. Bacterial decomposition D. Combustion of fossil fuels
label: Photosynthesis
rationale: [LABEL] is NOT a source of atmospheric carbon. It is a process that removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, making the given answer correct.

question: Excitatory Neurotransmitter in CNS-. Answer Choices: A. Acetyl choline B. Glycine C. GABA D. Glutamate
label: Glutamate
rationale: [LABEL] is the chief excitatory neurotransmitter in the CNS, associated with excitatory extracellular ligand-gated ion channel activity.

question: In oesophageal ca which Neoadjuvant chemotherapy is used -. Answer Choices: A. Cisplatin B. Cyclophosphamide C. Doxorubicin D. Methotrexate
label: Cisplatin
rationale: [LABEL] is the neoadjuvant chemotherapy used in esophageal cancer, often in combination with 5-fluorouracil. This combination is well-established and achieves a significant response rate, making it a cornerstone of treatment.

question: A 35-year-old man attends an appointment with a fertility specialist together with his wife. The couple has been attempting to conceive for over 2 years but without success. She has recently undergone a comprehensive gynecological exam and all the results were normal. He states that he has no prior medical history to report. He says that he does have a low libido compared to other men of his age. On physical examination, he is observed to be of a lean build with a height of 6ft 3 inches with slight evidence of gynecomastia. His testes are small and underdeveloped. His lab tests show an elevation of LH and FSH, along with azoospermia. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this man’s infertility?. Answer Choices: A. Absence of chloride channel B. Primary ciliary dyskinesia C. Androgen insensitivity D. Presence of Barr body
label: Presence of Barr body
rationale: The most likely cause of this man's infertility is Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY), which is associated with the presence of a Barr body.

question: Orally active hormone is:. Answer Choices: A. TSH B. Thyroxine C. GH D. Prolactin
label: Thyroxine
rationale: The orally active hormone is [LABEL] (T4), specifically in its natural form, levo[LABEL]. The mention of "dextro[LABEL]" in the question may be misleading, as it is not the standard form used clinically.

question: A 62-year-old man comes to the physician for hematemesis and progressive heartburn over the past 5 days. Ten days ago, he was started on a medication to treat a condition that causes hearing difficulties and pain of the lower legs. He has no other history of serious illness. He has smoked 1 pack of cigarettes daily for the past 20 years. Physical examination shows bowing of the tibias. Upper endoscopy shows inflammation of the mucosa and a 1-cm punched-out ulcer in the distal esophagus. Which of the following drugs is the most likely cause of the patient's current condition?. Answer Choices: A. Calcium citrate B. Denosumab C. Risedronate D. Acetaminophen
label: Risedronate
rationale: The most likely cause of the patient's current condition, including gastroesophageal reflux and the development of a peptic ulcer, is risedronic acid. This medication was likely prescribed for Paget's disease of bone, given the tibial bowing, hearing difficulties, and lower leg pain. The gastrointestinal side effects of risedronic acid, combined with the patient's smoking history, likely led to the observed complications.

question: Assuming the circulatory system in humans obeys Bernoulli’s principle of fluid dynamics, which of the statements most accurately compares the blood pressure in a capillary of the neck to a capillary with an equal crosssectional area in the right knee?. Answer Choices: A. The pressure in the neck is greater than the pressure in the knee because of the increase in pressure head B. The pressure in the neck is equal to the pressure in the knee because of the equal dynamic pressure according to the continuity equation C. The pressure in the knee is greater than the pressure in the neck because of the increase in pressure head D. An accurate comparison cannot be given without knowledge of the fluid’s density and viscosity
label: The pressure in the knee is greater than the pressure in the neck because of the increase in pressure head
rationale: The pressure in the knee is greater than the pressure in the neck due to the influence of hydrostatic pressure caused by the height difference between the two locations. This aligns with the principles of fluid dynamics and the effects of gravity on blood flow.

question: Best prognostic indicator for head injured patients: AIIMS 10. Answer Choices: A. GCS B. CT findings C. Age of the patient D. History
label: GCS
rationale: The Glasgow Coma Scale ([LABEL]) is the best prognostic indicator for head and brain injury due to its simplicity, reliability, and strong correlation with patient outcomes.

question: A 34-year-old woman comes to the physician for evaluation of a breast lump she noticed 2 days ago while showering. She has no history of major illness. Her mother died of ovarian cancer at age 38, and her sister was diagnosed with breast cancer at age 33. Examination shows a 1.5-cm, nontender, mobile mass in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. Mammography shows pleomorphic calcifications. Biopsy of the mass shows invasive ductal carcinoma. The underlying cause of this patient's condition is most likely a mutation of a gene involved in which of the following cellular events?. Answer Choices: A. Activity of cytoplasmic tyrosine kinase B. Arrest of cell cycle in G1 phase C. Repair of double-stranded DNA breaks D. Inhibition of programmed cell death
label: Repair of double-stranded DNA breaks
rationale: The underlying cause of this patient's condition is most likely a mutation in a gene involved in the [LABEL], specifically BRCA1 or BRCA2. These genes are central to the homologous recombination repair pathway and are the most common culprits in hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndromes.

question: A 67-year-old woman presents to the infectious disease clinic after her PPD was found to be positive. A subsequent chest radiography shows a cavity in the apex of the right upper lobe, along with significant hilar adenopathy. The patient is diagnosed with tuberculosis and is started on the standard four-drug treatment regimen. Four weeks later, she returns for her first follow-up appointment in panic because her eyes have taken on an orange/red hue. Which of the following describes the mechanism of action of the drug most likely responsible for this side effect?. Answer Choices: A. Inhibition of RNA polymerase B. Inhibition of arabinosyltransferase C. Inhibition of mycolic acid synthesis D. Inhibition of squalene epoxidase
label: Inhibition of RNA polymerase
rationale: The drug most likely responsible for the orange/red hue side effect is rifampin, which works by inhibiting RNA polymerase activity.

question: Characteristic feature of korsakoff psychosis is. Answer Choices: A. Disorientation B. Delirium C. Coarse tremor D. Amnesia
label: Amnesia
rationale: [LABEL], specifically anterograde and retrograde [LABEL], is indeed a characteristic feature of Korsakoff psychosis. This aligns with the understanding that memory impairment is central to the condition.

question: All the following represent risk factors associated with pelvic inflammatory disease except. Answer Choices: A. Recent new sexual paner B. Doughing C. Low socioeconomic status D. Age 30 - 39 years
label: Age 30 - 39 years
rationale: Based on the reasoning process, age 30-39 years is not typically considered a risk factor for pelvic inflammatory disease. The risk factors are more closely associated with younger age groups, particularly those aged 10-19 years, due to the reasons outlined above. Therefore, the conclusion aligns with the statement that age 30-39 years is not a risk factor for PID.

question: A 27-year-old man is witnessed falling off his bicycle. The patient rode his bicycle into a curb and hit his face against a rail. The patient did not lose consciousness and is ambulatory at the scene. There is blood in the patient's mouth and one of the patient's teeth is found on the sidewalk. The patient is transferred to the local emergency department. Which of the following is the best method to transport this patient's tooth?. Answer Choices: A. Submerged in milk B. Submerged in normal saline C. Submerged in water D. Wrapped in gauze soaked in normal saline
label: Submerged in milk
rationale: The best method to transport the patient's incisor tooth is to submerge it in milk. This medium provides an optimal environment for preserving the periodontal ligament cells, increasing the likelihood of successful reimplantation.

question: A 68-year-old community-dwelling woman is transported to the emergency department with decreased consciousness, headache, and nausea. The symptoms began after the patient had a syncopal episode and fell at her home. She has a history of arterial hypertension and atrial fibrillation. Her current medications include hydrochlorothiazide, lisinopril, metoprolol, and warfarin. On admission, her blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg, heart rate is 83/min and irregular, respiratory rate is 12/min, and temperature is 36.8°C (98.4°F). She is conscious and verbally responsive, albeit confused. She is able to follow motor commands. Her pupils are round, equal, and poorly reactive to light. She is unable to abduct both eyes on an eye movement examination. She has decreased strength and increased tone (Ashworth 1/4) and reflexes (3+) in her right upper and lower extremities. Her lungs are clear to auscultation. The cardiac examination shows the presence of S3 and a pulse deficit. A head CT scan is shown in the picture. Which of the following led to the patient’s condition?. Answer Choices: A. Rupture of the middle meningeal artery B. Rupture of a saccular aneurysm in the carotid circulation region C. Laceration of the leptomeningeal blood vessels D. Rupture of the cerebral bridging veins
label: Rupture of the cerebral bridging veins
rationale: The most likely cause of the patient's condition is the rupture of cerebral bridging veins leading to a subdural hematoma, exacerbated by the use of warfarin. This aligns with the history of a fall, anticoagulation therapy, and the neurological symptoms observed.

question: A 50-year-old Caucasian man presents for a routine checkup. He does not have any current complaint. He is healthy and takes no medications. He has smoked 10–15 cigarettes per day for the past 10 years. His family history is negative for gastrointestinal disorders. Which of the following screening tests is recommended for this patient according to the United States Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF)?. Answer Choices: A. Prostate-specific antigen for prostate cancer B. Carcinoembryonic antigen for colorectal cancer C. Abdominal ultrasonography for abdominal aortic aneurysm D. Colonoscopy for colorectal cancer
label: Colonoscopy for colorectal cancer
rationale: A colonoscopy is recommended for colorectal cancer screening in this 50-year-old man, as per USPSTF guidelines. The recommendation is primarily based on his age and average risk status, with smoking history being a secondary consideration that may slightly increase his risk.

question: A phase 2, multicenter trial was conducted to determine the efficacy of a new vaccine for prevention of HIV infection. The study enrolled 4000 subjects, aged 20 to 65 years. Of these subjects, 2100 were men and 1900 were women; 2500 were white, 1000 were African American, 300 were Hispanic, and 200 were Asian/Pacific Islanders. Results of the trial showed no overall benefit of the vaccine. However, post hoc analysis disclosed a small but statistically significant vaccine protection among African American subjects. Which of the following is the most accurate rationale for questioning the validity of efficacy analysis of the HIV vaccine among the African American study subjects?. Answer Choices: A. HIV infection is more prevalent among African American populations B. The study was not blinded C. There was a Type II error D. Vaccine response among African American subjects was not the primary outcome measure
label: Vaccine response among African American subjects was not the primary outcome measure
rationale: The [LABEL], and the finding from the post hoc analysis should be interpreted with caution. The lack of pre-specified analysis, potential for confounding factors, and absence of a clear biological mechanism raise questions about the validity of the efficacy analysis in this subgroup. Further research is needed to confirm or refute this observation.

question: A 50-year-old man with a history of atrial fibrillation presents to his cardiologist’s office for a follow-up visit. He recently started treatment with an anti-arrhythmic drug to prevent future recurrences and reports that he has been feeling well and has no complaints. The physical examination shows that the arrhythmia appears to have resolved; however, there is now mild bradycardia. In addition, the electrocardiogram recording shows a slight prolongation of the PR and QT intervals. Which of the following drugs was most likely used to treat this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Carvedilol B. Propranolol C. Sotalol D. Verapamil
label: Sotalol
rationale: Based on the reasoning process, [LABEL] is the most likely drug used to treat this patient, given the combination of bradycardia, prolonged PR interval, and prolonged QT interval observed in the patient.

question: A 54-year-old woman presents with acute pain in her left toe. She says she hasn’t been able to wear closed shoes for 2 weeks. Past medical history is significant for gastroesophageal reflux disease, diagnosed 2 years ago. The patient is afebrile and vital signs are within normal limits. Her BMI is 31 kg/m2. On physical examination, the left toe is warm to touch, swollen, and erythematous. A joint fluid aspiration from the left toe is performed and shows needle-shaped negatively birefringent urate crystals. The patient is started on a xanthine oxidase inhibitor. On her follow-up visit 6 weeks later, she has an elevated homocysteine level, a decreased serum folic acid level, and a normal methylmalonic acid level. Which of the following drugs would most likely cause a similar side effect to that seen in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Cephalosporins B. Azathioprine C. α-Methyldopa D. Cisplatin
label: Azathioprine
rationale: [LABEL] is the drug most likely to cause a similar side effect profile, including elevated homocysteine levels and reduced blood folate concentration.

question: An investigator is studying the effects of hyperphosphatemia on calcium homeostasis. A high-dose phosphate infusion is administered intravenously to a healthy subject over the course of 3 hours. Which of the following sets of changes is most likely to occur in response to the infusion? $$$ Serum parathyroid hormone %%% Serum total calcium %%% Serum calcitriol %%% Urine phosphate $$$. Answer Choices: A. go down go down go down go down B. go up go down go down go up C. go up go up go up go up D. go up go up go up go down
label: go up go up go up go up
rationale: The high-dose phosphate infusion is likely to: 1. Increase serum parathyroid hormone receptor activity due to PTH stimulation. 2. Decrease serum total calcium initially due to calcium-phosphate precipitation and reduced calcitriol synthesis, potentially leading to hypocalcemia. 3. Increase serum calcitriol as a compensatory response to PTH stimulation, though this may be delayed. 4. Cause abnormal urine phosphate concentration (hyperphosphaturia) due to FGF23-mediated renal phosphate wasting. Thus, the infusion leads to increased parathyroid hormone receptor activity, abnormal urine phosphate concentration, and transient hypocalcemia. However, the claim of increased serum total calcium is inconsistent with the expected physiological response. Wait, there might be something wrong with the provided answer regarding serum total calcium.

question: The drug that inhibits uterine contractility and cause pulmonary edema is :. Answer Choices: A. Ritodrine B. Nifedipine C. lndomethacin D. Atosiban
label: Ritodrine
rationale: [LABEL] is the drug that inhibits uterine contractility and can cause pulmonary edema. Continuous monitoring, such as with a pulse oximeter, is necessary during intravenous beta-adrenergic therapy to detect and manage this serious complication.

question: A 29-year-old Hispanic woman, gravida 3, para 3, comes to the office because of recurrent low back pain during the past year. Rest and analgesics usually eradicate the pain within 2 weeks. However, the pain seems to recur every 2 to 3 months. Medical history is remarkable for gestational diabetes mellitus during her pregnancies. She takes no medications except for an oral contraceptive. She walks 3 miles daily for exercise and works as a sales representative for a computer software company. She is 165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 100 kg (220 lb); BMI is 37 kg/m2 . Vital signs are normal, and physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?. Answer Choices: A. Administer an epidural injection of methylprednisolone B. Order MRI of the lumbosacral spine C. Order x-rays of the lumbosacral spine D. Recommend beginning a weight loss program
label: Recommend beginning a weight loss program
rationale: The most appropriate next step is to [LABEL]. This approach addresses the likely underlying cause of her recurrent low back pain (obesity) and reduces her risk of developing other obesity-related complications, such as type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease.

question: Nine healthy subjects participate in a study of gastric secretions. Subjects are asked to eat a meal at hour 0, at which time the pH of stomach contents and rate of stomach acid secretions are measured over the next 4 hours. Results of the study are shown. Which of the following mediators is most active at point A in the graph?. Answer Choices: A. Prostaglandin B. Somatostatin C. Glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide D. Acetylcholine
label: Acetylcholine
rationale: The mediator most active at point A in the graph is [LABEL], acting primarily through CHRM3 receptors in the stomach.

question: Bacterial cell wall is composed of all except -?. Answer Choices: A. Muramic acid B. Teichoic acid C. Glucosamine D. Mucopeptide
label: Teichoic acid
rationale: The statement "[LABEL] is not a component of the bacterial cell wall" is incorrect. [LABEL] is a key component of the bacterial cell wall in Gram-positive bacteria. It is not found in Gram-negative bacteria, but this does not exclude it from being a cell wall component in bacteria overall.

question: A 41-year-old woman with a past medical history significant for asthma and seasonal allergies presents with a new rash. She has no significant past surgical, social, or family history. The patient's blood pressure is 131/90 mm Hg, the pulse is 77/min, the respiratory rate is 17/min, and the temperature is 36.9°C (98.5°F). Physical examination reveals a sharply demarcated area of skin dryness and erythema encircling her left wrist. Review of systems is otherwise negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?. Answer Choices: A. Scabies B. Atopic dermatitis C. Psoriasis D. Contact dermatitis
label: Contact dermatitis
rationale: The most likely diagnosis for the patient’s condition is [LABEL], likely due to exposure to an irritant or allergen on her wrist.

question: A 34-year-old man is admitted to the hospital because of a 3-week history of abdominal distention and yellowing of the skin. He also has a 2-year history of progressively worsening breathlessness and cough. Three days after admission, he suddenly develops peritonitis and sepsis. Despite appropriate care, he dies. At autopsy, histopathological examination of liver and lung tissue shows periodic acid-Schiff-positive (PAS-positive) globules within periportal hepatocytes and low levels of a protein that is responsible for the recoil of the lungs during expiration. Which of the following processes most likely contributes to the elastic properties of this protein?. Answer Choices: A. Oxidative deamination of lysine residues B. Arrangement in a triple helical structure C. Formation of disulfide bridges D. N-glycosylation of serine residues
label: Oxidative deamination of lysine residues
rationale: The process contributing to the elastic properties of the extracellular matrix constituent conferring elasticity is peptidyl-lysine oxidation through [LABEL], mediated by lysyl oxidase (LOX). This aligns with the histopathological findings and the clinical presentation of alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency.

question: A 17-year-old white male is brought to the emergency department after being struck by a car. He complains of pain in his right leg and left wrist, and slowly recounts how he was hit by a car while being chased by a lion. In between sentences of the story, he repeatedly complains of dry mouth and severe hunger and requests something to eat and drink. His mother arrives and is very concerned about this behavior, noting that he has been withdrawn lately and doing very poorly in school the past several months. Notable findings on physical exam include conjunctival injection bilaterally and a pulse of 107. What drug is this patient most likely currently abusing?. Answer Choices: A. Cocaine B. Phencylidine (PCP) C. Benzodiazepines D. Marijuana
label: Marijuana
rationale: The drug this patient is most likely currently abusing is [LABEL], which contains dronabinol. This conclusion is supported by the presence of xerostomia, severe hunger, conjunctival injection, tachycardia, and behavioral changes, all of which are consistent with [LABEL] use.

question: The esophagus crosses the diaphragm at the level of:. Answer Choices: A. T8 B. T9 C. T10 D. T11
label: T10
rationale: The esophagus crosses the diaphragm at the level of the [LABEL] vertebra and ends at the gastric cardiac orifice, which is at the level of the T11 vertebra.

question: An inpatient psychiatrist recently had two patients who developed serious gastrointestinal infections while taking clozapine. He was concerned that his patients had developed agranulocytosis, a relatively rare but dangerous adverse event associated with clozapine. When the psychiatrist checked the absolute neutrophil count (ANC) of both patients, one was 450/mm3, while the other was 700/mm3 (N=1,500/mm3). According to the clozapine REMS (Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy) program, severe neutropenia in clozapine recipients has often been defined as an absolute neutrophil count (ANC) less than 500/mm3. Changing the cutoff value to 750/mm3 would affect the test performance of ANC with regard to agranulocytosis in which of the following ways?. Answer Choices: A. Increased positive predictive value B. Decreased true positives C. Increased false positives D. Decreased sensitivity "
label: Increased false positives
rationale: Changing the cutoff value for severe neutropenia from 500/mm³ to 750/mm³ would likely increase the number of false positives in identifying patients at risk for agranulocytosis. This is because more patients would be classified as having severe neutropenia, even if their risk of developing agranulocytosis is not significantly elevated.

question: A 7-year-old boy is brought in to clinic by his parents with a chief concern of poor performance in school. The parents were told by the teacher that the student often does not turn in assignments, and when he does they are partially complete. The child also often shouts out answers to questions and has trouble participating in class sports as he does not follow the rules. The parents of this child also note similar behaviors at home and have trouble getting their child to focus on any task such as reading. The child is even unable to watch full episodes of his favorite television show without getting distracted by other activities. The child begins a trial of behavioral therapy that fails. The physician then tries pharmacological therapy. Which of the following is most likely the mechanism of action of an appropriate treatment for this child's condition?. Answer Choices: A. Increases the duration of GABAa channel opening B. Antagonizes NMDA receptors C. Decreases synaptic reuptake of norepinephrine and dopamine D. Blockade of D2 receptors
label: Decreases synaptic reuptake of norepinephrine and dopamine
rationale: The mechanism of action of the appropriate treatment for this condition is decreasing synaptic reuptake of norepinephrine and dopamine. This aligns with the use of stimulant medications for ADHD.

question: Tamsulosin belongs to. Answer Choices: A. 5a-reductase inhibitors B. a1a receptor blockers C. a1a receptor agonist D. 5a-transferase inhibitors
label: a1a receptor blockers
rationale: Tamsulosin is effective in reducing symptoms of benign prostatic hyperplasia by specifically targeting the alpha-1A adrenergic receptors, leading to relaxation of smooth muscle in the bladder neck and prostate. This action reduces urinary outflow resistance. Other pharmacologic therapies, such as 5-alpha reductase inhibitors like Finasteride and Dutasteride, are also used to manage BPH by reducing prostate volume.

question: An 18-year-old man moves from sea level to an elevation of 2,400 m to train as a skier. The increased requirement for oxygen delivery to tissues at the higher elevation stimulates the synthesis of a renal hormone (erythropoietin), which targets hematopoietic stem cells in the bone marrow. Erythropoietin promotes the survival of early erythroid progenitor cells primarily through which of the following mechanisms?. Answer Choices: A. Altered cell-matrix adhesion B. Downregulation of p53 C. Enhanced glucose uptake D. Inhibition of apoptosis
label: Inhibition of apoptosis
rationale: Erythropoietin promotes the survival of early erythroid progenitor cells by inhibiting programmed cell death (negative regulation of cell death). This process increases red blood cell production and hemoglobin concentration, enhancing oxygen delivery to tissues at high altitudes.

question: A young woman is evaluated by a neurosurgeon because of injuries she received in an explosion. She has numerous lacerations of the front and back torso and bruising of the craniofacial region. Neurological examination shows lack of movement in her right lower extremity with hyperreflexia, and loss of proprioception and fine touch in this extremity, but pain and temperature sensation are intact. Pain and temperature sensation are absent in the left lower limb. Movement and reflexes are normal in the left lower extremity and upper extremities. A lesion in which of the following locations can explain her neurological examination?. Answer Choices: A. Hemisection of the left side of the spinal cord at the level of the first lumbar segment of the cord B. Hemisection of the left side of the spinal cord at the level of the fourth sacral segment of the cord C. Hemisection of the right side of the spinal cord at the level of the first lumbar segment of the cord D. Hemisection of the right side of the spinal cord at the level of the fourth sacral segment of the cord
label: Hemisection of the right side of the spinal cord at the level of the first lumbar segment of the cord
rationale: The symptoms are consistent with a hemisection of the right side of the spinal cord at the level of the first lumbar segment. This would explain the ipsilateral motor and proprioceptive deficits and the contralateral loss of pain and temperature sensation, aligning with the clinical presentation of Brown-Séquard syndrome.

question: A 58-year-old man presents with a sudden-onset severe headache and vomiting for the past 2 hours. Past medical history is significant for poorly controlled hypertension, managed with multiple medications. His blood pressure is 188/87 mm Hg and pulse is 110/min. A non-contrast CT of the head is unremarkable and cerebrospinal fluid analysis is within normal limits, except for an RBC count of 5.58 x 106/mm3. Labetalol IV is administered. Which of the following medications should also be added to this patient’s management?. Answer Choices: A. Nifedipine B. Furosemide C. Nimodipine D. Ecosprin
label: Nimodipine
rationale: [LABEL] should be added to this patient’s management to prevent cerebral vasospasm, a critical complication of subarachnoid hemorrhage. The clinical presentation, abnormal CSF findings, and history of malignant hypertension strongly support the diagnosis of SAH, even in the absence of CT findings.

question: Vesicular lesions, indistinguishable from primary infection, which arise in other pas of the body of an allergic individual infected with Trichophyton are referred to as. Answer Choices: A. Dermatiphytids B. Trichophytins C. Eschars D. Carbuncles
label: Dermatiphytids
rationale: The lesions described in the question are referred to as dermatophytids, which are hypersensitivity reactions to dermatophyte antigens. They occur distal to the primary site of infection, are immune-mediated, and fungal cultures from these lesions are often negative.

question: Risk factor prevention at which level. Answer Choices: A. Primordial B. Primary C. Secondary D. Tertiary
label: Primordial
rationale: [LABEL] prevention aims to minimize the emergence of risk factors for conditions like hypertension, abdominal obesity, and anxiety by promoting healthy behaviors and addressing underlying social and environmental determinants. This aligns with the reasoning that early interventions targeting lifestyle behaviors and environmental factors can prevent the development of these conditions.

question: A 33-year-old Honduran woman presents to your clinic with shortness of breath. She reports that her symptoms have progressed over the past several months and are now impacting her quality of life because she cannot complete her usual exercise routine. She recalls "normal" childhood illnesses, including sore throats and fevers, but never required hospitalization. Vital signs are temperature 37 degrees Celsius, blood pressure 110/70 mm Hg, heart rate 109/min, respiratory rate 22/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Physical exam reveals a holosystolic, high-pitched, blowing murmur at the cardiac apex. One would expect that this murmur would also:. Answer Choices: A. Radiate to the neck B. Increase with squatting or handgrip C. Also have a mid-systolic click loudest at S2 D. Have a characteristic machine-like sound
label: Increase with squatting or handgrip
rationale: The holosystolic murmur at the cardiac apex would [LABEL], consistent with mitral regurgitation.

question: Rigor mois not seen in: AIIMS 13. Answer Choices: A. Fetus < 7 months of age B. Full term fetus C. Elderly D. Anemic person
label: Fetus < 7 months of age
rationale: Rigor mortis is absent in a fetus under 7 months of age due to the physiological immaturity of muscle tissue, underdeveloped mitochondrial function, and incomplete biochemical pathways required for ATP depletion and muscle stiffness.

question: A 28-year-old woman with HIV comes to the physician because of an 8-day history of severe pain while swallowing. She has been hospitalized several times with opportunistic infections and has poor adherence to her antiretroviral drug regimen. Endoscopy shows extensive, white, plaque-like lesions in the proximal esophagus. Culture of a biopsy specimen grows Candida albicans. Treatment with intravenous anidulafungin is initiated. Which of the following is the primary mechanism of action of this drug?. Answer Choices: A. Decreased DNA synthesis B. Binding to tubulin C. Decreased glucan synthesis D. Inhibition of squalene epoxidase
label: Decreased glucan synthesis
rationale: Anidulafungin works by inhibiting 1,3-beta-D-glucan synthase, which is its primary mechanism of action. This inhibition disrupts the synthesis of the fungal cell wall, effectively treating Candida infections.

question: A 51-year-old man presents to the urgent care center with a blood pressure of 201/111 mm Hg. He is complaining of a severe headache and chest pain. Physical examination reveals regular heart sounds and clear bilateral lung sounds. Ischemic changes are noted on his electrocardiogram (ECG). What is the most appropriate treatment for this patient’s high blood pressure?. Answer Choices: A. Oral beta-blocker - lower mean arterial pressure no more than 25% over the 1st hour B. IV labetalol - redose until blood pressure within normal limits C. IV labetalol - lower mean arterial pressure no more than 50% over the 1st hour D. IV labetalol - lower mean arterial pressure no more than 25% over the 1st hour
label: IV labetalol - lower mean arterial pressure no more than 25% over the 1st hour
rationale: IV labetalol is the most appropriate treatment to lower mean arterial pressure no more than 25% over the first hour in this patient with a hypertensive emergency, severe headache, chest pain, and ischemic changes on ECG.

question: A 8-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his father for an annual well-check. His dad reports that he has been “difficult to handle” as he would not listen and follow instructions at home. “Telling him to sit still and do something is just so hard,” the father says. His teacher also reports difficulties in the classroom where the child would talk out of turn and interrupt the class intermittently by doing something else. His grades have been suffering as a result. Otherwise, the patient has been healthy and up to date on his immunizations. What is the best course of management for this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Haloperidol B. Methylphenidate C. Psychodynamic therapy D. Reassurance
label: Methylphenidate
rationale: [LABEL] is the best course of management for this patient, as the symptoms strongly suggest ADHD, and pharmacological treatment is appropriate for an 8-year-old with significant functional impairment.

question: About Vi polysaccharide vacine, true is:. Answer Choices: A. Can be given in patients with yellow fever and hepatitis B B. Has many contraindications C. Has many serious systemic side effects D. Has many serious local side effects
label: Can be given in patients with yellow fever and hepatitis B
rationale: The Typhoid Vi polysaccharide vaccine is safe for use in patients with yellow fever and hepatitis B. There are no contraindications or safety concerns that would prevent its administration in these patients.

question: A 25-year-old female comes to the clinic complaining of fatigue and palpitations. She has been undergoing immense stress from her thesis defense and has been extremely tired. The patient denies any weight loss, diarrhea, cold/heat intolerance. TSH was within normal limits. She reports a family history of "blood disease" and was later confirmed positive for B-thalassemia minor. It is believed that abnormal splicing of the beta globin gene results in B-thalassemia. What is removed during this process that allows RNA to be significantly shorter than DNA?. Answer Choices: A. 3'-poly(A) tail B. Exons C. Introns D. snRNPs
label: Introns
rationale: During RNA splicing, pre-mRNA intronic binding allows [LABEL] to be removed, making RNA significantly shorter than DNA. This process is disrupted in beta thalassemia due to mutations in the beta globin gene, leading to defective splicing and the associated clinical symptoms of anemia, fatigue, and palpitations.

question: Which of the following approach of brachial block is most suitable for catheter placement:-. Answer Choices: A. Supraclavicular B. Infraclavicular C. Axillary D. All
label: Infraclavicular
rationale: The [LABEL] approach has similar rates of spontaneous pneumothorax compared to other techniques but significantly reduces the risk of phrenic nerve involvement and diaphragmatic paralysis. It provides reliable anesthesia for the upper arm and is advantageous for catheter placement due to its proximity to the chest wall. This makes it a suitable and effective choice for patients, particularly when shoulder positioning is limited or respiratory function is a concern.

question: Chronic desquamative gingivitis is seen in which type of lichen planus:. Answer Choices: A. Atrophic lichen planus. B. Hypertrophic lichen planus. C. Ulcerative lichen planus. D. Hyperplastic lichen planus.
label: Atrophic lichen planus
rationale: The type of lichen planus most commonly associated with chronic desquamative gingivitis is [LABEL].

question: A 31-year-old G1P0 woman at 26 weeks gestation presents to the clinic for evaluation of an abnormal glucose tolerance test. She denies any symptoms, but states that she was given 50 g of oral glucose 1 week earlier and demonstrated a subsequent venous plasma glucose level of 156 mg/dL 1 hour later. The vital signs are: blood pressure, 112/78 mm Hg; pulse, 81/min; and respiratory rate, 16/min. Physical examination is within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?. Answer Choices: A. Repeat the 50 g oral glucose challenge B. Administer an oral, 3-hour 100 g glucose dose C. Advise the patient to follow an American Diabetic Association diet plan D. Begin insulin treatment
label: Administer an oral, 3-hour 100 g glucose dose
rationale: The most appropriate next step in management is to administer an oral, 3-hour 100 g glucose tolerance test to further evaluate the abnormal glucose challenge test.

question: A 66-year-old man with congestive heart failure presents to the emergency department complaining of worsening shortness of breath. These symptoms have worsened over the last 3 days. He has a blood pressure of 126/85 mm Hg and heart rate of 82/min. Physical examination is notable for bibasilar crackles. A chest X-ray reveals bilateral pulmonary edema. His current medications include metoprolol succinate and captopril. You wish to add an additional medication targeted towards his symptoms. Of the following, which statement is correct regarding loop diuretics?. Answer Choices: A. Loop diuretics inhibit the action of the Na+/K+/Cl- cotransporter B. Loop diuretics can cause ammonia toxicity C. Loop diuretics can cause metabolic acidosis D. Loop diuretics can cause hyperlipidemia
label: Loop diuretics inhibit the action of the Na+/K+/Cl- cotransporter
rationale: The most appropriate additional medication to target this patient's symptoms is loop diuretics, such as furosemide. These drugs act by inhibiting the Na+/K+/Cl- cotransporter in the loop of Henle, effectively reducing fluid overload and relieving pulmonary congestion.

question: A 72-year-old man presents to the physician with severe lower back pain and fatigue for 3 months. The pain increases with activity. He has no history of a serious illness. He takes ibuprofen for pain relief. He does not smoke. The blood pressure is 105/65 mm Hg, the pulse is 86/min, the respirations are 16/min, and the temperature is 36.7℃ (98.1℉). The conjunctivae are pale. Palpation over the 1st lumbar vertebra shows tenderness. The heart, lung, and abdominal examination shows no abnormalities. No lymphadenopathy is palpated. label: Multiple myeloma
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is plasma cell myeloma ([LABEL]).

question: A 44-year-old female with a 3-year history of biliary colic presents with acute cholecystitis. After further evaluation, she undergoes a laparoscopic cholecystectomy without complication. Which of the following is true following this procedure?. Answer Choices: A. Lipid absorption is decreased B. Lipid absorption is increased C. Lipid absorption is unaffected D. The overall amount of bile acids is reduced
label: Lipid absorption is unaffected
rationale: Following a laparoscopic cholecystectomy, intestinal lipid absorption remains unaffected. This is because bile production by the liver continues, and the small intestine adapts to the continuous bile flow, ensuring normal lipid digestion and absorption.

question: The Vitamin A activity of beta-carotene compared to that of retinol is?. Answer Choices: A. 2-Jan B. 4-Jan C. 6-Jan D. 8-Jan
label: 6-Jan
rationale: Beta-carotene possesses about one-sixth of the Vitamin A activity compared to that of retinol. This is due to the inefficient enzymatic conversion of beta-carotene to retinal in humans, as well as factors like bioavailability and absorption.

question: Glycolysis is the name given to the pathway involving the conversion of:. Answer Choices: A. glycogen to glucose-1-phosphate. B. glycogen or glucose to fructose. C. glycogen or glucose to pyruvate or lactate. D. glycogen or glucose to pyruvate or acetyl CoA.
label: glycogen or glucose to pyruvate or lactate
rationale: The conversion of glucose to pyruvate or lactate in glycolysis can involve glucokinase activity, but this is tissue-specific and not the sole enzyme responsible for this step. Hexokinase isoforms (e.g., HK1, HK2) also play a significant role in glycolysis, depending on the tissue. Therefore, while the statement is partially correct, it is not universally applicable.

question: Most characteristic of megaloblastic anemia ?. Answer Choices: A. Target cell B. Macrocyte C. Microovalocyte D. Macroovalocyte
label: Macroovalocyte
rationale: Oval macrocytosis, also referred to as [LABEL]s, is the most characteristic feature of megaloblastic anemia. This morphological change is a direct result of impaired DNA synthesis due to vitamin B12 or folate deficiency.

question: A patient presents with intermittent headache. On examination there is hypeension and a thyroid nodule. Which of the following steps is to be taken next -. Answer Choices: A. Urine HIAA levels B. Urine VMA and aspiration of the thyroickiodule C. Ultrasound abdomen D. Echocardiography
label: Urine VMA and aspiration of the thyroickiodule
rationale: The next steps in managing this patient should include: 1. Performing urine VMA testing to confirm catecholamine secretion indicative of pheochromocytoma. 2. Conducting a fine-needle aspiration of the thyroid nodule to assess for medullary thyroid carcinoma. These steps are crucial for confirming the diagnosis of MEN II syndrome and guiding further management.

question: A 25-year-old man comes to the physician for the evaluation of recurrent episodes of nosebleeds over the past 6 months. The nosebleeds occur spontaneously and stop after 10 minutes after pinching the nose at the nostrils. He has no history of serious illness except for prolonged bleeding following wisdom teeth extraction 2 years ago. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. He takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination of the nose shows no abnormalities. There are several bruises on the lower extremities. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 15 g/dL Leukocyte count 6,000/mm3 Platelet count 220,000/mm3 Bleeding time 9 minutes Prothrombin time 13 sec Partial thromboplastin time 55 sec Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?". Answer Choices: A. Bernard-Soulier Syndrome B. Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome C. Factor X deficiency D. Von Willebrand disease
label: Von Willebrand disease
rationale: Based on the symptoms of recurrent nosebleeds, prolonged bleeding after dental procedures, bruising, and the laboratory finding of prolonged PTT, the most likely diagnosis is [LABEL]. This condition aligns with the patient's presentation and the laboratory findings.

question: A 37-year-old man, otherwise healthy, has a routine CBC done prior to donating blood for the first time. The results are as follows: Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) 82 μm3 Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) 42% Reticulocyte count 3.2% White blood cell count 8,700/mm3 Platelet count 325,000/mm3 The patient is afebrile and his vital signs are within normal limits. label: Inherited membrane abnormality of red cells
rationale: The patient most likely has hereditary spherocytosis, a form of constitutional hemolytic anemia caused by a red cell membrane defect. This condition is consistent with the clinical findings of splenomegaly, increased MCHC, reticulocytosis, and a negative DAT.

question: A 73-year-old man presents to his primary care doctor with his son who reports that his father has been acting strangely. rationale: A brain biopsy in this patient would most likely reveal [LABEL] (Lewy bodies), consistent with a diagnosis of Lewy body dementia (LBD).

question: A 19-year-old woman presents with worsening pain in her neck for the past 5 days. She says she is not able to wear her tie for her evening job because is it too painful. question: An 18-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with a complaint of excessive daytime sleepiness. He denies any substance abuse or major changes in his sleep schedule. label: Hypothalamus
rationale: This patient is likely deficient in hypocretin, a neurotransmitter produced in the [LABEL], which is consistent with a diagnosis of narcolepsy type 1.

question: All are immediate causes of death in burns except. Answer Choices: A. Injury B. Suffocation C. Toxemia D. Shock

rationale: [LABEL] is not an immediate cause of death in burns but is categorized as a delayed cause.

question: A 30-year-old man presents to the physician after he discovered a raised, red, string-shaped lesion beneath the skin on his right foot. question: A 15-month-old girl is brought to the pediatrician by her mother with a history of 3 episodes of breath-holding spells. rationale: The patient is most likely to develop hand apraxia, including the [LABEL], particularly involving the inner digits of the hand. This is consistent with a diagnosis of atypical Rett syndrome, given the clinical presentation of secondary microcephaly, stereotypical hand wringing, poor social interaction, and generalized hypotonia.

question: A 66-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a motor vehicle accident. The patient was a restrained passenger in a car that was struck on the passenger side while crossing an intersection. In the emergency department, he is alert and complaining of abdominal pain. rationale: The most likely explanation for additional [LABEL] in this patient is referred pain due to diaphragmatic irritation from intra-abdominal injury (e.g., hemoperitoneum or organ laceration). This aligns with the findings of abdominal tenderness and fluid in Morrison's pouch. Therefore, [LABEL] is a plausible additional symptom.

question: The crown of a very carious tooth suddenly fractures while the patient is standing eating at a take-away food outlet and a fragment is inhaled. It is more likely to enter the. Answer Choices: A. right lung because the right main bronchus is wider and more vertical than the left. B. left lung because the left main bronchus is wider and more vertical than the right. C. right lung or left lung because the two main bronchi are of equal size and at the same angulation. rationale: The fragment is more likely to enter the right lung because the right main bronchus is wider, shorter, and more vertical than the left main bronchus, making it the path of least resistance for inhaled objects.

question: A 75-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of fatigue and black sticky stools during the past 3 days. rationale: The most likely underlying cause of this patient’s current condition is impaired platelet aggregation due to uremia from chronic kidney disease secondary to polycystic kidney disease.

question: Hernia with highest rate of strangulation is?. Answer Choices: A. Direct inguinal hernia B. Indirect inguinal hernia C. Femoral hernia D. Incisional hernia

question: A 42-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after having a seizure. Vitamin D

rationale: The most likely nutritional deficiency contributing to this patient's findings is [LABEL] deficiency. This is supported by the patient's history of alcohol abuse, malnutrition, and the constellation of symptoms and signs, including seizures, muscle weakness, brisk reflexes, prolonged QT interval, and palpitations.

question: Which of the following is true about sickle cell red blood cells?. Answer Choices: A. Stability B. Altered function C. Decreased oxygen carrying capacity D. Protective against adult malaria
label: Protective against adult malaria
rationale: HbA/S heterozygotes exhibit a cellular response to decreased oxygen levels, which impairs parasite growth and reduces cytoadherence to the microvasculature. This protection against malaria is mediated by the altered red blood cell environment and the disruption of parasite-host interactions, particularly involving symbiont proteins and host adhesion molecules like ICAM-1.

question: A 13-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents 30 minutes after having a 1-minute seizure. Examination shows a distended abdomen. The extremities are cool to the touch, and his capillary refill time is 2–3 seconds. Further evaluation is most likely to show which of the following?. label: Sunken anterior fontanelle
rationale: Further evaluation is most likely to show a [LABEL], indicating hypovolemia due to dehydration from diarrhea, vomiting, and fever.

question: A 80 year old patient presents with a midline tumor of the lower jaw, involving the alveolar margin. He is edentulous. Rx of choice is:. Answer Choices: A. Hemi mandibulectomy B. Commando operation C. Segmental mandiblectomy D. Marginal mandibulectomy

rationale: The most appropriate treatment for a midline carcinoma of the lower jaw involving the alveolar ridge is segmental mandibulectomy. This approach ensures complete removal of the tumor with clear margins, which is critical for preventing recurrence. While alternative reasoning paths explore developmental and genetic factors, they are not directly applicable to this case.

question: A 44-year-old man presents to his primary care physician for muscle pain and weakness. He says that his muscle pain mainly affects his legs. He also experiences difficulty with chewing gum and has poor finger dexterity. Medical history is significant for infertility and cataracts. On physical exam, the patient's face is long and narrow with a high arched palate and mild frontal balding. There is bilateral ptosis and temporalis muscle and sternocleidomastoid muscle wasting. rationale: Genetic testing is most likely to reveal a CTG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the DMPK gene, confirming the diagnosis of myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). This explains the patient's skeletal muscle atrophy, impaired mastication, poor hand-eye coordination, and myalgia.

question: Durck granuloma is seen in. Answer Choices: A. Congenital syphilis B. Cat scratch disease C. Histoplasmosis D. Cerebral malaria

rationale: The damage in [LABEL] results in the formation of Durck's granuloma, which is a collection of microglial cells surrounding an area of demyelination. This conclusion aligns with the pathophysiology of [LABEL] and the role of microglial cells in responding to brain tissue damage.

question: When assessing a patient's breathing:. Answer Choices: A. always remove dentures. B. look for chest movements and use a mirror to check for exhaled air. C. look for chest movements, listen for breath sounds, and feel for exhaled air on your cheek. D. assess for 30 seconds.

This aligns with the need to evaluate both mechanical and functional aspects of breathing, ensuring a comprehensive assessment of the respiratory system.

question: A 21-year-old woman presents to her primary care doctor for an initial visit. She is a Syrian refugee and arrived in the United States 2 weeks ago. question: A 56-year-old man presents to his primary care doctor to discuss his plans for diet and exercise. He currently has hypertension treated with thiazide diuretics but is otherwise healthy. On exam, his temperature is 98.8°F (37.1°C), blood pressure is 122/84 mmHg, pulse is 70/min, and respirations are 12/min. His weight is 95.2 kilograms and his BMI is 31.0 kg/m^2. Which of the following represents the approximate number of grams of carbohydrates the patient should consume per day?. Answer Choices: A. 67 B. 122 C. 275 D. 324

rationale: The most common cyanotic congenital heart disease is [LABEL]. This conclusion is supported by its clinical presentation, pathophysiology, and epidemiological prevalence.

question: A 60-year-old gentleman passes away after a car accident. On routine autopsy it is incidentally noted that he has both a ventral and dorsal pancreatic duct. question: An immunologist is studying the stages of development of T lymphocytes in the thymus. He knows that double-negative T cells do not express CD4 or CD8 molecules. question: Investigation of choice for lumbar prolapsed disc. Answer Choices: A. X-ray B. CT Scan C. MRI D. Myelogram Patt
label: MRI
rationale: Based on the reasoning above, [LABEL] is the investigation of choice for a lumbar prolapsed disc due to its ability to provide detailed visualization of soft tissues, intervertebral discs, and nerve roots, as well as its safety and diagnostic accuracy.

question: A 36-year-old man is brought to the emergency department for right upper quadrant abdominal pain that began 3 days ago. The pain is nonradiating and has no alleviating or exacerbating factors. He denies any nausea or vomiting. He immigrated from Mexico 6 months ago and currently works at a pet shop. He has been healthy except for 1 week of bloody diarrhea 5 months ago. He is 182 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 120 kg (264 lb); BMI is 36 kg/m2. His temperature is 101.8°F (38.8°C), pulse is 85/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 120/75 mm Hg. Lungs are clear to auscultation. question: A 22-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of cough and decreased urination. question: A 29-year-old woman, gravida 1, para 0, at 33 weeks' gestation comes to her doctor for a routine visit. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. rationale: The next best step in management is delivery, not necessarily [LABEL]. The mode of delivery (induction vs. cesarean) would depend on the fetal and maternal status, but the priority is to address the potential fetal compromise due to severe oligohydramnios and abnormal BPP.

question: A 12-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with a recent history of easy bleeding. He experienced multiple episodes of epistaxis and bleeding gums over the past two days. question: A 64-year-old nulliparous woman comes to the physician because of fatigue and an increase in abdominal girth despite a 5-kg (11.0-lb) weight loss over the past 6 months. question: A 57-year-old man comes to the emergency department for the evaluation of worsening fatigue, urinary frequency, and constipation over the past 5 days. question: A 19-year-old female complains of abnormal facial hair growth. This has been very stressful for her, especially in the setting of not being happy with her weight. question: Which of the following amino acid contains free sulphydryl group. Answer Choices: A. Cysteine B. Methionine C. Serine D. Glycine

question: Uterine prolapse is prevented by all the following EXCEPT:. Answer Choices: A. Broad ligament of uterus B. Levator ani muscle C. Uterosacral ligament D. Transcervical ligament

Its primary function is to act as a mesentery for the uterus, fallopian tubes, and ovaries, providing a pathway for blood vessels, lymphatics, and nerves rather than mechanical support.

question: A 53-year-old man with obesity and heart disease presents to your outpatient clinic with complaints of orthopnea, significant dyspnea on minimal exertion, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. question: A 70-year-old man is at his dermatologist’s office for the treatment of a severely pruritic erythroderma with scaling on his buttocks that has been slowly progressing over the past two weeks. question: Hot flushes are experienced as a result of. Answer Choices: A. Increased noradrenaline B. Decreased estrogen C. Increased noradrenaline and decreased estrogen D. Increased noradrenaline and estrogen

question: A 43-year-old woman visits her primary care physician complaining of abdominal pain for the past 6 months. question: A 22-year-old woman presents to the emergency department feeling lightheaded and states that her heart is racing. She does not have a history of any chronic medical conditions. question: A 40-year-old woman who is a known case of anxiety disorder and has prominent symptoms of frontal headache is being treated with a procedure in which the tension in the frontalis muscle is measured regularly. question: A 67-year-old man presents with fatigue, progressive abdominal distention and yellow skin coloration for the past 2 weeks. He denies fever, chills, or other symptoms. question: A previously healthy 13-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her mother because of a change in behavior. question: A CKD patient had to undergo dialysis. His Hb was 5.2gm% so two blood transfusions were to be given. First bag was completed in 2 hours. question: A 21-year-old gravida 1 presents to her physician’s office for an antepartum visit at 11 weeks gestation. She has complaints of malaise, occasional nausea, and changes in food preferences. question: Private Doctor refusing to treating a case of sexual offence and referring to govt hospital is. Answer Choices: A. Not an offence B. Punishable under 166 B IPC C. Punishable under 357 (c) Cr P C D. Punishable under 327 (c) Cr P C

question: Which of the following anaesthetic modality is to be avoided in sickle cell disease?. Answer Choices: A. General anaesthesia B. Brachial plexus block C. IV. Regional Anaesthesia D. Spinal

question: A 58-year-old man presents with an occasional tremor in his left hand. label: Pulmonary orifice and atrioventricular orifice
rationale: The supraventricular crest lies between the pulmonary orifice and the atrioventricular (tricuspid) orifice, separating the inlet and outlet parts of the right ventricle.

question: Over 1 year, a study is conducted to assess the antileukemic activity of a new tyrosine kinase inhibitor in patients with chronic myeloid leukemia in blast crisis. All patients enrolled in the study are informed that they would be treated with the tyrosine kinase inhibitor. They are assigned to successive dose cohorts of 300 to 1000 mg/day of the drug. Six to eight patients are assigned to each dose. Treatment efficacy is determined based on the results of complete blood counts and bone marrow assessments conducted regularly throughout the study. This study is best described as which of the following?. Answer Choices: A. Case-control study B. Crossover study C. Open-labeled clinical trial D. Randomized clinical trial
label: Open-labeled clinical trial
rationale: The study is best described as an open-label clinical trial involving a tyrosine kinase inhibitor, such as Dasatinib, targeting the BCR-ABL1 fusion protein in patients with chronic myeloid leukemia in blast crisis.

question: A 36-year-old nulligravid woman comes to the physician because of a 1-year history of pelvic discomfort and heavy menstrual bleeding. The pain is dull and pressure-like and occurs intermittently; the patient is asymptomatic between episodes. Menses occur at regular 30-day intervals and last 8 days with heavy flow. Her last menstrual period ended 5 days ago. She is sexually active and does not use contraception. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.8°F), pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 106/68 mm Hg. Pelvic examination shows white cervical mucus and a firm, irregularly-shaped uterus consistent in size with a 5-week gestation. A spot urine pregnancy test is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?. Answer Choices: A. Laparoscopy B. Pelvic MRI C. Pelvic ultrasound D. Repeat β-HCG test
label: Pelvic ultrasound
rationale: The most appropriate next step is a [LABEL] to evaluate the abnormal uterine morphology and assess for potential causes of the pelvic mass and menorrhagia.

question: A 4-month-old boy is brought to the physician for a well-child examination. He was born at 39 weeks gestation via spontaneous vaginal delivery and is exclusively breastfed. He weighed 3,400 g (7 lb 8 oz) at birth. At the physician's office, he appears well. His pulse is 146/min, the respirations are 39/min, and the blood pressure is 78/44 mm Hg. Which of the following developmental milestones has this patient most likely met?. Answer Choices: A. Grasps small objects between thumb and finger B. Intentionally rolls over C. Sits with support of pelvis D. Transfers objects from hand to hand
label: Intentionally rolls over
rationale: Based on the typical developmental milestones for a 4-month-old infant, it is reasonable to conclude that the patient has most likely met the developmental milestone of intentionally rolling over. This aligns with the expected motor skills development at this age.

question: Which statement best describes the cranial fossa?. Answer Choices: A. The middle cranial fossa contains the cribriform plate. B. The anterior cranial fossa contains the pituitary gland. C. The middle cranial fossa is floored by the sphenoid and temporal bones. D. The internal acoustic meatus lies in the middle cranial fossa.
label: The middle cranial fossa is floored by the sphenoid and temporal bones
rationale: The middle cranial fossa contains the pituitary gland, and the internal acoustic meatus lies in the posterior cranial fossa. This conclusion aligns with the anatomical relationships and structures of the cranial fossae.

question: Bronchogenic carcinoma commonly metastasise to which endocrine organ -. Answer Choices: A. Ovaries B. Testes C. Thyroid D. Adrenals
label: Adrenals
rationale: Based on the reasoning process, the adrenal gland is indeed a common site for metastasis of bronchogenic carcinoma. This conclusion aligns with clinical observations and the understanding of metastatic pathways in lung cancer.

question: A 58-year-old man comes to the physician for a 3-month history of progressive shortness of breath on exertion and tiredness throughout the day. His wife reports that he snores at night and that he sometimes chokes in his sleep. He has a history of hypertension treated with enalapril. His blood pressure is 149/96 mmHg. There is jugular venous distention and 2+ lower extremity edema bilaterally. The lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. An ECG shows right axis deviation. Hypertensive nephropathy "
label: Chronic hypoxia
rationale: The most likely underlying cause of this patient’s condition is [LABEL] due to obstructive sleep apnea, which has led to pulmonary hypertension and chronic pulmonary heart disease (cor pulmonale).

question: A 3-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her 30-year-old mother, who reports that her daughter has been passing multiple foul-smelling, bulky stools with flatulence every day for the last 6 months. The girl was born in Guatemala, and soon after her birth, her parents moved to the United States so that they could access better healthcare. During pregnancy, the mother had little prenatal care, but labor and delivery were uneventful. However, the newborn had significant abdominal distention immediately at birth that increased when she ate or yawned. She failed to pass stool in the first 24 hours of life and had greenish-black vomitus. The parents report similar symptoms in other family members. After diagnosis, the girl underwent a procedure that alleviated her symptoms; however, there was no remission. Her abdominal X-ray (see the first image) and barium contrast enema (second image) from when she was born is shown. Her blood pressure is 100/68 mm Hg, heart rate is 96/min, respiratory rate is 19/min, and temperature is 36.7°C (98.0°F). The girl is in the 10th percentile for height and weight. On physical exam, she has periumbilical and midepigastric tenderness to palpation without rebound tenderness or guarding. There is a slight genu varum deformity and bony tenderness noted in her legs. She has foul-smelling flatulation 2–3 times during the visit. Her rectosphincteric reflex is intact. She has decreased fecal elastase and a negative D-xylose test. Which of the following is the most appropriate long-term treatment for her condition?. Answer Choices: A. Enzyme-replacement therapy B. Rectal suction biopsy and surgical correction (Hirschsprung) C. Duodenal atresia repair D. Cholecalciferol
label: Enzyme-replacement therapy
rationale: The most appropriate long-term treatment for this patient is [LABEL] with pancrelipase. This addresses the underlying exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, which is the likely cause of her malabsorption syndrome and associated complications such as rickets.

question: The most common cause of Macular edema is. Answer Choices: A. Secondary glaucoma B. Cataract surgery C. Retinitis pigmentosa D. Uveitis
label: Cataract surgery
rationale: The most common cause of macular retinal edema is [LABEL], specifically as a postoperative complication (e.g., cystoid macular edema). This conclusion is based on the high prevalence of [LABEL] and the well-documented association between the procedure and macular retinal edema.

question: 3-year-old child comes in ER with Hv'o vomiting, loose watery motion for 3 days, on examination, the child was drowsy, sunken eye. question: A 3-year-old male is brought by his mother to the pediatrician because she is concerned about a lump in his neck. rationale: Histologic examination of the neck lesion would reveal [LABEL], consistent with an infected thyroglossal duct cyst.

question: Mutations that cause achondroplasia exert an effect which can be classified as:. Answer Choices: A. dominant negative. B. gain-of-function. C. haploinsufficiency. D. loss-of-function.

question: A 45-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after vomiting blood. However, FBP1 does not exhibit fructose-2,6-bisphosphate 6-phosphatase activity. The given answer is incorrect in attributing this activity to FBP1.

question: A 3-year-old girl is brought to the physician for a well-child visit. Her father is concerned about the color and strength of her teeth. question: Over the course of a year, 5 children with identical congenital heart defects were referred to a pediatric cardiac surgeon for evaluation. All 5 children had stable vital signs and were on appropriate medication. Upon review of medical records, all of them had a loud holosystolic murmur over the third intercostal space at the left sternal border. The surgeon ordered echocardiograms for all 5 children and recommended surgical closure of the defect in one of them. Which of the following patients required surgical repair of their defect?. Answer Choices: A. A 4-month-old male infant with a 12-mm muscular defect, without heart failure, pulmonary hypertension or growth failure B. A 2-year-old boy with a 2-mm supracristal defect, without heart failure, pulmonary hypertension, or growth failure C. A 5-year-old girl with a 2-mm membranous defect, no heart failure, a Qp:Qs ratio less than 2:1, and no growth failure D. A 7-year-old boy with an 11-mm muscular defect and severe pulmonary vascular disease non-responsive to pulmonary vasodilators
label: A 2-year-old boy with a 2-mm supracristal defect, without heart failure, pulmonary hypertension, or growth failure
rationale: The 2-year-old boy with a 2-mm supracristal VSD likely required surgical repair due to the risk of aortic valve prolapse and regurgitation, even in the absence of heart failure, pulmonary hypertension, or growth failure. This aligns with standard indications for surgical closure of supracristal VSDs.

question: Diisopropylfluorophosphate (DFP) binds to the active site of acetylcholinesterase (ACE) in the synapses of neurons. When DFP binds to ACE, the ACE enzyme is rendered permanently inactive. Noncompetitive inhibition C. Irreversible inhibition D. Partially competitive inhibition

rationale: The interaction between Isoflurophate and ACHE is best characterized as [LABEL], demonstrating enzyme inhibitor activity.

question: Fluorescein is an artificial dark orange colored organic molecule used in the diagnosis of corneal ulcers and herpetic corneal infections. rationale: Fluorescein is best described as a [LABEL], based on its inability to stimulate an immune response independently and its requirement for conjugation to a larger molecule to elicit antibody production.

question: A 65-year-old man presents to his primary care provider after noticing increasing fatigue over the past several weeks. He now becomes short of breath after going up 1 flight of stairs. He was previously healthy and has not seen a doctor for several years. He denies any fever or changes to his bowel movements. label: Superior mesenteric vein
rationale: The [LABEL] is the structure most at risk for involvement in this patient’s disease. This conclusion is based on the venous drainage of the cecum and the metastatic spread of colorectal cancer to the liver.

question: A 62-year-old man comes to the physician because of easy bruising and recurrent nosebleeds over the past 4 months. rationale: The patient's presentation, laboratory findings, and bone marrow biopsy results are consistent with hairy cell leukemia (HCL). This diagnosis is supported by the presence of [LABEL], indicating acid phosphatase activity.

question: A 21-year-old male presents to the ED with a stab wound to the right neck. The patient is alert and responsive, and vital signs are stable. Which of the following neurologic findings would most likely support the diagnosis of right-sided spinal cord hemisection?. Answer Choices: A. Right-sided tactile, vibration, and proprioception loss; left-sided pain and temperature sensation loss; right-sided paresis B. Left-sided tactile, vibration, and proprioception loss; right-sided pain and temperature sensation loss; left-sided paresis C. Right-sided tactile, vibration, and proprioception loss; right-sided pain and temperature sensation loss; right-sided paresis D. This aligns with the expected presentation of Brown-Séquard syndrome following a right-sided neck injury.

question: After falling on the pavement, a 72-year-old woman is found to have a fracture of the radius and ulna (Colles' fracture). What is true of this fracture?. Answer Choices: A. The fall occurs on the dorsum of the wrist. B. Open reduction is most commonly indicated. C. Younger men are generally affected. D. The distal radial metaphysis is displaced dorsally.
label: The distal radial metaphysis is displaced dorsally
rationale: In a Colles' fracture, the distal metaphysis of the radius is displaced dorsally. This type of fracture is common in older women due to osteoporosis, and the styloid process of the ulna is often fractured as a secondary injury.

question: A 56-year-old man with chronic kidney disease and type 2 diabetes mellitus is brought to the emergency department by his neighbor because of impaired consciousness and difficulty speaking for 1 hour. Try to contact the father for consent D. Consult ethics committee

rationale: The physician should [LABEL] regarding the patient's care.

question: Carriers for Herpes simplex virus is: March 2009. Answer Choices: A. Man B. Monkey C. Both D. None

rationale: Asymptomatic carriers are indeed an important source of infection for herpes simplex virus, particularly in the context of genital herpes. They can transmit the virus through saliva, skin contact, and potentially respiratory secretions, even when they do not exhibit symptoms. This makes them a significant factor in the spread of the virus, especially in intimate or close-contact situations.

question: A 33-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-week history of fatigue and worsening shortness of breath on exertion. There is no family history of serious illness. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 115 kg (254 lb); BMI is 44 kg/m2. Her pulse is 83/min and blood pressure is 125/85 mm Hg. Cardiac examination shows a loud pulmonary component of the S2. Hyperplasia of pulmonary vascular walls B. Blockade of the right bundle branch C. Fibrosis of pulmonary interstitium D. Calcification of the pulmonary valve

rationale: The most likely underlying cause of this patient’s shortness of breath is hyperplasia of the pulmonary vascular walls, secondary to diethylpropion-induced pulmonary arterial hypertension.

question: A 23-year-old male presents with complaints of polydipsia and frequent, large-volume urination. Laboratory testing does not demonstrate any evidence of diabetes; however, a reduced urine osmolality of 120 mOsm/L is measured. question: A 63-year-old woman presents to her primary care doctor with increased urinary frequency. She has noticed that over the past 6 months, she has had to urinate more often than usual. Several times per day, she develops a rapid-onset need to urinate and has occasionally been unable to reach the restroom. She has tried pelvic floor exercises, decreasing her caffeine consumption, and has intentionally lost 20 pounds in an effort to alleviate her symptoms. She denies urinary hesitancy or hematuria. rationale: The most appropriate intervention for this patient with symptoms of overactive bladder syndrome (spastic/hyperactive bladder) is [LABEL], an anticholinergic medication that addresses detrusor overactivity.

question: Avascular necrosis of bone is LEAST likely to be associated with?. Answer Choices: A. Osgood -Schlatter disease B. Legg-Perthes disease C. Long-term use of corticosteroids D. Sickle-cell disease
label: Osgood -Schlatter disease
rationale: Osgood-Schlatter disease is least likely to be associated with avascular necrosis because it is a localized inflammatory condition caused by mechanical stress, not ischemia or vascular compromise.

question: A 55-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fluctuating mood for the past 2 years. He feels great and full of energy for some months when he is very creative with tons of ideas just racing through his mind. He is noted to be very talkative and distracted by his different ideas. The accompanying feeling of hopelessness from these cycling “ups” and “downs” have him eating and sleeping more during the “downs.” He does not remember a period within the last 2 years where he felt “normal.” What is the most likely diagnosis?. Answer Choices: A. Cyclothymic disorder B. Bipolar II disorder C. Persistent depressive disorder D. Dysthymic disorder

question: `Coronory steal phenomenon' is caused by ?. Answer Choices: A. Dipyridamole B. Diltiazem C. Propanolol D. Verapamil

However, its ability to inhibit platelet aggregation and prevent thrombosis makes it useful for the prophylaxis of coronary thrombosis, intracranial thrombosis, and stroke disorder, particularly in post-MI and post-stroke patients.

question: Right anterior oblique view of chest X–ray true is/are – a) Cassette near right shoulderb) Cassette near left shoulderc) Arch of Aorta best seend) Left atrial enlargement can be diagnosede) Mitral & tricuspid valves better seen. Answer Choices: A. ad B. c C. ac D. ab

question: Blisters are seen in. Answer Choices: A. 1st degree burn B. Superficial 2nd degree burn C. Deep 2nd degree burn D. 3rd degree burn

question: A 17-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with abdominal and pelvic pain. She states it started 3 days ago and it has been getting gradually worse. question: You are a resident on a pediatric service entering orders late at night. question: A 12-year-old African American boy is brought to the emergency room due to a severely painful penile erection for the past 5 hours.
