A complete blood count and a cavernous blood gas analysis showed the following: Hemoglobin (Hb) 11.5 g/dL; 14.5 g/dL (-2SD 13.0 g/dL) for boys 12–18 years of age Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) 95 fL; 80–96 fL Platelet count 250,000/mm3 pO2 38 mm Hg pCO2 65 mm Hg pH 7.25 sO2 % 60% HCO3- 10 mEq/L A peripheral blood smear reveals RBCs with Howell-Jolly bodies. Rapid detumescence is achieved after aspiration of blood and administration of an adrenergic agonist and analgesia. Which of the following etiologies should be considered in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Sickle cell disease (SCD) B. Glucose-6 phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency C. Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) D. Hereditary spherocytosis
label: Sickle cell disease (SCD)
rationale: Based on the reasoning paths explored, sickle cell disease should be considered as the etiology in this patient. The combination of priapism and the presence of Howell-Jolly bodies strongly suggests sickle cell disease, given the known complications and hematological findings associated with this condition.

question: A 70-year-old male with a 10-year history of COPD visits his pulmonologist for a checkup. Physical examination reveals cyanosis, digital clubbing, and bilateral lung wheezes are heard upon auscultation. The patient has a cough productive of thick yellow sputum. Which of the following findings is most likely present in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Decreased arterial carbon dioxide content B. Increased pulmonary arterial resistance C. Increased pH of the arterial blood D. Increased cerebral vascular resistance
label: Increased pulmonary arterial resistance
rationale: The most likely finding in this patient is increased pulmonary vascular resistance, secondary to chronic hypoxia and pulmonary vasoconstriction caused by advanced COPD.

question: A student is experimenting with the effects of nitric oxide in the body. He used a variety of amino acid isolates and measured the resulting nitric oxide levels and the physiological effects on the body. The amino acids function as substrates for nitric oxide synthase. After supplement administration, blood vessels dilated, and the systemic blood pressure decreased. Which of the following amino acids was used in this study?. Answer Choices: A. Histidine B. Arginine C. Leucine D. Tyrosine
label: Arginine
rationale: The amino acid used in this study is [LABEL], which serves as a substrate for nitric oxide synthase (specifically NOS2) activity, leading to nitric oxide production and its associated effects on vasodilation, blood pressure, and the inflammatory response.

question: Tamoxifene -. Answer Choices: A. SSRI B. SERM C. SNRI D. DNRI
label: SERM
rationale: Tamoxifen, a selective estrogen receptor modulator, is primarily used for the treatment of estrogen-receptor positive breast cancer. It offers additional benefits such as increased bone mineral density and improved lipid profile (decreased LDL cholesterol). However, it carries a significant risk of deep venous thrombosis due to its pro-thrombotic effects.

question: Which of the following is the most severe form of Diptheria. Answer Choices: A. Nasal B. Cutaneous C. Nasopharynx D. Laryngopharynx
label: Laryngopharynx
rationale: The most severe form of diphtheria is laryngeal diphtheria, which directly affects the larynx and can cause life-threatening airway obstruction. This conclusion aligns with the understanding of the disease's progression and its clinical manifestations.

question: A 12-year-old boy presents to the emergency room with difficulty breathing after several days of severe sore throat. Further history reveals that his family immigrated recently from Eastern Europe and he has never previously seen a doctor. Physical exam shows cervical lymphadenopathy with extensive neck edema as well as the finding shown in the image provided. You suspect a bacteria that causes the disease by producing an AB type exotoxin. Which of the following is the proper medium to culture the most likely cause of this infection?. Answer Choices: A. Bordet-Genou Agar B. Eaton's Agar C. Tellurite Agar D. Thayer-Martin Agar
label: Tellurite Agar
rationale: The proper medium for the detection of the bacterium causing this infection (*Corynebacterium diphtheriae*) is [LABEL].

question: Which category of ICD is associated with schizophrenia. Answer Choices: A. F0 B. F1 C. F2 D. F3
label: F2
rationale: Schizophrenia, schizotypal personality disorder, and delusional disorders are categorized under F20-F29 in the ICD classification system for mental disorders.

question: A 39-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department in a semi-unconscious state by her neighbor who saw her lose consciousness. There was no apparent injury on the primary survey. She is not currently taking any medications. She has had loose stools for the past 3 days and a decreased frequency of urination. No further history could be obtained. The vital signs include: blood pressure 94/62 mm Hg, temperature 36.7°C (98.0°F), pulse 105/min, and respiratory rate 10/min. The skin appears dry. Routine basic metabolic panel, urine analysis, urine osmolality, and urine electrolytes are pending. Which of the following lab abnormalities would be expected in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Urine osmolality < 350 mOsm/kg B. Urine Na+ > 40 mEq/L C. Serum blood urea nitrogen/creatinine (BUN/Cr) > 20 D. Serum creatinine < 1 mg/dL
label: Serum blood urea nitrogen/creatinine (BUN/Cr) > 20
rationale: The expected lab abnormality in this patient is a serum BUN/Cr ratio greater than 20, consistent with prerenal azotemia caused by dehydration and hypovolemia. This reflects the kidney’s response to osmotic stress and impaired renal perfusion.

question: A 52-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband because of weakness, abdominal pain, and a productive cough for 4 days. She also reports increased urination for the past 2 days. This morning, she had nausea and five episodes of vomiting. She has type 1 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Current medications include insulin and lisinopril. She admits to have forgotten to take her medication in the last few days. Her temperature is 38.4°C (101.1°F), pulse is 134/min, respirations 31/min, and blood pressure is 95/61 mm Hg. Examination shows dry mucous membranes and decreased skin turgor. Abdominal examination shows diffuse tenderness with no guarding or rebound. Bowel sounds are normal. Laboratory studies show: Serum Na+ 139 mEq/L K+ 5.3 mEq/L Cl- 106 mEq/L Glucose 420 mg/dL Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL Urine Blood negative Glucose 4+ Ketones 3+ Arterial blood gas analysis on room air shows: pH 7.12 pCO2 17 mm Hg pO2 86 mm Hg HCO3- 12 mEq/L Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's increased potassium?". Answer Choices: A. Increased renal potassium absorption B. Muscle cell breakdown C. Extracellular potassium shift D. Repeated vomiting
label: Extracellular potassium shift
rationale: The most likely underlying cause of this patient's increased potassium is an [LABEL] due to diabetic ketoacidosis. The lack of insulin administration has led to DKA, causing metabolic acidosis and an extracellular shift of potassium ions.

question: In a previous experiment infecting hepatocytes, it was shown that viable HDV virions were only produced in the presence of a co-infection with HBV. To better understand which HBV particle was necessary for the production of viable HDV virions, the scientist encoded in separate plasmids the various antigens/proteins of HBV and co-infected the hepatocytes with HDV. In which of the experiments would viable HDV virions be produced in conjunction with the appropriate HBV antigen/protein?. Answer Choices: A. HBsAg B. HBcAg C. HBV RNA polymerase D. HBeAg
label: HBsAg
rationale: Viable HDV virions would be produced in conjunction with the appropriate HBV antigen/protein, specifically [LABEL]. This is because [LABEL] provides the necessary envelope for HDV virion assembly and release. Other HBV proteins or host cell factors are not directly required for this process.

question: In your peripheral tissues and lungs, carbonic anhydrase works to control the equilibrium between carbon dioxide and carbonic acid in order to maintain proper blood pH. Through which mechanism does carbonic anhydrase exert its influence on reaction kinetics?. Answer Choices: A. Lowers the activation energy B. Changes the delta G of the reaction C. Raises the activation energy D. Lowers the free energy of products
label: Lowers the activation energy
rationale: Carbonic anhydrase exerts its influence on reaction kinetics through its carbonate dehydratase activity, lowering the activation energy of the CO₂ hydration/dehydration reaction. This activity is central to its role in CO₂ homeostasis and pH regulation. However, it does not act on a protein substrate; instead, it catalyzes reactions involving small molecules (CO₂ and H₂O). Therefore, the given answer's mention of "acting on a protein" is incorrect.

question: Hydatidiform - mole, characterized histologically by. Answer Choices: A. Hyaline membrane degeneration B. Hydropic degeneration of the villous stroma C. Non proliferation of cytotrophoblasts D. Non proliferation of syncytiotrophoblasts
label: Hydropic degeneration of the villous stroma
rationale: Hydatidiform mole is histologically characterized by [LABEL], absence of blood vessels, and trophoblastic proliferation. These features are diagnostic and also contribute to its potential for malignant transformation into gestational trophoblastic neoplasms or trophoblastic tumors.

question: A 22-year-old man comes to the physician for a follow-up evaluation for chronic lower back pain. He has back stiffness that lasts all morning and slowly improves throughout the day. He has tried multiple over-the-counter medications, including ibuprofen, without any improvement in his symptoms. Physical examination shows tenderness over the iliac crest bilaterally and limited range of motion of the lumbar spine with forward flexion. The results of HLA-B27 testing are positive. An x-ray of the lumbar spine shows fusion of the lumbar vertebrae and sacroiliac joints. The physician plans to prescribe a new medication but first orders a tuberculin skin test to assess for the risk of latent tuberculosis reactivation. Inhibition of which of the following is the most likely primary mechanism of action of this drug?. Answer Choices: A. Inosine monophosphate dehydrogenase B. TNF-α C. NF-κB D. mTOR kinase
label: TNF-α
rationale: The drug prescribed most likely works by inhibiting tumor necrosis factor binding. This aligns with the use of TNF inhibitors in the treatment of ankylosing spondylitis.

question: Best advantage of doing transcranial Doppler ultrasound?. Answer Choices: A. Detect AV malformation B. Detect emboli C. Detect vasopasm D. Detect brain blood vessels stenosis
label: Detect vasopasm
rationale: The best advantage of transcranial Doppler ultrasound is its ability to detect vasospasm, especially in cases of subarachnoid hemorrhage, where vasoconstriction leads to increased flow velocity in the middle cerebral artery. This non-invasive technique allows for real-time monitoring, making it a valuable tool in managing patients with SAH.

question: A person's X-ray chest showing homogenous opacity on right side with shifting of mediastinum on opposite side. Most probable diagnosis is/are:. Answer Choices: A. Collapse B. Pleural effusion C. Pneumothorax D. Consolidation
label: Pleural effusion
rationale: The most probable diagnosis is [LABEL], a pleural disease characterized by homogenous opacity, mediastinal shift, and a concave upper border (Ellis's curve).

question: Mode of excretion of cyclophosphamide is -. Answer Choices: A. Lung B. Liver C. Kidney D. Skin
label: Kidney
rationale: The mode of excretion of cyclophosphamide is primarily through the [LABEL].

question: A patient of rectal carcinoma found to have tumor invading through the muscularis propria into the perirectal tissues, and two enlarged lymph nodes. How will you manage the patient?. Answer Choices: A. Surgery only B. Surgery + Adjuvant chemotherapy C. Preoperative chemoradiation followed by surgical resection D. Palliative chemoradiation
label: Preoperative chemoradiation followed by surgical resection
rationale: Based on the reasoning process, the management of a patient with rectal carcinoma that has invaded through the muscularis propria into the perirectal tissues and has enlarged lymph nodes likely involves [LABEL]. This approach aligns with standard treatment protocols for locally advanced rectal cancer, aiming to improve surgical outcomes and reduce recurrence risk.

question: In an elective lap cholecystectomy with no gross spillage, antibiotics required are. Answer Choices: A. Single dose of Preoperative IV antibiotic followed by a 5 day course of oral antibiotics B. Post operative IV antibiotics for 1 day followed by a 5 day course of oral antibiotics C. Single dose of Preoperative IV antibiotic only D. 5 day course of oral antibiotics only
label: Single dose of Preoperative IV antibiotic only
rationale: A single dose of preoperative IV antibiotic is sufficient for prophylaxis against skin infection in elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy, provided there is no gross spillage or other complicating factors. This aligns with standard surgical guidelines and minimizes unnecessary antibiotic use.

question: Blood clots are responsible for repeatedly blocking a catheter. What should you do?. Answer Choices: A. Call for help. B. Inform medical staff. C. Inform the patient. D. Remove the catheter and recatheterize.
label: Inform medical staff
rationale: You should [LABEL] to address the issue. This ensures timely intervention to resolve the blockage and prevent further complications.

question: A 21 year old lady with a history of hypersensitivity to neostigmine is posted for an elective caesarean section under general anesthesia. The best muscle relaxant of choice in this patient should be:. Answer Choices: A. Pancuronium B. Atracurium C. Rocuronium D. Vecuronium
label: Atracurium
rationale: [LABEL] is the most appropriate muscle relaxant for this patient due to its unique feature of spontaneous non-enzymatic degradation (Hoffmann elimination), which eliminates the need for reversal with neostigmine.

question: A 40 year old female patient complains of excessive bleeding and drowsiness. Patient gives a history of road traffic accident 5 hours ago and had a lacerated wound on lower back region. General physical examination reveals- Blood pressure-80/60mmHg. Juglar venous pressure- low. Pulsus paradoxus- present. Cardiac output- Increased. The patient is in which type of shock?. Answer Choices: A. Neurogenic B. Obstructive C. Distributive D. Hypovolemic
label: Distributive
rationale: The patient is most likely in [LABEL] shock, specifically toxic shock syndrome, due to the lacerated wound and the associated clinical findings of vasodilation, increased cardiac output, and hypovolemia.

question: A 44-year-old woman comes to the physician for the evaluation of a 1-month history of fatigue and difficulty swallowing. During this period, she has also had dry skin, thinning hair, and rounding of her face. She has type 1 diabetes mellitus and rheumatoid arthritis. Her father had a thyroidectomy for papillary thyroid cancer. The patient had smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 20 years but quit 3 years ago. She drinks 2–3 glasses of wine daily. Her current medications include insulin, omeprazole, and daily ibuprofen. She appears well. Her temperature is 36.3°C (97.3°F), pulse is 62/min, and blood pressure is 102/76 mm Hg. Examination of the neck shows a painless, diffusely enlarged thyroid gland. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Further evaluation is most likely to show which of the following?. Answer Choices: A. Increased uptake on radioactive iodine scan in discrete 1-cm area B. Diffusely increased uptake on a radioactive iodine scan C. Positive immunohistochemical stain for calcitonin on thyroid biopsy D. Positive thyroid peroxidase antibodies and thyroglobulin antibodies in serum
label: Positive thyroid peroxidase antibodies and thyroglobulin antibodies in serum
rationale: Further evaluation is most likely to show [LABEL], indicating an autoimmune abnormality of the thyroid gland, specifically Hashimoto's thyroiditis.

question: A 26-day-old newborn is brought to the physician because of poor feeding and lethargy for 2 weeks. During this period, he has had a raspy cry. The child was delivered at term at home and has not yet been evaluated by a physician. He is at the 90th percentile for head circumference, 50th percentile for length, and 60th percentile for weight. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows scleral icterus and an enlarged tongue. The abdomen is distended and there is a reducible, soft protruding mass at the umbilicus. Muscle tone is decreased in all extremities. Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings?. Answer Choices: A. Thyroid dysgenesis B. Acid maltase deficiency C. Trisomy 21 D. Chromosome 11p alteration
label: Thyroid dysgenesis
rationale: The most likely cause of these findings is [LABEL], leading to congenital hypothyroidism.

question: A 26-year-old woman with HIV infection comes to the office because of a 4-day history of pain and redness of her left lower leg. She says the symptoms began after she tripped over a tree branch in her yard and scraped her left leg. Current medications include antiretroviral therapy and ibuprofen as needed. Vital signs are temperature 38.3°C (100.9°F), pulse 86/min, respirations 14/min, and blood pressure 138/70 mm Hg. There is a 5×8-cm area on the anterior surface of her left lower extremity that is swollen, erythematous, and tender. She previously has developed a rash after taking erythromycin, and she has had an anaphylactic response to penicillin. Which of the following antibiotic therapies is most appropriate for this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Amoxicillin B. Ciprofloxacin C. Clarithromycin D. Clindamycin
label: Clindamycin
rationale: [LABEL] is the most appropriate antibiotic therapy for this patient. It is effective against the likely pathogens causing cellulitis and is safe to use given her history of penicillin anaphylaxis and erythromycin hypersensitivity.

question: A healthy 4-year-old girl is brought for a well-child examination. A grade 2/6 systolic ejection murmur is heard along the upper left sternal border. S2 is widely split and does not vary with respiration. A soft mid-diastolic murmur is heard along the lower left sternal border. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?. Answer Choices: A. Aortic stenosis B. Atrial septal defect C. Coarctation of the aorta D. Mitral valve prolapse
label: Atrial septal defect
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is [LABEL] (ASD). The findings of a systolic ejection murmur, widely split S2 that does not vary with respiration, and a mid-diastolic murmur are classic for this condition.

question: A 70-year-old man presents with fever, headache, and vomiting. He says that symptoms onset acutely 2 days ago and have not improved. He also reports associated weakness and chills. Past medical history is significant for occasional heartburn. His temperature is 39.4°C (103.0°F), the pulse rate is 124/min, the blood pressure is 130/84 mm Hg, and the respiratory rate is 22/min. On physical examination, there is significant nuchal rigidity. No signs of raised intracranial pressure are present. A lumbar puncture is performed and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis shows lymphocyte-dominant pleocytosis with increased CSF protein levels. Bacteriological culture of the CSF reveals the growth of Listeria monocytogenes. Which of the following antibiotics is the best choice for the treatment of this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Ampicillin B. Ceftriaxone C. Chloramphenicol D. Vancomycin
label: Ampicillin
rationale: [LABEL] is the best choice of antibiotic for the treatment of *Listeria monocytogenes* meningitis in this patient.

question: All of the following are true about Asthma, Except:. Answer Choices: A. Charcol Leydin crystals may be seen in sputum B. Reversible Airflow obstruction is a characteristic feature C. Large airways are involved D. Small airways are not involved
label: Small airways are not involved
rationale: Asthma involves both large and small airways but not the alveoli of the lung. Reversible airflow obstruction is a characteristic feature, though irreversible obstruction may occur in chronic cases. Salbutamol is effective for intermittent asthma but insufficient for persistent symptoms.

question: A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department for sudden pain in his foot. The patient states that when he woke up, he experienced severe pain in his right great toe. The patient’s wife immediately brought him to the emergency department. The patient has a past medical history of diabetes mellitus, obesity, and hypertension and is currently taking insulin, metformin, lisinopril, and ibuprofen. The patient is a current smoker and smokes 2 packs per day. He also drinks 3 glasses of whiskey every night. The patient is started on IV fluids and corticosteroids. His blood pressure, taken at the end of this visit, is 175/95 mmHg. As the patient’s symptoms improve, he asks how he can avoid having these symptoms again in the future. Which of the following is the best initial intervention in preventing a future episode of this patient’s condition?. Answer Choices: A. Allopurinol B. Hydrochlorothiazide C. Lifestyle measures D. Probenecid
label: Lifestyle measures
rationale: [LABEL], such as weight loss, reducing alcohol intake, smoking cessation, and dietary changes, are the best initial intervention to prevent future episodes of this patient’s condition (gout). These measures address the modifiable risk factors contributing to hyperuricemia and gout attacks.

question: A 65-year-old woman presents with severe abdominal pain and bloody diarrhea. Past medical history is significant for a myocardial infarction 6 months ago. The patient reports a 25-pack-year smoking history and consumes 80 ounces of alcohol per week. Physical examination shows a diffusely tender abdomen with the absence of bowel sounds. Plain abdominal radiography is negative for free air under the diaphragm. Laboratory findings show a serum amylase of 115 U/L, serum lipase 95 U/L. Her clinical condition deteriorates rapidly, and she dies. Which of the following would most likely be the finding on autopsy in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Small bowel obstruction B. Small bowel ischemia C. Ulcerative colitis D. Acute pancreatitis
label: Small bowel ischemia
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is acute intestinal ischemia due to ischemic bowel disease. This is supported by the patient's vascular risk factors (myocardial infarction, smoking, ethanol use), symptoms (severe abdominal pain, bloody diarrhea, lack of bowel sounds), and the absence of findings suggestive of other conditions like obstruction or pancreatitis.

question: After learning in a lecture that cesarean section rates vary from < 0.5% to over 30% across countries, a medical student wants to investigate if national cesarean section rates correlate with national maternal mortality rates worldwide. For his investigation, the student obtains population data from an international registry that contains tabulated cesarean section rates and maternal mortality rates from the last 10 years for a total of 119 countries. Which of the following best describes this study design?. Answer Choices: A. Case series B. Meta-analysis C. Ecological study D. Prospective cohort study "
label: Ecological study
rationale: The study design described is an [LABEL].

question: Drugs causing macular toxicity when given intravitreally-. Answer Choices: A. Gentamycin B. Vancomycin C. Dexamethasone D. Ceftazidime
label: Gentamycin
rationale: Gentamicin is a drug that can cause macular toxicity when given intravitreally. This toxicity is primarily due to the drug's cytotoxic effects and the gravity-induced settling of the drug at the macula, leading to higher local concentrations.

question: A 62-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus is brought to the emergency room because of a 3-day history of fever and shaking chills. Her temperature is 39.4°C (103°F). Examination of the back shows right costovertebral angle tenderness. Analysis of the urine shows WBCs, WBC casts, and gram-negative rods. Ultrasound examination of the kidneys shows no signs of obstruction. Biopsy of the patient's kidney is most likely to show which of the following?. Answer Choices: A. Polygonal clear cells filled with lipids and carbohydrates B. Polymorphonuclear leukocytes in tubules C. Cystic dilation of the renal medulla D. Tubular eosinophilic casts "
label: Polymorphonuclear leukocytes in tubules
rationale: The most likely histological finding in this patient is polymorphonuclear leukocytes in the renal tubules, including intratubular leukocyte casts and an increased urine neutrophil count. This is consistent with the diagnosis of acute pyelonephritis.

question: Which classification is used to divide the liver into segments?. Answer Choices: A. Couinaud B. Muhe C. Starzl D. Anatomical
label: Couinaud
rationale: [LABEL]'s classification is used to divide the liver into 8 segments based on its vascular and biliary anatomy.

question: A 62-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after his wife found him unresponsive 1 hour ago. He had fallen from a flight of stairs the previous evening. Four years ago, he underwent a mitral valve replacement. He has hypertension and coronary artery disease. Current medications include aspirin, warfarin, enalapril, metoprolol, and atorvastatin. On arrival, he is unconscious. His temperature is 37.3°C (99.1°F), pulse is 59/min, respirations are 7/min and irregular, and blood pressure is 200/102 mm Hg. The right pupil is 5 mm and fixed. The left pupil is 4 mm and reactive to light. There is extension of the extremities to painful stimuli. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination shows a systolic click. The abdomen is soft and nontender. He is intubated and mechanically ventilated. A mannitol infusion is begun. A noncontrast CT scan of the brain shows a 6-cm subdural hematoma on the right side with an 18-mm midline shift. Which of the following is the most likely early sequela of this patient's current condition?. Answer Choices: A. Right eye esotropia and elevation B. Bilateral lower limb paralysis C. Left-side facial nerve palsy D. Right-sided hemiplegia
label: Right-sided hemiplegia
rationale: The most likely early sequela of this patient's condition is [LABEL].

question: Which of the following is aberrant epiphysis. Answer Choices: A. Coracoid process B. Greater tubercle of humerus C. Base of 1" metacarpal D. Base of 2nd metacarpal
label: Base of 2nd metacarpal
rationale: Based on the reasoning process, the base of the second metacarpal can indeed be identified as an aberrant epiphysis. This conclusion aligns with the understanding of typical and atypical epiphyseal locations in the metacarpals.

question: A grant reviewer at the National Institutes of Health is determining which of two studies investigating the effects of gastric bypass surgery on fasting blood sugar to fund. Study A is spearheaded by a world renowned surgeon, is a multi-center study planning to enroll 50 patients at each of 5 different sites, and is single-blinded. Study B plans to enroll 300 patients from a single site and will be double-blinded by virtue of a sham surgery for the control group. The studies both plan to use a t-test, and they both report identical expected treatment effect sizes and variance. If the reviewer were interested only in which trial has the higher power, which proposal should he fund?. Answer Choices: A. Study A, because it has a superior surgeon B. Study A, because it is a multi-center trial C. Study B, because it has a larger sample size D. Both studies have the same power
label: Study B, because it has a larger sample size
rationale: Study B has several advantages, including a larger sample size and a double-blinded design, which reduce bias and increase statistical power. However, the ethical concerns surrounding the use of sham surgery must be carefully considered. If the ethical review deems the sham surgery acceptable, Study B is the stronger choice due to its methodological rigor and higher power. If ethical concerns outweigh the benefits, Study A may be the more appropriate option despite its limitations.

question: A patient having glaucoma develops blepharoconjunctivitis after instilling some anti-glaucoma drug. Which of the following drug can be responsible for it:. Answer Choices: A. Timolol B. Latanosprost C. Dipiverine D. Pilocarpine
label: Timolol
rationale: [LABEL], a beta-blocker used in the treatment of glaucoma, can cause allergic blepharoconjunctivitis as a side effect. This aligns with the known side effect profile of [LABEL] when used as an anti-glaucoma medication.

question: A 6-month-old infant girl is brought by her parents to the emergency room due to abnormal jerky movements of the upper and lower limbs for the past month. When questioned about her birth history, the parents mention that a prenatal scan of the fetal heart revealed that the patient had a mass in the left ventricle, which led to the diagnosis of a neurocutaneous disorder in the child. Which of the following findings is a characteristic cutaneous finding associated with this young patient’s disorder?. Answer Choices: A. Ash-leaf spots B. Cafe-au-lait spots C. Cavernous hemangioma of the skin D. Port-wine stain
label: Ash-leaf spots
rationale: Based on the reasoning above, the infant's symptoms and findings are consistent with tuberous sclerosis. [LABEL] are indeed a characteristic cutaneous finding associated with this disorder.

question: Mechanism of action of theophylline in bronchial asthma is:. Answer Choices: A. Phosphodiesterase 4 inhibition B. Beta2 agonism C. Anticholinergic action D. Inhibition of mucociliary clearance
label: Phosphodiesterase 4 inhibition
rationale: The primary mechanism of action of theophylline in bronchial asthma is likely the inhibition of phosphodiesterase 4 (PDE-4), which increases cAMP levels and leads to bronchodilation and anti-inflammatory effects. Additionally, theophylline's antagonism of adenosine receptors and its role in enhancing histone deacetylation may also contribute to its therapeutic effects. Therefore, the inhibition of PDE-4 is a key mechanism, but the other actions also play significant roles in its overall efficacy in asthma management.

question: Most common cause of urethral stricture is. Answer Choices: A. Trauma B. Infection C. Congenital D. Post endoscopy
label: Trauma
rationale: The most common cause of urethral stricture is [LABEL], specifically injuries such as bulbar urethral injury and pelvic fracture urethral disruption injury.

question: Not a treatment option for psoriasis -. Answer Choices: A. Retinoids B. Methotrexate C. Cyclosporine D. Oral corticosteroids
label: Oral corticosteroids
rationale: [LABEL] (e.g., Prednisone, Prednisolone, Methylprednisolone) are not a standard treatment option for psoriasis due to the risk of severe flares upon withdrawal. Their use is generally avoided in favor of other systemic treatments like Methotrexate, Cyclosporine, and Acitretin. Therefore, the statement that "[LABEL] are not a treatment option for psoriasis" is correct.

question: Pseudosulcus in larynx is. Answer Choices: A. Laryngopharangeal reflux B. Vocal abuse C. Chronic steroid use D. Tuberculosis
label: Laryngopharangeal reflux
rationale: Patients with pseudosulcus in the larynx are likely to have laryngopharyngeal reflux, which is related to gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). This is due to the irritation and inflammation caused by stomach acid reaching the larynx, leading to the characteristic edema seen in pseudosulcus.

question: An 18-month-old boy presents to the pediatrician by his mother for a routine check-up. The mother has no concerns, although she asks about the "hole in his heart" that the patient had at birth. The patient has no history of cyanosis or heart failure; however, a holosystolic, harsh murmur was noted at the 3- and 6-month check-ups. On examination, the patient is playful and alert. He has met all developmental milestones. The cardiac examination reveals a regular rate and rhythm with persistence of the holosystolic, harsh murmur. What is the most likely cause of the murmur in this child?. Answer Choices: A. Defect of muscular interventricular septum B. Defect of the membranous interventricular septum C. Defective dynein functioning D. Failure of endocardial cushion to form
label: Defect of the membranous interventricular septum
rationale: The most likely cause of the murmur in this child is a defect of the membranous part of the interventricular septum, consistent with a ventricular septal defect (VSD).

question: A 3-day-old newborn is brought to the physician because of abdominal distention, inconsolable crying, and 3 episodes of bilious vomiting since the previous evening. He was delivered at home at 40 weeks' gestation by a trained midwife. He has not passed meconium. Physical examination shows abdominal distention, a tight anal sphincter, and an explosive passage of air and feces on removal of the examining finger. Abnormal development of which of the following best explains this patient's condition?. Answer Choices: A. Muscularis mucosae and serosa B. Submucosa and muscularis externa C. Epithelium and submucosa D. Muscularis mucosae and lamina propria
label: Submucosa and muscularis externa

question: A 44-year-old man with HIV comes to the physician for a routine follow-up examination. He has been noncompliant with his antiretroviral medication regimen for several years. question: A 44-year-old male is brought to the emergency department by fire and rescue after he was the unrestrained driver in a motor vehicle accident. The patient's history and clinical presentation strongly suggest undiagnosed pheochromocytoma, and the lack of appropriate alpha-blockade led to unopposed alpha-adrenergic vasoconstriction and hemodynamic collapse.

He has had an influenza-like infection that lasted about 6 weeks in the past 3 months which exacerbated his asthma attacks. He used his puffers to relieve his symptoms and did not seek medical treatment. He is otherwise healthy. On examination, his blood pressure is 118/75 mm Hg, respirations are 17/min, pulse is 78/min, and temperature is 36.7°C (98.1°F). There is no evidence of enlarged lymph nodes or a sore throat. On palpation, the thyroid gland is enlarged and tender. He is a non-smoker with a BMI of 25 kg/m2. He has not used any medications recently. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?. Answer Choices: A. Lymphoma B. Chronic lymphocytic thyroiditis C. Subacute granulomatous thyroiditis D. Fibrous thyroiditis
label: Subacute granulomatous thyroiditis
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is [LABEL], an inflammatory condition of the thyroid gland often triggered by a preceding viral infection.

question: A 27-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with right lower quadrant abdominal pain and vaginal spotting. She denies diarrhea, constipation, or blood in the stool. The medical history is unremarkable. She does not use tobacco or drink alcohol. She is sexually active with her husband and uses an IUD for contraception. The temperature is 37.2 °C (99.0°F), the blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg, the pulse is 80/min, and the respiratory rate is 12/min. The physical examination reveals localized tenderness in the right adnexa, but no masses are palpated. The LMP was 8 weeks ago. Which of the following is most likely associated with this patient’s diagnosis?. Answer Choices: A. Physical examination reveals rebound tenderness and tenderness at McBurney’s point B. Positive urinary beta-HCG and no intrauterine mass C. Barium enema shows true diverticuli in the colon D. Positive urinary beta-HCG and some products of conception in the uterus
label: Positive urinary beta-HCG and no intrauterine mass
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is ectopic pregnancy, which is associated with a positive urinary beta-HCG and the absence of an intrauterine mass on ultrasound. This conclusion aligns with the clinical presentation and the reasoning process.

question: The mother of a newborn boy is alarmed that her baby regurgitates at every feeding. An endoscopic examination reveals that the child's esophagus is almost completely occluded. This finding represents an example of which of the following errors of morphogenesis?. Answer Choices: A. Aplasia B. Atresia C. Dysplasia D. Dysraphic anomaly
label: Atresia
rationale: The diagnosis is esophageal [LABEL], a condition characterized by esophageal occlusion due to incomplete lumen formation during embryogenesis. This conclusion aligns with the clinical presentation, endoscopic findings, and the described embryological defect.

question: Nerve supply of tip of nose. Answer Choices: A. Ext nasal branch of ophthalmic division of B. Inferior orbital nerve C. Buccal br of mandibular nerve D. Orbital of maxillary n
label: Ext nasal branch of ophthalmic division of
rationale: The external nasal branch of the anterior ethmoidal nerve, which is a branch of the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve, is responsible for the nerve supply to the tip of the nose.

question: A 29-year-old woman presents to the primary care office for a recent history of falls. She has fallen 5 times over the last year. These falls are not associated with any preceding symptoms; she specifically denies dizziness, lightheadedness, or visual changes. However, she has started noticing that both of her legs feel weak. She's also noticed that her carpet feels strange beneath her bare feet. Her mother and grandmother have a history of similar problems. On physical exam, she has notable leg and foot muscular atrophy and 4/5 strength throughout her bilateral lower extremities. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is decreased up to the mid-calf. Ankle jerk reflex is absent bilaterally. Which of the following is the next best diagnostic test for this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Ankle-brachial index B. Electromyography (including nerve conduction studies) C. Lumbar puncture D. MRI brain
label: Electromyography (including nerve conduction studies)
rationale: The next best diagnostic test is electromyography (EMG) and nerve conduction studies (NCS) to evaluate for genetic peripheral neuropathy, such as Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease. These studies will help confirm the diagnosis and guide further genetic testing if needed.

question: The most rapid method to resynthesize ATP during exercise is through:. Answer Choices: A. glycolysis. B. phosphocreatine breakdown. C. tricarboxylic acid cycle (Krebs' cycle). D. glycogenolysis.
label: phosphocreatine breakdown
rationale: The most rapid method for ATP resynthesis during exercise is [LABEL], facilitated by creatine kinase activity. This aligns with the physiological need for immediate energy during high-intensity activities.

question: A 16-year-old boy is brought to a psychotherapist for counseling because he was physically abused by his father. During the first therapy session, the patient recounts the numerous encounters that he had with his abuser. At the end of the session, the therapist, who lost her own son in a car accident when he was 15 years old, refuses to let the patient take the bus back alone to his custodial guardian's home. She offers to take him back in her own car instead, saying, “This way, I will rest assured that you have reached home safely”. The therapist's behavior can be best described as an instance of which of the following?. Answer Choices: A. Isolation B. Sublimation C. Countertransference D. Identification
label: Countertransference
rationale: The therapist's behavior can be best described as [LABEL], as her personal history and unresolved grief appear to be influencing her professional actions and decision-making in this situation.

question: A 24-year-old professional soccer player presents to the clinic with discomfort and pain while walking. He says that he has an unstable knee joint that started after an injury during a match last week. He adds that he heard a popping sound at the time of the injury. Physical examination of the knee reveals swelling of the knee joint with a positive anterior drawer test. Which of the following structures is most likely damaged in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Lateral collateral ligament B. Anterior cruciate ligament C. Posterior cruciate ligament D. Ligamentum patellae
label: Anterior cruciate ligament
rationale: The [LABEL] (ACL) is the structure most likely damaged in this patient.

question: A 45-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 1-day history of malaise and abdominal pain. Six weeks ago, he had vomiting and watery diarrhea for 2 days that resolved without treatment. Twelve weeks ago, he underwent orthotopic liver transplantation for alcoholic cirrhosis. At the time of discharge, his total serum bilirubin concentration was 1.0 mg/dL. He stopped drinking alcohol one year ago. His current medications include daily tacrolimus, prednisone, valganciclovir, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. His temperature is 37.7°C (99.9°F), pulse is 95/min, and blood pressure is 150/80 mm Hg. He appears uncomfortable and has mild jaundice. Examination shows scleral icterus. The abdomen is soft and tender to deep palpation over the right upper quadrant, where there is a well-healed surgical scar. His leukocyte count is 2500/mm3, serum bilirubin concentration is 2.6 mg/dL, and serum tacrolimus concentration is within therapeutic range. Which of the following is the next appropriate step in diagnosis?. Answer Choices: A. CT scan of the abdomen with contrast B. Viral loads C. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy D. Ultrasound of the liver
label: Ultrasound of the liver
rationale: The next appropriate step in diagnosis is an [LABEL] to evaluate for biliary obstruction, graft dysfunction, or other structural abnormalities.

question: A 52-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of shortness of breath and nonproductive cough. He has smoked 1 pack of cigarettes daily for 15 years. Cardiopulmonary examination shows fine inspiratory crackles bilaterally. There is clubbing present in the fingers bilaterally. Pulmonary function tests (PFTs) show an FVC of 78% of expected and an FEV1/FVC ratio of 92%. A CT scan of the chest is shown. Which of the following is the most likely underlying diagnosis?. Answer Choices: A. Pulmonary fibrosis B. Bronchopulmonary aspergillosis C. Chronic bronchiectasis D. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
label: Pulmonary fibrosis
rationale: The most likely underlying diagnosis is idiopathic [LABEL] (IPF). This is supported by the patient’s symptoms (dyspnea, nonproductive cough), physical findings (fine inspiratory crackles, clubbing), restrictive PFTs, and the absence of other systemic or environmental factors.

question: Trendlenburg's test done for varicose veins is for detection of:. Answer Choices: A. Perforator in competency B. Deep veins patency C. Saphenofemoral incompetency D. Site of perforators
label: Saphenofemoral incompetency
rationale: The primary purpose of Trendelenburg's test is to assess [LABEL], focusing on the function of the saphenous vein and its role in venous circulation. This aligns with its use in diagnosing conditions like chronic venous insufficiency and varicose veins.

question: A 37-year-old primigravid woman at 36 weeks' gestation is admitted to the hospital 30 minutes after the onset of labor. On arrival, contractions occur every 8–10 minutes. During the last 2 days she has noted decreased fetal movements. The pregnancy had been complicated by gestational hypertension. Current medications include labetalol and a pregnancy multivitamin. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 94/min, and blood pressure is 154/96 mm Hg. On pelvic examination, the cervix is 40% effaced and 2 cm dilated; the vertex is at -2 station. The uterus is consistent in size with a 30-week gestation. Ultrasonography shows the fetus in vertex position and a decreased amount of amniotic fluid. A fetal heart tracing is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?. Answer Choices: A. Umbilical cord prolapse B. Umbilical cord compression C. Physiologic fetal heart rate pattern D. Placental insufficiency
label: Placental insufficiency
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is [LABEL], as it explains the combination of gestational hypertension, decreased fetal movement, oligohydramnios, and uterine size discrepancy. These findings are consistent with impaired placental function leading to fetal growth restriction and distress.

question: Body Mass Index is expressed as: March 2012, March 2013 (c, g). Answer Choices: A. Gram/metre2 B. Gram/ centimetre2 C. Kilogram/ metre2 D. Kilogram/centimetre2
label: Kilogram/ metre2
rationale: Body Mass Index (BMI) is expressed as \( \text{kilogram/metre}^2 \) (\(kg/m^2\)). This conclusion aligns with the formula and dimensional analysis.

question: Two days after hospitalization for urgent chemotherapy to treat Burkitt’s lymphoma, a 7-year-old boy develops dyspnea and reduced urine output. He also feels a tingling sensation in his fingers and toes. Blood pressure is 100/65 mm Hg, respirations are 28/min, pulse is 100/min, and temperature is 36.2°C (97.2°F). The lungs are clear to auscultation. He has excreted 20 mL of urine in the last 6 hours. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 15 g/dL Leukocyte count 6,000/mm3 with a normal differential serum K+ 6.5 mEq/L Ca+ 7.6 mg/dL Phosphorus 5.4 mg/dL HCO3− 15 mEq/L Uric acid 12 mg/dL Urea nitrogen 44 mg/dL Creatinine 2.4 mg/dL Arterial blood gas analysis on room air: pH 7.30 PCO2 30 mm Hg O2 saturation 95% Which of the following is most likely to have prevented this patient’s condition?. Answer Choices: A. Allopurinol B. Ciprofloxacin C. Sodium bicarbonate D. No prevention would have been effective
label: Allopurinol
rationale: [LABEL] could have prevented the patient’s condition by managing hyperuricemia and reducing the risk of uric acid nephropathy, a key component of tumor lysis syndrome.

question: A 25-year-old medical student is doing an international health elective in the Amazon River basin studying tropical disease epidemiology. As part of his pre-trip preparation, he wants to be protected from malaria and is researching options for prophylaxis. Which of the following agents should be avoided for malarial prophylaxis in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Chloroquine B. Mefloquine C. Atovaquone-proguanil D. Quinine
label: Chloroquine
rationale: [LABEL] should indeed be avoided for malarial prophylaxis in this patient because the Amazon River basin is a region with widespread [LABEL]-resistant *P. falciparum*. Alternative prophylactic agents should be considered to ensure effective protection against malaria.

question: A 36-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital with severe head injuries after a car crash. During neurologic examination it is noted that her uvula is deviated to the right. Which of the following muscles is paralyzed?. Answer Choices: A. Left levator veli palatini B. Left tensor veli palatini C. Right levator veli palatini D. Right tensor veli palatini
label: Left levator veli palatini
rationale: The [LABEL] muscle is paralyzed, causing the uvula to move toward the intact right side. This is consistent with a left vagus nerve (CN X) injury, likely due to the head trauma.

question: The most malignant form of malignant melanoma is ?. Answer Choices: A. Nodular B. Hutchinson's melanotic freckle C. Acral lentiginous type D. Superficial spreading
label: Nodular
rationale: [LABEL] malignant melanoma is the most malignant form of malignant melanoma due to its rapid vertical growth phase, early invasion, and higher metastatic potential, even when matched for tumor thickness, gender, and age.

question: A 23-year-old woman with Ehlers-Danlos syndrome is brought to the emergency department with right knee pain and swelling after she twisted it while dancing. She had two similar episodes last year that were treated conservatively. She was treated for a fracture of her humerus 2 years ago. She has gastroesophageal reflux disease. Her sister has allergic rhinitis. Her only medication is omeprazole. She appears tense and uncomfortable. Her temperature is 37.1°C (99.3°F), pulse is 97/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Examination shows mild scoliosis. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination shows a mid-systolic click. The right knee shows a large, tender effusion and the patella is displaced laterally. Lachman's test, the posterior drawer test, and Apley's test are negative. A complete blood count and serum concentrations of electrolytes, urea nitrogen, and creatinine are within the reference range. Toxicology screening is negative. X-ray of the knee joint shows an osteochondral fragment within the knee joint. What is the most appropriate next step in management?. Answer Choices: A. Arthroscopy B. Above knee cast C. Total knee replacement D. Physiotherapy only
label: Arthroscopy
rationale: The most appropriate next step in management for this patient is [LABEL] to evaluate and treat the osteochondral fragment and address any associated intra-articular pathology.

question: A 32-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of diarrhea. During this period, he has had about 10 bowel movements per day. He states that his stools are light brown and watery, with no blood or mucus. He also reports mild abdominal pain and nausea. Over the past year, he has had 6 episodes of diarrhea that lasted several days and resolved spontaneously. Over this time, he also noticed frequent episodes of reddening in his face and neck. He returned from a 10-day trip to Nigeria 3 weeks ago. There is no personal or family history of serious illness. He has smoked a pack of cigarettes daily for the past 13 years. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 100/60 mm Hg. Physical examination shows dry mucous membranes. The abdomen is tender with no rebound or guarding. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Serum studies show: Na+ 136 mEq/L Cl- 102 mEq/L K+ 2.3 mEq/L HCO3- 22 mEq/L Mg2+ 1.7 mEq/L Ca2+ 12.3 mg/dL Glucose (fasting) 169 mg/dL Nasogastric tube aspiration reveals significantly decreased gastric acid production. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?". Answer Choices: A. Excessive accumulation of mast cells B. Elevated serum VIP concentration C. Increased conversion of 5-hydroxytryptophan to serotonin D. Transmural inflammation of the intestinal walls
label: Elevated serum VIP concentration
rationale: The most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms is a VIPoma, characterized by [LABEL] and increased vasoactive intestinal polypeptide receptor activity.

question: During DNA replication, mistakes are coded into the leading strand about once every 100,000/1 million copies. This DNA is subject to proofreading by several mechanisms. If a mistake is noted and the incorrect base is removed shortly following the time RNA primer is removed, this would most likely be the work of which repair mechanism?. Answer Choices: A. DNA polymerase I B. DNA polymerase III C. Mismatch repair mechanism D. Endonuclease repair
label: DNA polymerase I
rationale: The repair mechanism responsible for removing the incorrect base shortly after the RNA primer is removed is [LABEL] in prokaryotes. However, in eukaryotes, this function is typically carried out by a combination of FEN1, DNA2, and DNA polymerase δ or ε. The answer is context-dependent, but for prokaryotes, [LABEL] is correct.

question: A 40-year-old man is rushed to the emergency department after being involved in a motor vehicle accident. He has lacerations on his right arm and some minor abrasions on his face and lower limbs. The resident on call quickly manages the patient with proper care of his open wounds in the emergency department. The patient is admitted to the surgery unit for the daily care of his wounds. His lacerations begin to heal with proper dressing and occasional debridement. Which of the following best describes the healing process in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Abundant lymphocytes accumulate during the healing process, forming a granuloma. B. Healing by secondary intention will occur along with the deposition of large amount of granulation tissue. C. Healing involves abscess formation, which should be drained. D. Healing by primary intention will occur without granulation tissue formation.
label: Healing by secondary intention will occur along with the deposition of large amount of granulation tissue
rationale: The healing process in this patient involves wound healing by secondary intention, characterized by the deposition of a large amount of granulation tissue, which is typical for cutaneous wounds. This aligns with the expected physiological response to the described injuries.

question: Which of the following best defines the "Saccade"-. Answer Choices: A. Voluntary slow eye movements B. Involuntary slow eye movement C. Abrupt, involuntary slow eye movements D. Abrupt, involuntary rapid eye movements
label: Abrupt, involuntary rapid eye movements
rationale: Saccades are defined as abrupt, rapid, and typically voluntary eye movements that are part of normal physiological function. They are not a type of abnormal involuntary eye movement. The provided answer appears to be incorrect.

question: In metabolic alkalosis, which Is true about excretion in urine. Answer Choices: A. More of NH3 B. Less of aceto-acetic acid C. Betahydroxy butyric acid D. Less ammonia
label: Less ammonia
rationale: In metabolic alkalosis, [LABEL] (NH3) is excreted in urine due to reduced NH3 production. This reduction is a consequence of decreased H+ availability, which limits the activity of renal glutaminase and NH4+ formation. This process impacts the ammonium ion metabolic process and sodium ion binding in the renal tubule and distal tubule.

question: Which of the following is false about mucocele of gallbladder. Answer Choices: A. Complication of gallstones B. Treatment is early cholecystectomy C. Obstruction at neck of gallbladder D. Gallbladder is never palpable
label: Gallbladder is never palpable
rationale: The statement "[LABEL]" is false. A palpable gallbladder is a recognized clinical feature in cases of mucocele or hydrops of the gallbladder. Early cholecystectomy is advised to prevent complications.

question: A 31-year-old G3P1 woman who is at 37 weeks of gestation is brought into the emergency department by her husband after repeated twitching. According to the husband, they were watching TV when she suddenly became unresponsive and her hands and legs started shaking. The episode lasted about 5 minutes and she “seemed to be sleeping afterwards.” Her past medical history is significant for pregnancy induced hypertension. The patient is tired but responsive and denies urinary incontinence, paresthesia, fever, upper respiratory signs, or new medication changes. She denies a history of seizures. Her temperature is 99°F (37°C), blood pressure is 186/97 mmHg, pulse is 96/min, and respirations are 12/min. Physical examination demonstrates a lethargic patient with moderate right upper quadrant tenderness. What is the most appropriate next step for this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Emergency cesarean section B. Expectant management C. Intravenous infusion of oxytocin D. Intravenous magnesium sulfate
label: Intravenous magnesium sulfate
rationale: The most appropriate next step for this patient is [LABEL] to manage her eclampsia and prevent further seizures.

question: A 43-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department for evaluation of worsening abdominal pain that suddenly started 2 hours ago. The patient also has nausea and has vomited twice. She has hypothyroidism, systemic lupus erythematosus, major depressive disorder, and chronic right knee pain. Current medications include levothyroxine, prednisone, fluoxetine, naproxen, and a chondroitin sulfate supplement. She appears distressed. Her temperature is 37.9°C (100.2°F), pulse is 101/min, and blood pressure is 115/70 mm Hg. Examination shows a rigid abdomen with rebound tenderness; bowel sounds are hypoactive. Laboratory studies show a leukocyte count of 13,300/mm3 and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 70 mm/h. An x-ray of the chest is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?. Answer Choices: A. Peritoneal lavage B. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy C. Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography D. Exploratory laparotomy
label: Exploratory laparotomy
rationale: The most appropriate next step in management is [LABEL]. This is supported by the clinical presentation of peritonitis, the elevated inflammatory markers, and the patient's risk factors (SLE, prednisone, and NSAID use).

question: A 58-year-old Caucasian woman visits her primary care physician for an annual check-up. She has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and stage 3A chronic kidney disease. Her estimated glomerular filtration rate has not changed since her last visit. Today, her parathyroid levels are moderately elevated. She lives at home with her husband and 2 children and works as a bank clerk. Her vitals are normal, and her physical examination is unremarkable. Which of the following explains this new finding?. Answer Choices: A. Phosphate retention B. Hyperuricemia C. Hypercalcemia D. Uremia
label: Phosphate retention
rationale: The elevated circulating parathyroid hormone level in this patient is best explained by impaired phosphate excretion and reduced activation of vitamin D in chronic kidney disease, leading to secondary hyperparathyroidism. The provided explanation (increased renal tubular phosphate reabsorption) is incorrect, as elevated PTH typically decreases phosphate reabsorption in the renal tubules.

question: A 35-year-old man with spina bifida is admitted to the hospital for a urologic procedure. He has been functionally independent in activities of daily living and is employed doing inventory control in a local sporting goods store. He has maintained continence through periodic self-catheterization. The patient is paraplegic, has recurrent calcium oxalate kidney stones, and recent onset of incontinence secondary to detrusor and bladder neck dysfunction. Vital signs are normal. Physical examination shows a well-developed, well-nourished man in no acute distress. Aside from paraplegia, lower extremity muscle atrophy, and lower abdominal surgical scars, the physical examination discloses no abnormalities. He had an episode of anaphylaxis secondary to latex allergy during a previous operation for functional expansion of his bladder through a bowel anastomosis. Which of the following is most important to consider in the care of this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Administration of injectable medications with disposable syringes B. Preparation of food by outside contractors C. Type of cleaning agents used to sterilize bed linens D. Use of rubber urethral catheters
label: Use of rubber urethral catheters
rationale: The most important consideration in the care of this patient is avoiding latex-containing materials, such as rubber urethral catheters, to prevent life-threatening allergic reactions. Additionally, managing overactive bladder and bladder neck obstruction is essential to address his urinary symptoms and improve his quality of life.

question: Calcaneum is associated most commonly with which # -. Answer Choices: A. # rib B. # vertebrae C. # skull D. # fibula
label: # vertebrae
rationale: The calcaneum is most commonly associated with vertebral injuries due to the axial loading mechanism of a fall from height. While injuries to the pelvis and hip are also possible, crumpled long bones are not a typical outcome of this mechanism. Therefore, the most accurate conclusion is that the calcaneum is associated most commonly with vertebral injuries.

question: Blood stained sputum may be the only symptom in?. Answer Choices: A. Bronchiectasis B. Carcinoma bronchus C. Adenoma bronchus D. Pulmonary T.B.
label: Adenoma bronchus
rationale: Based on the reasoning, bronchus adenoma is a plausible condition where hemoptysis may be the only symptom. This is because bronchial adenomas, particularly carcinoid tumors, are known to present with isolated hemoptysis in some cases without other significant symptoms.

question: Which of the following is true about the carpal tunnel syndrome?. Answer Choices: A. It can be caused by rheumatoid arthritis B. It is caused by compression of the ulna nerve C. It causes pain and tingling in the medial three and half digits D. Paraesthesia is worse during the day
label: It can be caused by rheumatoid arthritis
rationale: Carpal tunnel syndrome can indeed be caused by rheumatoid arthritis, as RA can lead to inflammation, tenosynovitis, and structural changes in the wrist that contribute to median nerve compression.

question: Maturity Onset Diabetes of the Young (MODY) type 2 is a consequence of a defective pancreatic enzyme, which normally acts as a glucose sensor, resulting in a mild hyperglycemia. The hyperglycemia is especially exacerbated during pregnancy. Which of the following pathways is controlled by this enzyme?. Answer Choices: A. Glucose --> glucose-6-phosphate B. Glucose-6-phosphate --> fructose-6-phosphate C. Fructose-6-phosphate --> fructose-1,6-biphosphate D. Phosphoenolpyruvate --> pyruvate
label: Glucose --> glucose-6-phosphate
rationale: The pathway controlled by the glucokinase (GCK) enzyme involves glucose binding and the conversion of glucose to glucose-6-phosphate, which is a key step in glucose metabolism. This explains the mild hyperglycemia observed in MODY type 2 and its exacerbation during pregnancy due to increased insulin resistance.

question: Which of the following endocrine tumors is most commonly seen in MEN I ?. Answer Choices: A. Insulinoma B. Gastrinoma C. Glucagonoma D. Somatotrophinoma
label: Gastrinoma
rationale: The most commonly seen endocrine gland neoplasm in MEN I is pancreatic [LABEL].

question: Autozygosity mapping is used to map disorders that show which pattern of inheritance?. Answer Choices: A. Autosomal dominant B. Autosomal recessive C. X-linked dominant D. X-linked recessive
label: Autosomal recessive
rationale: Autozygosity mapping is primarily used to map disorders that follow an [LABEL] inheritance pattern. This is because the technique is particularly effective in identifying homozygous mutations in regions of the genome that are identical by descent, which are common in [LABEL] disorders.

question: Which of the following is known as monkey fever -. Answer Choices: A. Plague B. KFD C. Yellow fever D. Trench fever
label: KFD
rationale: The disease known as "monkey fever" is also referred to as Kyasanur Forest Disease ([LABEL]).

question: Most common site of GIST is -. Answer Choices: A. Ileum B. Esophagus C. Colon D. Stomach
label: Stomach
rationale: The most common site of gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GISTs) is the [LABEL].

question: A 5-year-old boy presents to a pediatric orthopedic surgeon for evaluation of spinal curvature. His primary care physician noticed during an annual checkup that the boy's shoulders were uneven, and radiograph revealed early onset scoliosis. His past medical history is significant for multiple fractures as well as short stature. Based on the early presentation of scoliosis and the unusual history of fractures, the surgeon orders further workup and discovers a genetic mutation in an extracellular protein. This protein exists in two different forms. The first is an insoluble dimer that is linked by disulfide bonds and links integrins to the extracellular matrix. The second is a soluble protein that assists with clotting. Based on these descriptions, which of the following proteins is most likely mutated in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Dermatan sulfate B. Fibronectin C. Type 1 collagen D. Type 3 collagen
label: Fibronectin
rationale: The genetic mutation most likely involves [LABEL] (FN1), an extracellular protein that links integrins to the extracellular matrix and is associated with scoliosis, multiple rib fractures, and short stature.

question: An 18 year old male patient presented to the clinic with pain and swelling in the lower jaw. Intraoral examination revealed localized gingival bleeding in the right posterior region. On palpation, pulsations can be appreciated. Lesions are also appreciated on the frontal bone on a radiograph. Which condition is present in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Vascular malformation B. Cherubism C. Eosinophilic granuloma D. Brown tumor
label: Vascular malformation
rationale: The condition present in this patient is [LABEL], likely involving the mandible and associated with symptoms such as gingival bleeding, tenderness, and abnormal pulse.

question: Which of the following tests are used in the evaluation of a suspected CSF leak?. Answer Choices: A. Beta 2 microglobulin B. Beta 2 transferrin C. Tyroglobulin D. Transthyretin
label: Beta 2 transferrin
rationale: Beta-2 transferrin testing is a highly specific and reliable diagnostic tool for detecting cerebrospinal fluid rhinorrhea. It helps confirm the presence of a CSF leak by identifying a marker unique to CSF, differentiating it from other bodily fluids.

question: APC gene exes its effect in combination with which of the following. Answer Choices: A. Beta catenin B. E cadherin C. Alpha catenin D. BCX
label: Beta catenin
rationale: The APC gene plays a crucial role in the regulation of the Wnt signaling pathway by controlling the levels of beta-catenin. This regulation is essential to prevent genes that stimulate cell division from being activated too frequently, thereby preventing cell overgrowth. The interaction of APC with components like Axin1 and CSNK1A1 supports its function in maintaining normal cellular processes and preventing tumorigenesis.

question: Which of the following factors of balanced occlusion is given by the patient?. Answer Choices: A. Condylar guidance B. Incisal guidance C. Inclination of the cusps D. Orientation of the occlusal plane
label: Condylar guidance
rationale: Out of the five factors of balanced occlusion, only [LABEL] is recorded from the patient using protrusive records. This guidance is influenced by the anatomy of the bone fossa (glenoid fossa) and is not under the dentist’s control.

question: True about Glomus- jugulare tumour - a) Most common in male b) Arises from non- chromaffin cells c) Lymph node metastasis seen d) Multicentric e) Fluctuating tinnitus and conductive type of hearing loss seen. Answer Choices: A. acde B. abc C. bde D. bcde
label: bde
rationale: The glomus jugulare neoplasm is a benign tumor that does not exhibit lymph node metastasis. It is associated with symptoms such as fluctuating pulsatile tinnitus, conductive hearing impairment, and multifocal occurrences in some cases, particularly in familial forms.

question: A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a lacerated wound on her left forearm. She got the wound accidentally when she slipped in her garden and scraped her hand against some nails sticking out of the fence. The patient has rheumatoid arthritis and takes methylprednisolone 16 mg/day. She cannot recall her vaccination history. On physical examination her blood pressure is 140/95 mm Hg, heart rate is 81/min, respiratory rate is 16/min, and temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F). The wound is irregularly shaped and lacerated and measures 4 × 5 cm with a depth of 0.5 cm. It is contaminated with dirt. The physician decides to administer both the tetanus toxoid and immunoglobulin after wound treatment. What is true regarding the tetanus prophylaxis in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. It does not make sense to administer tetanus toxoid as it will fail to induce sufficient immunity in a patient who takes oral glucocorticoids. B. The immunoglobulin administration will provide sufficient levels of anti-tetanus toxin antibodies until the production of the patient’s own antibodies starts. C. It does not make sense to administer tetanus toxoid as it will fail to induce sufficient immunity in patients aged more than 60 years. D. Immunoglobulin administration can provide constant levels of antibodies in the patient’s blood for more than 4 months.
label: The immunoglobulin administration will provide sufficient levels of anti-tetanus toxin antibodies until the production of the patient’s own antibodies starts

question: Which of the following is not false about annual risk of TB. Answer Choices: A. ARI of 1% = 75 new cases B. Current ARI in India is 1.7% C. It is represents new cases of TB D. It is assessed by tuberculin conversion in previously non-vaccinated children

question: A 45-year-old woman, suspected of having colon cancer, is advised to undergo a contrast-CT scan of the abdomen. She has no comorbidities and no significant past medical history. question: A histological section of the left ventricle of a deceased 28-year-old male shows classic contraction band necrosis of the myocardium.
