rationale: [LABEL] alkaloid is the hepatotoxin responsible for endemic ascites. It causes hepatic veno-occlusive disease, leading to liver damage, ascites, and jaundice.

question: A 42-year-old man is brought in to the emergency department by his daughter. She reports that her father drank heavily for the last 16 years, but he stopped 4 days ago after he decided to quit drinking on his birthday. She also reports that he has been talking about seeing cats running in his room since this morning, although there were no cats. There is no history of any known medical problems or any other substance use. On physical examination, his temperature is 38.4ºC (101.2ºF), heart rate is 116/min, blood pressure is 160/94 mm Hg, and respiratory rate is 22/min. He is severely agitated and is not oriented to his name, time, or place. On physical examination, profuse perspiration and tremors are present. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiologic mechanism underlying his condition?. Answer Choices: A. Functional increase in GABA B. Increased activity of NMDA receptors C. Increased inhibition of norepinephrine D. Increased inhibition of glutamate
label: Increased activity of NMDA receptors
rationale: The pathophysiologic mechanism underlying this patient's condition involves increased activity of NMDA glutamate receptors. This is consistent with the clinical presentation of severe alcohol withdrawal (delirium tremens) and the known neurochemical changes associated with chronic alcohol use and withdrawal.

question: A 30-year-old man presents with a 1-month history of frequent intermittent headaches. He says the headaches typically occur between 3–4 times/day, mostly at night, each lasting minutes to 1–2 hours. He describes the pain as severe, stabbing, unilateral, and localized to the left periorbital region. He says he frequently notes increased tear production and conjunctival injection in the left eye and rhinorrhea during these headaches. He mentions that he had a similar 3-week episode of these same, frequent intermittent headaches 3 months ago which stopped completely until 1 month ago. He denies any seizures, loss of consciousness, nausea, vomiting, photophobia, or phonophobia. His past medical history is significant for stable angina secondary to coronary artery disease diagnosed on a stress echocardiogram 1 year ago. He reports occasional alcohol use, which he says precipitates the headaches, but denies any smoking or recreational drug use. The patient is afebrile, and his vital signs are within normal limits. A physical examination is unremarkable. A noncontrast computed tomography (CT) scan of the head is normal. Which of the following is the best abortive treatment for this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Sumatriptan B. High-flow 100% oxygen C. Hydrocodone D. Intranasal lidocaine
label: High-flow 100% oxygen
rationale: The best abortive treatment for this patient is [LABEL], as it is effective in alleviating symptoms of cluster headaches and is safe for a patient with coronary artery disease.

question: A 48-year-old woman visits the clinic with unintentional weight loss for the past 3 months. She is also concerned about difficulty swallowing solid food. She also has early satiety and mild abdominal discomfort. An upper gastrointestinal endoscopy is advised along with a biopsy. The histopathological report reveals gastric adenocarcinoma. She then undergoes a subtotal gastrectomy and is started on an adjuvant chemotherapy regimen with platinum and fluoropyrimidine. 2 weeks later she develops acute respiratory distress and chest pain. A D-dimer test is positive. Her blood pressure is 125/78, heart rate is 110/min, and oxygen saturation is 88%. CT scan of the chest reveals a clot in the anterior segmental artery in the right upper lung. Which of the following therapies should the patient be started on for her acute condition?. Answer Choices: A. Clopidogrel B. Low-molecular weight heparin C. Ticagrelor D. Aspirin
label: Low-molecular weight heparin
rationale: The patient should be started on low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) for the management of acute venous thromboembolism (pulmonary embolism). This is the most appropriate treatment given her underlying cancer, chemotherapy, and confirmed diagnosis of a pulmonary embolism.

question: An 80-year-old African American male presents complaining of worsening shortness of breath that occurs during his weekly round of golf. He also notes he has been waking up at night "choking and gasping for air", though he has been able to gain some relief by propping his head on a stack of pillows before he goes to bed. Upon auscultation, a low frequency, early diastolic gallop is heard over the apex while the patient rests in the left lateral decubitus position. This finding is most consistent with which of the following?. Answer Choices: A. Right atrial hypertrophy B. Left atrial hypertrophy C. Left ventricular eccentric hypertrophy D. Mitral stenosis
label: Left ventricular eccentric hypertrophy
rationale: The findings are most consistent with [LABEL], which is typically seen in conditions like dilated cardiomyopathy or chronic volume overload. This conclusion aligns with the patient's symptoms (exertional dyspnea, orthopnea) and physical findings (S3 gallop at the apex).

question: A 50-year-old female teacher presents to the clinic with complaints of discoloration of the skin around the right ankle accompanied by itching. She began noticing it a month ago and the pruritus worsened over time. She also has some pain and swelling of the region every night, especially on days when she teaches late into the evening. Her past medical history is significant for diabetes mellitus type 2, for which she takes metformin. She lives with her husband and takes oral contraceptive pills. On examination, the physician observes hyperpigmentation of the medial aspect of her right ankle. The skin is dry, scaly, and edematous along with some superficial varicosities. Dorsiflexion of the foot is extremely painful. Peripheral pulses are equally palpable on both lower limbs. There is a small 2 cm ulcer noted near the medial malleolus with thickened neighboring skin and indurated edges. Laboratory studies show D-dimer of 1,000 µg/L and HbA1c of 9%. Doppler ultrasound of the lower extremity reveals an intramural thrombus in the popliteal vein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Basal cell carcinoma B. Stasis dermatitis C. Atopic dermatitis D. Cellulitis
label: Stasis dermatitis
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is [LABEL], which is supported by the patient’s symptoms (pruritus, pain, swelling), examination findings (hyperpigmentation, edema, ulceration), and the Doppler ultrasound finding of a popliteal vein thrombus.

question: Following death of a person, what happens to potassium level in the vitreous.. Answer Choices: A. Potassium level falls B. Potassium level increases C. Remains unchanged D. It depends on cause of death
label: Potassium level increases
rationale: The potassium level in the vitreous body increases after death at a rate of approximately 0.17-0.25 mmol per hour. This predictable increase is a valuable tool in forensic science for determining the time since death.

question: A 54-year-old male comes to the clinic to initiate care with a new physician. He has no complaints at this time. When taking his history, the patient says his medical history is notable for diabetes and hypertension both of which are well managed on his medications. His medications are metformin and lisinopril. A review of systems is negative. While taking the social history, the patient hesitates when asked about alcohol consumption. Further gentle questioning by the physician leads the patient to admit that he drinks 5-6 beers per night and up to 10-12 drinks per day over the weekend. He says that he has been drinking like this for “years.” He becomes emotional and says that his alcohol is negatively affecting his relationship with his wife and children; however, when asked about efforts to decrease his consumption, the patient says he has not tried in the past and doesn’t think he has “the strength to stop”. Which of the following stages of change most accurately describes this patient’s behavior?. Answer Choices: A. Contemplation B. Preparation C. Action D. Maintenance
label: Contemplation
rationale: The patient is in the [LABEL] stage of change, as he recognizes the negative impact of his alcohol consumption on his relationships but has not yet taken steps to reduce or stop drinking.

question: A 47-year-old woman with chronic epigastric pain comes to the physician because of a 1-month history of intermittent, loose, foul-smelling stools. She has also had a 6-kg (13-lb) weight loss. She has consumed 9–10 alcoholic beverages daily for the past 25 years. Seven years ago, she traveled to Mexico on vacation; she has not been outside the large metropolitan area in which she resides since then. She appears malnourished. The stool is pale and loose; fecal fat content is elevated. An immunoglobulin A serum anti-tissue transglutaminase antibody assay is negative. Further evaluation is most likely to show which of the following?. Answer Choices: A. Trophozoites on stool microscopy B. Pancreatic calcifications C. Villous atrophy of duodenal mucosa D. Positive lactulose breath test
label: Pancreatic calcifications
rationale: The most likely finding on further evaluation is [LABEL], which are consistent with chronic pancreatitis caused by the patient’s long history of heavy alcohol use.

question: A 9-year-old girl is brought to her primary care physician because of a 4-month history of early morning headaches and worsening fatigue. The headaches are often accompanied by nausea and sometimes relieved by vomiting. She stopped going to her ballet lessons 2 weeks ago because she was embarrassed about falling frequently and having increasing difficulty performing the steps. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 98/min, and blood pressure is 105/65 mm Hg. She has a broad-based gait. While standing with both feet together and raising her arms, closing her eyes does not affect her balance. She is unable to perform rapid, alternating movements with her hands. An MRI of the brain is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?. Answer Choices: A. Acoustic schwannoma B. Pilocytic astrocytoma C. Oligodendroglioma D. Ependymoma
label: Pilocytic astrocytoma
rationale: The most likely diagnosis, based on the symptoms of increased intracranial pressure and cerebellar dysfunction, is a juvenile [LABEL]. This type of tumor is consistent with the girl's symptoms and is a common pediatric brain tumor affecting the cerebellum.

question: A 31-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 2-week history of diarrhea. She says that she has also noticed that she is losing weight, which makes her feel anxious since she has relatives who have suffered from anorexia. Finally, she says that she is worried she has a fever because she feels warm and has been sweating profusely. On physical examination she is found to have proptosis, fine tremor of her hands, and symmetrical, non-tender thyroid enlargement. Which of the following types of enzymes is targeted by a treatment for this disease?. Answer Choices: A. Cyclooxygenase B. Kinase C. Peroxidase D. Phosphatase
label: Peroxidase
rationale: The treatment for this disease targets thyroid [LABEL] (TPO) activity, which is consistent with the management of hyperthyroidism, particularly in conditions like Graves' disease.

question: An agent added to local anesthetics to speed the onset of action is:. Answer Choices: A. Methylparapben B. Bicarbonate C. Fentanyl D. Adrenaline
label: Bicarbonate
rationale: Sodium [LABEL] is the agent added to local anesthetics to speed the onset of action by creating an alkaline medium, which increases the proportion of the unionized (lipid-soluble) form of the drug, facilitating its transport across the neuronal membrane.

question: Kidney cortex contains:. Answer Choices: A. Loop of henle B. Pyramids C. Collecting tubule and duct D. Calyces
label: Collecting tubule and duct
rationale: The kidney cortex contains the cortical collecting ducts, which are part of the renal tubule system. Therefore, the statement that the kidney cortex contains the [LABEL], which are part of the renal tubule system, is correct.

question: All are cerebral neurotics except. Answer Choices: A. Opioids B. Alcohol C. Nux vomica D. Cannabis
label: Nux vomica
rationale: Based on the reasoning above, [LABEL] is not considered a cerebral neurotic, as its primary action is on the spinal cord and peripheral nerves rather than the cerebral hemisphere. This aligns with the definition of cerebral neurotics and the pharmacological profile of [LABEL].

question: Primary sites of lymphocyte production are. Answer Choices: A. Bone marrow and Thymus B. Spleen and Lymph nodes C. Lymph nodes and mucosa associated lymphoid tissue D. Spleen and mucosa associated lymphoid tissue
label: Bone marrow and Thymus
rationale: The primary sites of lymphocyte production are the [LABEL]. These organs are responsible for the development and maturation of lymphocytes, with the bone marrow producing lymphocyte precursors and maturing B cells, and the thymus maturing T cells. While cytokine production and NFAT transcription factors are important in immune regulation, they are not the primary determinants of lymphocyte production in these organs.

question: True statements about alpha -1 - anti-trypsin deficiency-a) Autosomal dominant diseaseb) Emphysemac) Fibrosis of Portal tractd) Diastase resistant positive hepatocytese) Orcein positive granules. Answer Choices: A. abc B. acd C. bcd D. bd
label: bcd
rationale: Alpha-1-antitrypsin deficiency is associated with both pulmonary emphysema and cirrhosis of the liver. The primary mechanism involves mutations in the SERPINA1 gene, leading to protease-antiprotease imbalance in the lungs (causing emphysema) and accumulation of misfolded proteins in the liver (causing cirrhosis). Fibrosis of the portal tract is a pathological feature of cirrhosis. Therefore, the condition involves pulmonary emphysema and cirrhosis of the liver, with portal tract fibrosis as a result of cirrhosis.

question: Two days after undergoing hemicolectomy for colon cancer, a 78-year-old man is found agitated and confused in his room. He says that a burglar broke in. The patient points at one corner of the room and says “There he is, doctor!” Closer inspection reveals that the patient is pointing to his bathrobe, which is hanging on the wall. The patient has type 2 diabetes mellitus and arterial hypertension. Current medications include insulin and hydrochlorothiazide. His temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse is 89/min, respirations are 15/min, and blood pressure is 145/98 mm Hg. Physical examination shows a nontender, nonerythematous midline abdominal wound. On mental status examination, the patient is agitated and oriented only to person. Which of the following best describes this patient's perception?. Answer Choices: A. Illusion B. Delusion C. Loose association D. Hallucination
label: Illusion
rationale: The most likely explanation for the patient's confusion and agitation is post-surgical delirium, potentially exacerbated by factors such as hypoglycemia or electrolyte imbalance. It is important to assess the patient's blood glucose and electrolyte levels to confirm the underlying cause.

question: Multiple painful ulcers over glans without in duration is suggestive of –. Answer Choices: A. LGV B. Granuloma inguinale C. Chancroid D. 2° syphilis
label: Chancroid
rationale: The condition suggested by multiple painful ulcers over the glans without induration is [LABEL].

question: A 46-year-old African American man presents to the physician with dyspnea on exertion for the past 2 months. He also has occasional episodes of coughing at night. He says that he has been healthy most of his life. He is a non-smoker and a non-alcoholic. He does not have hypercholesterolemia or ischemic heart disease. His father died due to congestive heart failure. On physical examination, the pulse rate was 116/min, the blood pressure was 164/96 mm Hg, and the respiratory rate was 20/min. Chest auscultation reveals bilateral fine crepitations at the lung bases. A complete diagnostic work-up suggests a diagnosis of hypertension with heart failure due to left ventricular dysfunction. Which of the following drug combinations is most likely to benefit the patient?. Answer Choices: A. Amlodipine-Atenolol B. Amlodipine-Valsartan C. Isosorbide dinitrate-Hydralazine D. Metoprolol-Atorvastatin
label: Isosorbide dinitrate-Hydralazine
rationale: The drug combination of Isosorbide Dinitrate and Hydralazine is most likely to benefit this patient. This therapy is particularly effective in African American patients with heart failure due to left ventricular systolic dysfunction, addressing both the pathophysiology and the patient’s specific needs.

question: A 57-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of swelling of both her feet. It improves a little bit with elevation but is still bothersome to her because her shoes no longer fit. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with metformin and linagliptin. She was diagnosed with hypertension 6 months ago and started treatment with amlodipine. Subsequent blood pressure measurements on separate occasions have been around 130/90 mm Hg. She otherwise feels well. Today, her pulse is 80/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 132/88 mm Hg. Cardiovascular examination shows no abnormalities. There is pitting edema of both ankles. Which of the following would have been most likely to reduce the risk of edema in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Addition of enalapril B. Addition of furosemide C. Use of compression stockings D. Use of nifedipine instead
label: Addition of enalapril
rationale: The [LABEL] would most likely reduce the risk of edema in this patient. This is because enalapril can counteract the edema caused by amlodipine while also providing renal protection and maintaining blood pressure control.

question: A 72-year-old woman comes to the physician because she is seeing things that she knows are not there. Sometimes she sees a dog in her kitchen and at other times she sees a stranger in her garden, both of which no one else can see. She also reports a lack of motivation to do daily tasks for the past week. Three years ago, she was diagnosed with Parkinson disease and was started on levodopa and carbidopa. Her younger brother has schizophrenia. The patient also takes levothyroxine for hypothyroidism. She used to drink a bottle of wine every day, but she stopped drinking alcohol 2 months ago. Neurologic examination shows a mild resting tremor of the hands and bradykinesia. Her thought process is organized and logical. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?. Answer Choices: A. Adverse effect of medication B. Schizophrenia C. Major depressive disorder D. Poorly controlled hypothyroidism
label: Adverse effect of medication
rationale: The most likely underlying cause of this patient’s symptoms is an adverse drug response to levodopa, a known side effect in Parkinson’s disease treatment.

question: A 31-year-old male with cirrhosis, dementia, and Parkinson-like symptoms is diagnosed with a hereditary metabolic disease resulting from the accumulation of a certain metal in various tissues. Impairment of which of the following elimination pathways is most likely responsible?. Answer Choices: A. Secretion into bile B. Loop of Henle secretion into lumen of kidney C. Glomerular filtration D. Bleeding
label: Secretion into bile
rationale: The impairment of the transport of bile salts, organic acids, and metal ions, specifically their [LABEL], is most likely responsible for the patient's condition. This aligns with the pathophysiology of Wilson's disease, where defective copper transport leads to its accumulation in various tissues, causing cirrhosis, dementia, and Parkinson-like symptoms.

question: The descending loop of Henle of the nephron of the kidney is permeable to which of the following substances?. Answer Choices: A. Na+ B. H2O C. K+ D. Cl-
label: H2O
rationale: The descending limb of the loop of Henle is permeable to [LABEL].

question: A 24-year-old woman comes to the physician because of progressively worsening episodes of severe, crampy abdominal pain and nonbloody diarrhea for the past 3 years. Examination of the abdomen shows mild distension and generalized tenderness. There is a fistula draining stool in the perianal region. Immunohistochemistry shows dysfunction of the nucleotide oligomerization binding domain 2 (NOD2) protein. This dysfunction most likely causes overactivity of which of the following immunological proteins in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. β-catenin B. NF-κB C. IL-10 D. IL-1β
label: NF-κB
rationale: The dysfunction of the NOD2 protein most likely causes overactivity of [LABEL] in this patient due to impaired bacterial recognition and dysregulation of inflammatory pathways, leading to chronic inflammation.

question: A 33-year-old man presents with his recent laboratory results. He has no symptoms currently, but he underwent a medical evaluation as a requirement for taking up a new job. His medical history is not significant. His laboratory reports are as follows: Blood hemoglobin 13.7 g/dL Leukocyte count 8,000/mm3 Platelet count 350,000/mm3 Serum creatinine 0.8 mg/dL Serum alanine aminotransferase 16 U/L Serum aspartate aminotransferase 14 U/L Serum cholesterol 450 mg/dL Serum triglyceride 790 mg/dL Serum LDL cholesterol 150 mg/dL Serum HDL cholesterol 55 mg/dL Which of the following findings is most likely to be present on physical examination of this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Achilles tendon xanthoma B. Palmar xanthomas in flexor creases C. Metacarpophalangeal extensor tendon xanthoma D. Xanthelasma
label: Palmar xanthomas in flexor creases
rationale: Based on the elevated levels of both cholesterol and triglycerides, the most likely underlying condition is Type III Hyperlipoproteinemia, which is associated with palmar xanthomas in the flexor creases. Therefore, it is reasonable to conclude that palmar xanthomas are likely to be present on physical examination.

question: A 40-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a small lump on the right side of her neck that she noticed while putting lotion on 1 week ago. She does not have any weight change, palpitations, or altered bowel habits. There is no family history of serious illness. Menses occur at regular 30-day intervals and lasts for 4 days. She appears well. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6° F), pulse is 88/min, and blood pressure is 116/74 mm Hg. Examination shows a small swelling on the right side of the neck that moves with swallowing. There is no lymphadenopathy. Ultrasound of the neck shows a 0.9-cm (0.35-in) right lobe thyroid mass with microcalcifications and irregular margins. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?. Answer Choices: A. Open biopsy B. Thyroid scintigraphy C. Thyroid-stimulating hormone level D. CT of the neck
label: Thyroid-stimulating hormone level
rationale: The most appropriate next step in diagnosis is to measure the thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) level to assess thyroid function and guide further management.

question: A 3-year-old boy is brought to his pediatrician for a regular checkup by his mother. The patient’s mother is concerned about a slight deviation of his left eye and she also notes that her child’s left eye looks strange on the photos, especially if there is a flash. The patient is the first child in the family born to a 31-year-old woman. The boy was born at 39 weeks gestation via spontaneous vaginal delivery. He is up to date on all vaccines and is meeting all developmental milestones. Family history is unremarkable. The eye examination shows left eye converging strabismus. The pupillary reflex cannot be elicited from an illumination of the left eye. Fundal examination reveals are shown in the picture. On testing, visual evoked potential cannot be elicited from the left retina but is normal from the right retina. MRI of the orbits shows a retina-derived tumor in the left eye with an initial spread along the intrabulbar part of the optic nerve and vitreous seeding. The other eye is completely intact. Which of the following methods of treatment is indicated for this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Brachytherapy B. Eye enucleation C. Cryotherapy D. Laser coagulation
label: Eye enucleation
rationale: [LABEL] is the indicated treatment for retinoblastoma in the left eye, given the tumor's spread to the optic nerve and vitreous body. This approach is necessary to prevent further progression of the disease and ensure the best possible outcome for the child.

question: A 55-year-old woman presents to a primary care clinic for a physical evaluation. She works as a software engineer, travels frequently, is married with 2 kids, and drinks alcohol occasionally. She does not exercise regularly. She currently does not take any medications except for occasional ibuprofen or acetaminophen. She is currently undergoing menopause. Her initial vital signs reveal that her blood pressure is 140/95 mmHg and heart rate is 75/min. She weighs 65 kg (143 lb) and is 160 cm (63 in) tall. Her physical exam is unremarkable. A repeat measurement of her blood pressure is the same as before. Among various laboratory tests for hypertension evaluation, the physician requests fasting glucose and hemoglobin A1c levels. Which of the following is the greatest risk factor for type 2 diabetes mellitus?. Answer Choices: A. Age B. Body mass index C. Menopause D. Occupation
label: Body mass index
rationale: Increased [LABEL] (BMI) is the greatest risk factor for type 2 diabetes mellitus in this patient. Her overweight status, combined with menopause, hypertension, and lack of regular exercise, places her at significant risk for T2DM.

question: Most serious complication seen in other eye after traumatic injury to one eye: March 2005, September 2008. Answer Choices: A. Subconjunctival hemorrhage B. Corneal edema C. Sympathetic ophthalmia D. Sudden loss of vision
label: Sympathetic ophthalmia
rationale: [LABEL] is a serious autoimmune condition that can lead to blindness if untreated. The mainstay of treatment is immunosuppressive therapy, including drugs like azathioprine, often combined with corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone or triamcinolone), to control the inflammatory response and improve prognosis. Early intervention is critical to prevent complications and preserve vision.

question: Glucose detection can be done by the all except. Answer Choices: A. Glucose oxidase B. Ferric Chloride test C. Dextrostix D. Follin and Wu method
label: Ferric Chloride test
rationale: The [LABEL] is not used for glucose detection. It is designed to detect phenolic or aromatic compounds, such as those found in metabolic disorders like phenylketonuria and alkaptonuria, and does not react with glucose.

question: A 57-year-old man comes to the emergency department with fatigue and palpitations for several weeks. An ECG shows atrial fibrillation. Echocardiography shows thrombus formation in the left atrium. Which of the following organs is most likely to continue to function in the case of an embolic event?. Answer Choices: A. Spleen B. Kidney C. Liver D. Colon
label: Liver
rationale: The [LABEL] is the organ most likely to continue functioning in the case of an embolic event due to its dual blood supply.

question: A 69-year-old man with metastatic colon cancer is brought to the emergency department because of shortness of breath, fever, chills, and a productive cough with streaks of blood for the past 5 days. He has a history of emphysema. The patient does not have abdominal pain or headache. He receives chemotherapy with 5-fluorouracil, leucovorin, and oxaliplatin every 6 weeks; his last cycle was 3 weeks ago. His temperature is 38.3°C (101°F), pulse is 112/min, and blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 83%. A few scattered inspiratory crackles are heard over the right lung. His mucous membranes are dry. Cardiac examination is normal. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 9.3 mg/dL Leukocyte count 700/mm3 Segmented neutrophils 68% Lymphocytes 25% Eosinophils 4% Monocytes 3% Platelet count 104,000/mm3 Serum Glucose 75 mg/dL Urea nitrogen 41 mg/dL Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL Galactomannan antigen Positive Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?". Answer Choices: A. Ceftriaxone and azithromycin B. Piperacillin-tazobactam C. Ceftazidime and levofloxacin D. Voriconazole
label: Voriconazole
rationale: The most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy for this patient is [LABEL], as the clinical presentation, diagnostic findings, and risk factors strongly suggest invasive aspergillosis.

question: A 43-year-old man with a history of untreated HIV presents with fever, shortness of breath, and a nonproductive cough for the past week. Past medical history is significant for HIV diagnosed 10 years ago and never treated. His most recent CD4+ T cell count was 105/µL. Physical examination reveals bilateral crepitus over all lobes. No lymphadenopathy is present. A chest radiograph reveals bilateral infiltrates. Which of the following is the best treatment for this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART) B. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole C. Ganciclovir D. Azithromycin
label: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole
rationale: The best treatment for this patient with AIDS and suspected Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia is [LABEL] (TMP-SMX).

question: Which of the following drug is used in scabies as single oral dose agent: March 2011. Answer Choices: A. Permethrin B. Retinoids C. Ivermectin D. Co-trimoxazole
label: Ivermectin
rationale: [LABEL] is indeed used as a single oral dose agent for scabies, including in cases of Norwegian scabies. This conclusion aligns with the reasoning that systemic treatment is necessary for severe forms of scabies, and [LABEL] is a well-established option for this purpose.

question: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome (NRDS) is a serious complication seen in infants born prematurely who have a compromised ability to facilitate oxygen diffusion across their alveolar membranes. This is caused by inadequate surfactant production. What role does surfactant play in facilitating oxygen diffusion?. Answer Choices: A. Increases surface permeability B. Maintains alveoli in an open state C. Depresses cilia of the lung D. Dilates blood vessels
label: Maintains alveoli in an open state
rationale: Surfactant plays a critical role in maintaining alveoli in an open state, thereby facilitating oxygen transport. This is achieved by reducing surface tension in the alveoli, preventing their collapse, and ensuring efficient oxygen diffusion across the alveolar wall into the bloodstream.

question: A 38-year-old woman presents with dysphagia. She says the dysphagia is worse for solids than liquids and is progressive. She also complains of associated weakness, fatigue, and dyspnea. The patient denies any recent history of weight loss. Laboratory findings are significant for a hemoglobin of 8.7 g/dL. A peripheral blood smear shows evidence of microcytic hypochromic anemia. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her dysphagia?. Answer Choices: A. Failure of the relaxation of lower esophageal sphincter B. Upper esophageal web C. Lower esophageal spasm D. Esophageal carcinoma
label: Upper esophageal web
rationale: The most likely cause of her dysphagia is an [LABEL], likely due to Plummer-Vinson syndrome, given the combination of progressive dysphagia, microcytic hypochromic anemia, and the mechanical nature of her swallowing difficulty.

question: Most common rheumatic disease in children is ________. Answer Choices: A. Juvenile idiopathic ahritis B. Juvenile ankylosing spondylitis C. Systemic lupus erythematosus D. Morphea
label: Juvenile idiopathic ahritis
rationale: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis is the most common rheumatic disease in children, characterized by joint swelling, pain, and limitation of joint mobility due to inflammation in the synovial joints.

question: Glutathione requires which vitamin to act as antioxidant -. Answer Choices: A. Vitamin E B. Niacin C. Vitamin C D. Vitamin A
label: Niacin
rationale: [LABEL] is the vitamin required for glutathione to act as an antioxidant. This is because [LABEL] is a precursor for NADP+, which is reduced to NADPH, a molecule essential for regenerating reduced glutathione (GSH) from its oxidized form (GSSG).

question: Patellar plexus is formed by which nerve. Answer Choices: A. Lateral cutaneous nerve B. Intermediate cutaneous nerve C. Medial cutaneous nerve D. All of the above
label: All of the above
rationale: The patellar plexus is formed by contributions from the anterior division of the lateral cutaneous nerve, the intermediate cutaneous nerve, the anterior division of the medial cutaneous nerve, and the infrapatellar branch of the saphenous nerve. This conclusion is based on the anatomical distribution and sensory roles of these nerves in the region around the patella.

question: Which of the following is the most common cataract in the newborn -. Answer Choices: A. Zonular Cataract B. Morgagnian Cataract C. Ant. polar Cataract D. Post, polar Cataract
label: Zonular Cataract
rationale: [LABEL] is the most common congenital cataract associated with a visual field defect.

question: A 63-year-old man comes to the physician for evaluation of fever and a nonproductive cough for the past 2 weeks. During this period, he has also had fatigue, myalgia, and difficulty breathing. Five weeks ago, he underwent an aortic prosthetic valve replacement due to high-grade aortic stenosis. The patient has a history of hypertension, asthma, and type 2 diabetes mellitus. A colonoscopy 2 years ago was normal. The patient has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for the past 40 years. He has never used illicit drugs. Current medications include aspirin, warfarin, lisinopril, metformin, inhaled albuterol, and a multivitamin. The patient appears lethargic. Temperature is 38.6°C (101.5°F), pulse is 105/min, and blood pressure is 140/60 mm Hg. Rales are heard on auscultation of the lungs. question: A 61-year-old man presents to the emergency department because he has developed blisters at multiple locations on his body. question: Mycetoma is caused by the following agents except?Allescheria boydii. Answer Choices: A. Allescheria boydii B. Madurella mycetomii C. Trichosporum beigelli D. Nocardia asteroides

question: Pearl's index indicates:. Answer Choices: A. Malnutrition B. Population C. Contraceptive failure D. LBW

rationale: Pearl's index measures [LABEL] by calculating the failure rate per hundred women-years of pregnancy exposure. question: A 23-year-old woman presents to the emergency department complaining of nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. question: A patient with autoimmune thyroditis present with hypothyroidism. Which of the following is true. Answer Choices: A. Thyroid peroxidase antibodies B. Painless enlargement of thyroid C. Common in men D. No malignant risk
label: Thyroid peroxidase antibodies
rationale: Thyroid peroxidase (TPO) antibodies are strongly associated with autoimmune thyroid disease, particularly Hashimoto thyroiditis. Hashimoto thyroiditis is also linked to an increased risk of thyroid lymphoma, which can occasionally present as mediastinal malignant lymphoma. Additionally, there is a less frequent association between Hashimoto thyroiditis and papillary carcinoma. Therefore, the provided answer aligns with the reasoning process.

question: Two weeks after undergoing allogeneic stem cell transplant for multiple myeloma, a 55-year-old man develops a severely pruritic rash, abdominal cramps, and profuse diarrhea. question: Anesthetic agent/s which have tocolytic effect are?. Answer Choices: A. Halothane B. Enflurane C. Isoflurane D. All the above

rationale: Halothane, enflurane, and isoflurane are anesthetic agents with tocolytic effects. They reduce uterine tone and contractions in a dose-dependent manner, likely through mechanisms involving ion channels (e.g., KCNMA1, CACNA1G) and GABA receptors (e.g., GABRP, GABRB2, GABRB3).

question: Meniscal tears usually result from which of the following circumstances?. Answer Choices: A. Hyperextension B. Flexion and rotation C. Simple hyperflexion D. Compression
label: Flexion and rotation
rationale: The injury involves rapid rotation of the flexed condyle of the femur about the tibial plateaux, frequently tearing the medial meniscus. A tear in the inner free border of the cartilage tissue is common. Early surgical removal of the displaced menisci is recommended to prevent further damage to the cartilage or ligaments.

question: The number of line angles in a permanent maxillary central incisor is. Answer Choices: A. Two B. Four C. Six D. Eight
label: Six
rationale: The number of line angles in a permanent maxillary central incisor tooth is [LABEL].

question: An 8-year-old girl is brought into your clinic with a 5 day history of decreased oral intake, body aches and lymphadenopathy. She has no significant medical history. question: A 36-year-old male is brought to the emergency department for severe chest pain and vomiting. question: A 30-year-old patient complains of pain abdomen for 5 days, with similar history in recent past as well. question: A 16-year-old male presents to his pediatrician concerned that he is not maturing like his friends. He has a history of cleft palate status-post multiple surgeries and asthma treated with budesonide and albuterol. He is a good student and is very active on his school’s gymnastics team. His mother is also concerned that her son does not understand good personal hygiene. She reports that he always forgets to put on deodorant. When asked about this, he says he does not notice any body odor on himself or others. His temperature is 99.2°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 105/70 mmHg, pulse is 70/min, and respirations are 18/min. His height and weight are in the 20th and 25th percentiles, respectively. On physical examination, his penis and testicles show no evidence of enlargement. He has no pubic or axillary hair. Which of the following sets of hormone levels is most likely to be found in this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Decreased testosterone, decreased FSH, decreased LH, decreased GnRH B. Increased testosterone, decreased FSH, decreased LH, decreased GnRH C. Decreased testosterone, decreased FSH, decreased LH, increased GnRH D. Normal testosterone, normal FSH, normal LH, normal GnRH
label: Decreased testosterone, decreased FSH, decreased LH, decreased GnRH
rationale: The patient most likely has Kallmann syndrome, characterized by decreased GnRH, FSH, LH, and testosterone. The history of cleft palate and partial anosmia supports this diagnosis. The underlying issue is a defect in GnRH neuron migration, leading to hypogonadotropic hypogonadism.

question: Measles vaccine is not given before:. Answer Choices: A. 9 months B. 12 months C. 15 months D. 18 months

rationale: The measles vaccine is not given before [LABEL] of age because maternal antibodies can interfere with the vaccine's effectiveness, and the infant's immune system may not yet be mature enough to mount an adequate response. Additionally, clinical guidelines and safety studies support this timing to ensure optimal efficacy and safety.

question: Lordaceous spleen is seen in -. Answer Choices: A. Alcoholic hepatitis B. Chronic active hepatits C. Focal amyloidosis D. Diffuse amyloidosis

question: Hea rate increase with one of the following?. Answer Choices: A. Stimulation of trigeminal nerve pain receptor B. Increased intracranial tension C. Decreased stimulation of Baroreceptors D. Increased parasympathetic stimulation

question: A 58-year old man comes to the emergency department because of progressively worsening shortness of breath and fatigue for 3 days. question: A 15-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her mother because of lower abdominal pain for the past 5 days. The pain is constant and she describes it as 7 out of 10 in intensity. question: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the pediatrician by his mother for a routine medical examination. His medical history is relevant for delayed gross motor milestones. question: A 38-year-old woman is diagnosed with a stage IIIa infiltrating ductal carcinoma involving the left breast. Following completion of the intensive phase, she is started on tamoxifen as an adjuvant therapy. 6 months later, she presents with increasing fatigue, orthopnea, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. rationale: The most likely cause of the patient’s dilated cardiomyopathy, severe left ventricular dysfunction, and congestive heart failure is [LABEL]. question: A 46-year-old obese man comes to the emergency room because of paresthesias in his feet and a hypopigmented skin lesion on his knee that he first noticed 6 weeks ago. Topical betamethasone C. Intravenous amphotericin D. Oral rifampicin and dapsone

rationale: The most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy for this patient is [LABEL], consistent with the standard treatment for paucibacillary leprosy.

question: A 15-year-old male presents to his pediatrician after school for follow-up after an appendectomy one week ago. What is the best next step in management?. Answer Choices: A. Basic metabolic panel B. Renal biopsy C. Urine dipstick in the morning and in the afternoon D. Urine electrolytes and creatinine

rationale: The best next step in management is to perform a urine dipstick test in the morning and in the afternoon to assess renal filtration and determine if the proteinuria is transient or persistent.

question: A 31-year-old woman, gravida 1, para 0, at 10 weeks' gestation comes to the physician because of a rash on her upper arm that appeared 3 days ago. rationale: The most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient is [LABEL], as it is the first-line treatment for early Lyme disease in pregnant patients.

question: A patient is more talkative than usual. He used to speak loudly. While talking, he rapidly shifts from topic to topic. He also keeps himself busy in chatting or messaging friends. question: A 36-year-old man presents to the physician with a cough accompanied by expectoration and recurrent wheezing, for the last 2 years. rationale: Based on the clinical presentation, risk factors, and examination findings, the most likely diagnosis is [LABEL]. The erythema, edema, tenderness, warmth, and the presence of a skin break are consistent with this diagnosis, especially in a patient with diabetes mellitus.

question: Peripheral resistance is best indicated by:. Answer Choices: A. Diastolic blood pressure B. Pulse pressure C. Systolic resistance in aoa as it increases in its length D. Mean aerial pressure, which is responsible for blood flow to an organ

Elevated systolic blood pressure and high pulse pressure are associated with reduced arterial elasticity, while low pulse pressure is linked to higher elasticity.

question: What is the strength of shock given to below victim of cardiac arrest with shockable rhythm:. Answer Choices: A. 200 J asynchronous Shock B. 300 AC shock C. 200 Syn. Shock D. 300 J Syn. Shock

question: A 27-year-old male arrives in the emergency department with a stab wound over the precordial chest wall. The patient is in distress and is cold, sweaty, and pale. question: A 27-year-old woman is admitted to the emergency room with dyspnea which began after swimming and progressed gradually over the last 3 days. question: All of the following are causes of Renal Vein Thrombosis, Except:. Answer Choices: A. Membranous Nephropathy B. Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis C. Lupus Nephritis D. Post streptococcal Glomerulonephritis (PSGN)

label: Albumin
rationale: [LABEL] is responsible for transporting long-chain free fatty acids in the plasma during lipid oxidation, while fatty acids are attached to FABP12 within the cell for intracellular transport and metabolism. This conclusion aligns with the physiological roles of [LABEL] and FABP12 in fatty acid transport and lipid oxidation.

question: A 41-year-old man comes to the physician for generalized fatigue and weakness of his left hand for 4 weeks. During this period he also had multiple episodes of cramping abdominal pain and nausea. He works at a battery manufacturing plant. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 75/min, and blood pressure is 124/74 mm Hg. Examination shows pale conjunctivae and gingival hyperpigmentation. There is weakness when extending the left wrist against resistance. The brachioradialis reflex is 1+ on the left and 2+ on the right. The radial pulse is palpable bilaterally. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Further evaluation of this patient is most likely to show which of the following?. Answer Choices: A. Basophilic stippling of erythrocytes B. Beta‑2 microglobulin in urine C. White bands across the nails D. Increased total iron binding capacity
label: Basophilic stippling of erythrocytes
rationale: Further evaluation of this patient is most likely to show [LABEL], indicating abnormal erythrocyte morphology due to lead poisoning.

question: A 7-year-old girl is brought to the pediatrician by her parents for red papules over her left thigh and swelling in the right axilla for the past few days. Her parents say that she had a cat bite on her left thigh 2 weeks ago. Her temperature is 38.6°C (101.4°F), pulse is 90/min, and respirations are 22/min. On her physical examination, hepatosplenomegaly is present with a healing area of erythema on her left hand. Her laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 12.9 gm/dL Leukocyte count 9,300/mm3 Platelet count 167,000/mm3 ESR 12 mm/hr Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?. Answer Choices: A. Doxycycline + rifampin B. Rifampin + azithromycin C. No treatment is required D. Surgical excision of the lymph node
label: Rifampin + azithromycin
rationale: The most appropriate next step in management is treatment with Azithromycin alone as the first-line therapy for cat scratch disease. However, given the presence of hepatosplenomegaly, which suggests possible systemic involvement, combination therapy with Rifampin and Azithromycin may be justified in this case. Therefore, the provided answer aligns with the reasoning, and the treatment with Rifampin and Azithromycin is appropriate.

question: A 52-year-old man comes to the physician because of progressive abdominal distention and weight gain over the last 2 months. He was diagnosed with alcoholic liver cirrhosis with large ascites 1 year ago. He has congestive heart failure with a depressed ejection fraction related to his alcohol use. For the last 6 months, he has abstained from alcohol and has followed a low-sodium diet. His current medications include propranolol, spironolactone, and furosemide. His temperature is 36.7°C (98°F), pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 109/56 mm Hg. Physical examination shows reddening of the palms, telangiectasias on the face and trunk, and prominent blood vessels around the umbilicus. The abdomen is tense and distended; there is no abdominal tenderness. On percussion of the abdomen, there is dullness that shifts when the patient moves from the supine to the right lateral decubitus position. When the patient stretches out his arms with the wrists extended, a jerky, flapping motion of the hands is seen. Mental status examination shows a decreased attention span. Serum studies show: Sodium 136 mEq/L Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL Albumin 3.6 mg/dL Total bilirubin 1.9 mg/dL INR 1.0 Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in treatment?". Answer Choices: A. Refer for transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt B. Refer for liver transplantation C. Refer for peritoneovenous shunt D. Perform large-volume paracentesis "
label: Perform large-volume paracentesis "
rationale: The most appropriate next step in treatment is to perform large-volume paracentesis for the management of ascites and abdominal distention. This will provide symptomatic relief and address the refractory nature of the ascites.

question: A 30-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-day history of abdominal pain. She has a history of recurrent upper respiratory tract infections, sinusitis, and pancreatitis. She has thick nasal secretions. She says that her sweat is salty and crystallizes on her skin. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows epigastric tenderness. Genetic testing for the 36 most common mutations shows a detectable mutation (G551D) in one allele of the CFTR gene. Which of the following best explains this patient's clinical phenotype?. Answer Choices: A. Only one G551D allele is needed in CFTR B. The patient is a CFTR obligate carrier C. The patient's CFTR mutation is unrelated to her clinical phenotype D. The second CFTR mutation was not detected by the testing obtained
label: The second CFTR mutation was not detected by the testing obtained
rationale: The patient's clinical phenotype is best explained by cystic fibrosis. The absence of a second detectable CFTR mutation on the genetic panel suggests that the second mutation may be rare, not included in the panel, or located in a region not covered by standard testing. Further genetic testing, such as whole-gene sequencing, may be necessary to identify the second mutation.

question: A 34-year-old woman is recovering in the post-operative unit following a laparoscopic procedure for chronic endometriosis. She had initially presented with complaints of painful menstrual cramps that kept her bedridden most of the day. She also mentioned to her gynecologist that she had been diagnosed with endometriosis 4 years ago, and she could not find a medication or alternative therapeutic measure that helped. Her medical history was significant for surgery she had 6 years ago to remove tumors she had above her kidneys, after which she was prescribed hydrocortisone. An hour after the laparoscopic procedure, she calls the nurse because she is having difficulty breathing. The nurse records her vital signs include: blood pressure 85/55 mm Hg, respirations 20/min, and pulse 115/min. The patient suddenly loses consciousness. Intravenous fluids are started immediately. She gains consciousness, but her blood pressure is unchanged. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the hypotension?. Answer Choices: A. Loss of fluids during the procedure B. Bleeding profusely through the surgical site C. Improper supplementation of steroids D. High doses of anesthetic drugs
label: Improper supplementation of steroids
rationale: The most likely cause of the hypotension is adrenal insufficiency due to inadequate hydrocortisone supplementation, leading to hypovolemia. This is supported by the patient’s history of hydrocortisone use, the stress of surgery, and the partial response to IV fluids.

question: A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician for evaluation of a 6-month history of irregular menstrual periods. Her last period was 3 months ago. Previously, her periods occurred at regular 28-day intervals and lasted 4–5 days with moderate flow. She has also noticed breast tenderness and scant nipple discharge. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus and refractory bipolar I disorder. Current medications include metformin, glipizide, lithium, and risperidone. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. A urine pregnancy test is negative. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the changes in her menstrual cycle?. Answer Choices: A. Dysregulation of theca and granulosa cell steroidogenesis B. Reduced renal elimination of prolactin C. Impaired production and release of thyroxine D. Blockade of pituitary dopamine receptors
label: Blockade of pituitary dopamine receptors
rationale: The most likely cause of the changes in her menstrual cycle is hyperprolactinemia due to the blockade of pituitary D2 dopamine receptors by risperidone. This explains the irregular menstruation, breast tenderness, and nipple discharge.

question: An enzyme that protects the brain from free radical injury is -. Answer Choices: A. Myeloperoxidase B. MAO C. Superoxide dismutase D. Hydroxylase
label: Superoxide dismutase
rationale: Based on the reasoning process, the enzyme that protects the brain from free radical injury, is involved in enzyme activator activity, and is crucial for reducing increased reactive oxygen species production is indeed [LABEL], particularly SOD1.

question: Which one of the following is true about follicular carcinoma of thyroid?. Answer Choices: A. Is the most common thyroid cancer B. Is readily diagnosed by FNAC C. More commonly spreads through hematogenous route D. Is commonly seen as multifocal
label: More commonly spreads through hematogenous route
rationale: Follicular carcinoma of the thyroid gland spreads hematogenously, often leading to metastases in the skull, lung, and liver. This pattern of spread is consistent with the tumor's vascular invasion and the rich blood supply of these organs.

question: 29-year-old G2P2002 presents with foul-smelling lochia and fever. She is post-partum day three status-post cesarean section due to eclampsia. Which of the following is the next best step in management?. Answer Choices: A. Endometrial culture B. Intravenous clindamycin and gentamicin treatment C. Intramuscular cefotetan treatment D. Prophylactic intravenous cefazolin treatment
label: Intravenous clindamycin and gentamicin treatment
rationale: The next best step in management is treatment with intravenous clindamycin and gentamicin to address the likely diagnosis of postpartum endometritis.

question: A bacterial isolate obtained from a hospitalized patient is found to be resistant to amikacin. The isolated bacteria most likely has which of the following characteristics?. Answer Choices: A. DNA topoisomerase II mutation B. Low-affinity penicillin binding protein C. Increased drug influx capacity D. Enhanced ability to transfer acetyl groups
label: Enhanced ability to transfer acetyl groups
rationale: The isolated bacteria most likely have enhanced acyltransferase activity, transferring groups other than amino-acyl groups. This enzymatic modification is consistent with known mechanisms of aminoglycoside resistance.

question: An obese 34-year-old primigravid woman at 20 weeks' gestation comes to the physician for a follow-up examination for a mass she found in her left breast 2 weeks ago. Until pregnancy, menses had occurred at 30- to 40-day intervals since the age of 11 years. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows a 3.0-cm, non-mobile, firm, and nontender mass in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. There is no palpable axillary lymphadenopathy. Pelvic examination shows a uterus consistent in size with a 20-week gestation. Mammography and core needle biopsy confirm an infiltrating lobular carcinoma. The pathological specimen is positive for estrogen and human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2) receptors and negative for progesterone receptors. Staging shows no distant metastatic disease. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?. Answer Choices: A. Surgical resection and radiotherapy B. Surgical resection and chemotherapy C. Surgical resection D. Radiotherapy and chemotherapy
label: Surgical resection and chemotherapy
rationale: The most appropriate management for this patient with infiltrating lobular carcinoma during pregnancy is surgical resection followed by chemotherapy during the second trimester. HER2-targeted therapy and endocrine therapy should be deferred until after delivery. This approach balances effective cancer treatment with minimizing risks to the fetus.

question: A 23-year-old male presents to his primary care physician after an injury during a rugby game. The patient states that he was tackled and ever since then has had pain in his knee. The patient has tried NSAIDs and ice to no avail. The patient has no past medical history and is currently taking a multivitamin, fish oil, and a whey protein supplement. On physical exam you note a knee that is heavily bruised. It is painful for the patient to bear weight on the knee, and passive motion of the knee elicits some pain. There is laxity at the knee to varus stress. The patient is wondering when he can return to athletics. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?. Answer Choices: A. Medial collateral ligament tear B. Lateral collateral ligament tear C. Anterior cruciate ligament tear D. Posterior cruciate ligament tear
label: Lateral collateral ligament tear
rationale: The most likely diagnosis is a lateral collateral ligament (LCL) tear, given the joint laxity to varus stress, pain with passive motion, difficulty bearing weight, bruising, and the mechanism of injury during rugby.

question: A 70 - year old male presents with a C/O fatigue & jaundice. There is no H/O alcohol or liver disease. The liver and spleen are nonpalpable. He has a normocytic, normochromic anemia. The first step in evaluation of this patient is. Answer Choices: A. CT scan of the abdomen B. Hepatitis profile C. LFT D. Abdominal ultrasound
label: LFT
rationale: The first step in evaluating this patient is to perform liver function tests ([LABEL]s) to determine whether the jaundice is due to conjugated or unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. This will guide further investigation into potential causes such as hemolytic anemia, Gilbert syndrome, or liver dysfunction.

question: The commonest valvular lesion in SLE-related cardiac disease is-. Answer Choices: A. Tricuspid regurgitation B. Mitral regurgitation C. Mitral stenosis D. Aoic regurgitation
label: Mitral regurgitation
rationale: [LABEL] is indeed the most common valvular lesion in systemic lupus erythematosus-related cardiac disease, while valvular stenosis is rarely seen. question: Which of the following is the Post - Chemotherapy based staging system used in Wilm's tumor?. Answer Choices: A. National Wilm's Tumor Staging System (NWTSG) B. International Society of Pediatric Oncology (SIOP) C. AJCC TNM Staging D. Chadwick Staging
label: International Society of Pediatric Oncology (SIOP)
rationale: The [LABEL] staging system is used for Wilms tumor in the context of post-chemotherapy staging. This system involves initial clinical staging, followed by neoadjuvant chemotherapy to reduce tumor burden, which aids in surgical resection and can decrease the necessity for extensive postoperative chemotherapy and radiotherapy.

question: A 39-year-old African American woman is admitted to the hospital following a seizure with a severe post-ictal headache. She was diagnosed with breast cancer 1 year ago when she presented with a hard, rock-like, immobile mass with irregular borders accompanied by changes in the breast skin, including erythema and dimpling. Which of the following molecular profile most likely characterizes this patient?. Answer Choices: A. Progesterone receptor (PR) positive B. ER, PR, HER2 negative C. PR, ER, HER2 positive D. HER2 positive
label: ER, PR, HER2 negative
rationale: Based on the patient’s clinical presentation, family history, and the aggressive nature of her disease, the molecular profile most likely characterizing this patient is ER negative, PR negative, HER2 negative (triple-negative breast cancer).

question: A professional musician visits his physician after a morning concert. He complains of painless swelling in his right cheek when he plays his tuba. CN VII – Buccal branch D. CN VII – Marginal mandibular branch
label: CN VII – Buccal branch
rationale: The nerve associated with motor function to prevent air from entering the affected duct in this patient is cranial nerve VII (CN VII) – the buccal branch.

question: A 19-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because, she says, "I'm burning up." Medical history is significant for intravenous drug use. Physical examination discloses a systolic heart murmur over the precordium. An expected physical finding will be which of the following?. Answer Choices: A. Decreased intensity of S1 B. question: A 16-year-old girl is brought to the physician because of generalized fatigue and an inability to concentrate in school for the past 4 months. During this period, she has had excessive daytime sleepiness. While going to sleep, she sees cartoon characters playing in her room. She wakes up once or twice every night. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?. Answer Choices: A. Modafinil B. Citalopram C. Risperidone D. Venlafaxine

question: Which one of the following lysosomal storage disorder is associated with mental retardation?. Answer Choices: A. Metachromatic leucodystrophy B. Fabry's disease C. Tay Sach's disease D. All of the above
label: All of the above
rationale: Intellectual disability is not a rare presentation in lysosomal storage disorders such as metachromatic leukodystrophy and Tay-Sachs disease, as these conditions involve significant neurological damage that can lead to cognitive impairments. However, in Fabry disease, intellectual disability is less commonly observed and may occur only in specific cases with severe neurological involvement. Therefore, the statement that intellectual disability is a "rare presentation" in these disorders is partially correct but oversimplifies the variability among the disorders.

question: Orange-skin cornea results due to:. Answer Choices: A. Chalcosis B. Siderosis C. Ammonia burn D. Mustard gas

rationale: Eggs are considered an ideal protein due to their high biological efficiency and Net Protein Utilization (NPU). These factors indicate that eggs provide a complete and highly usable source of protein, making them superior to many other protein sources in terms of nutritional quality.

question: Hamaoma is. Answer Choices: A. Proliferation of cells in foreign site B. Proliferation of native cells in tissue C. Malignant condition D. Acquired condition

rationale: Hamartomas are proliferations of native cells in tissue, often leading to the formation of hamartomatous polyps. question: A 30-year-old woman presents with a heart murmur. There is a history of recurrent episodes of arthritis, skin rash, and glomerulonephritis. Blood cultures are negative. question: A 22-year-old man presents to clinic with a chief concern about a painless ulcer on his penis that he noticed 4 weeks ago and resolved one week ago. question: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the pediatrician by his mother with nausea, vomiting, and decreased frequency of urination. question: A 32-year-old woman comes to the physician with fever and malaise. For the past 2 days, she has felt fatigued and weak and has had chills. Last night, had a temperature of 40.8°C (104.2°F). question: A 24-year-old gravida 1 is admitted to the hospital after a tonic-clonic seizure at 37 weeks gestation. question: A 59-year-old man comes to the physician for evaluation of progressively worsening back pain that began about 2 months ago. question: A 57-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by the police after he was found running around a local park naked and screaming late at night. During intake, the patient talks non-stop about the government spying on him and his family, but provides little useful information besides his name and date of birth. question: Which of the following vitamin enhances intestinal absorption of calcium?. Answer Choices: A. Vitamin D B. Vitamin K C. Vitamin B1 D. Vitamin B2
label: Vitamin D

question: A 22-year-old female with no past medical history presents to her primary care physician with a 3-day history of knee pain. She denies any recent injury or trauma.
